If your floor is significantly uneven, you may need to shim under the feet. Use solid material like hardwood blocks rather than soft material that will compress over time.

Once level, give the machine a gentle push from each side. It should feel rock-solid with no wobble. A wobbling machine during excited play will eventually walk across the floor and potentially damage itself or your flooring.

## Electrical Requirements

Slot machines have modest electrical needs but specific requirements.

### Power Specifications

Almost every slot machine sold for home use operates on standard North American household power: 120 volts AC, 60 Hz. They draw between 2 and 5 amps during normal operation, with brief higher draws during startup and when the hopper motor runs. A standard 15-amp household circuit has more than enough capacity.

You do not need a dedicated circuit for a slot machine, but be aware of what else is on the same circuit. If your game room circuit is also running a mini-fridge, a television, a sound system, and a neon sign, adding a slot machine might push things close to the 15-amp breaker limit.

### Surge Protection

This is not optional. Plug your slot machine into a quality surge protector. Not a $8 power strip from the discount store, but a real surge protector rated for at least 1,000 joules with an indicator light that shows the protection is active.

Slot machines contain sensitive logic boards that cost $200 to $800 to replace. A single power surge can destroy them instantly. A $40 surge protector is cheap insurance.

Do not plug the machine directly into a wall outlet without surge protection. Do not use an extension cord longer than 6 feet. Do not daisy-chain power strips. One quality surge protector, plugged directly into a wall outlet, with the machine plugged into it.

### Grounding

The machine must be plugged into a properly grounded (three-prong) outlet. Never remove or bypass the ground pin on the power cord. Slot machines have metal cabinets that must be grounded for safety. An ungrounded machine is a shock hazard.

If your game room only has two-prong outlets, have an electrician install a proper grounded outlet. This typically costs $100 to $200 and is money well spent.

## First Power On

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

You have waited for acclimation. The machine is level. It is plugged into a surge protector connected to a grounded outlet. Now the moment of truth.

Locate the power switch. On most IGT machines, it is inside the main door on the right side, or on the back panel near the bottom. On Bally and WMS machines, it is typically on the back panel. Check your documentation if you are unsure.

Before flipping the switch, open the main door if you can (it should be unlocked or you should have received keys). Look inside briefly. Make sure nothing has shifted during shipping. No loose wires, no components that have come unbolted, no packing material left inside.

Flip the power switch. Here is what you should see and hear in order.

First, the power supply fan will spin up with a soft hum. The monitor will begin to warm up (CRT monitors take 10 to 20 seconds to show an image, LCDs are nearly instant). You may hear the hopper motor briefly test itself. The bill acceptor may cycle its transport mechanism.

The machine will go through a boot sequence. On IGT machines, you will see a series of diagnostic screens with memory tests and hardware checks. This takes 30 to 90 seconds depending on the model. On Bally and WMS machines, the boot process is similar but the screens look different.

If everything passes, the machine will display its attract mode or idle screen. Congratulations. Your machine is alive.

### If Something Goes Wrong

If the machine does not power on at all, verify the outlet has power (plug in a lamp to test), check that the surge protector is switched on, and verify the machine's power switch is in the on position.

If the machine powers on but shows error codes, write down the exact error displayed. Most errors on a freshly-delivered machine are door-related (the machine thinks a door is open) or communication errors that clear themselves after a full power cycle (turn off, wait 30 seconds, turn back on).

If you purchased from us, call our tech support line. We can walk you through 95 percent of first-startup issues over the phone in under 10 minutes.

## Setting the Denomination

Your machine may be set to any denomination from pennies to dollars or higher. For home use, denomination is largely cosmetic since most people play on **free play mode**, but it affects what displays on screen.

To change the denomination, you need to access the machine's setup menu. This requires a special key called the service or setup key, which should have been included with your purchase. Insert the key in the service keyswitch (usually inside the main door or on the belly panel) and turn it.

The setup menu varies by manufacturer but generally has a clearly labeled denomination option. Common choices for home use are 25 cents (quarter) or one dollar. Select your preference, save the setting, and exit the menu.

On IGT S2000 and S-Plus machines, you may need to install the correct denomination-specific EPROM chip. If you purchased from us, we set this up before shipping based on your preference.

## Setting Up Free Play Mode

Free play mode is what most home owners want. It lets you play without inserting money or coins. You just press the button and spin. Credits never run out.

Accessing free play varies by platform.

On IGT machines with the AVP or I-Game platform, free play is a setting in the operator menu. Enter the setup menu with the service key, navigate to the accounting or credits section, and enable free play. On older IGT mechanical reel machines like the S-Plus, free play may require a specific chip configuration.

On Bally machines, free play is typically under the game options in the operator menu. Look for "credit mode" and change it from "standard" to "free play" or "persistent credits."

On WMS Bluebird machines, free play is in the operator settings under the credit configuration section.

If you purchased a refurbished machine from us, free play is already configured before shipping. We set every machine to free play mode unless you specifically request coin or bill operation.

## Connecting a Player Tracking Display (Optional)

Some machines have a player tracking unit in the top box or belly panel. In a casino, this connects to the casino's network. In your home, it will either show an error or a default screen. You can usually disable it in the operator menu to eliminate any error messages, or simply leave it alone if it is not bothersome.

## Sound and Volume

Slot machines are loud. Casino-floor loud. The first thing most home owners do is turn down the volume.

Most machines have a volume control inside the main door, either a physical knob on the speaker amplifier board or a setting in the operator menu. Some newer machines have a player-accessible volume button on the front panel.

Find the volume control and set it to a level appropriate for home use before your first real play session. Your family and neighbors will thank you.

## Final Checklist

Before you declare your installation complete, verify the following.

- Machine is level and stable with no wobble
- Plugged into a surge protector connected to a grounded outlet
- All doors are closed and locked
- No error codes displayed
- Monitor is bright and clear with proper colors
- Sound is working and set to appropriate volume
- Free play mode is active (if desired)
- Bill acceptor works (if you want bill operation)
- All buttons respond when pressed
- The spin or handle mechanism works smoothly

If everything checks out, you are done. Enjoy your machine. And remember, we offer [free lifetime tech support](/warranty) on every machine we sell. If you hit a snag at any point during setup, just call. We have walked thousands of customers through this exact process and we are happy to help.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Slot Machine Maintenance: A Homeowner's Guide
excerpt: Keep your home slot machine running like new with this complete maintenance schedule covering monthly, quarterly, and annual tasks.
body:
A slot machine is a mechanical and electronic device with moving parts, electrical connections, and components that wear over time. The good news is that home machines see a tiny fraction of the use they received on a casino floor. A casino machine might get 8 to 12 hours of continuous play daily. Your home machine might see 2 to 5 hours per week. That dramatically extends the life of every component.

But low use does not mean no maintenance. Dust accumulates. Lubricants dry out. Batteries drain. Connections oxidize. A few minutes of preventive maintenance each month keeps your machine reliable and extends its life by many years.

After 33 years of servicing and refurbishing slot machines, we have distilled everything into a practical maintenance schedule any homeowner can follow. No special training required. Just basic tools, some common supplies, and a willingness to open up the machine occasionally.

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

## Tools and Supplies You Will Need

Before we get into the schedule, gather these items. You probably have most of them already.

- Phillips head screwdriver (medium size)
- Compressed air cans (the kind for cleaning electronics)
- Isopropyl alcohol (90% or higher, not the 70% stuff)
- Lint-free cloths or microfiber towels
- Cotton swabs
- A small vacuum with a brush attachment
- Glass cleaner (ammonia-free for monitors)
- Light machine oil or white lithium grease (small tube)
- Your machine keys (main door key, top box key, service key)

Do not use household cleaning sprays, furniture polish, or any petroleum-based solvents on your machine. These can damage plastics, acrylics, and electronic components.

## Monthly Maintenance (15 Minutes)

These tasks should be done once per month regardless of how often you use the machine. Set a recurring reminder and knock these out. Fifteen minutes once a month prevents expensive repairs.

### Exterior Wipe-Down

Using a slightly damp microfiber cloth, wipe down all exterior surfaces. The button panel, the glass front, the top box glass, the coin tray, and the cabinet sides. This removes dust, fingerprints, and any sticky residue from spilled drinks (it happens).

For the glass panels, use ammonia-free glass cleaner sprayed onto the cloth, not directly onto the glass. Liquid sprayed directly can seep behind the glass and damage backlighting or printed graphics on the reverse side.

### Button Panel Check

Press each button on the machine and verify it responds correctly. Check the spin button, the bet buttons, the cash-out button, and any other player-facing buttons. They should feel responsive with a positive click. If any button feels mushy or requires extra pressure, it may need cleaning or the microswitch behind it may be wearing out.

### Listen for Unusual Sounds

Power the machine on and just listen during normal operation. You should hear the normal sounds: fan humming quietly, reels spinning (on mechanical machines), or the speaker playing normal game sounds. Any new sounds like grinding, clicking, buzzing, or rattling indicate something needs attention.

### Check the Power Cord and Surge Protector

Glance at the power cord where it connects to the machine and where it connects to the surge protector. Look for any fraying, kinks, or discoloration (which indicates heat). Verify the surge protector's indicator light still shows active protection. Surge protectors wear out over time and stop protecting even while still passing power. Replace them every 3 to 5 years.

## Quarterly Maintenance (30-45 Minutes)

Every three months, do the monthly tasks plus these additional items.

### Interior Dusting

Open the main door and use compressed air to blow out dust from the interior components. Focus on the power supply (dust buildup causes overheating), the main logic board area, the fan and its intake/exhaust vents, and any circuit boards visible inside.

Hold the compressed air can upright to avoid spraying liquid propellant onto components. Use short bursts rather than continuous spray. Work from top to bottom so dust falls downward and out.

If dust accumulation is heavy (which indicates your game room needs better filtration or more frequent cleaning), use the small vacuum with brush attachment first to remove the bulk, then follow with compressed air for the tight spots.

### Bill Acceptor Cleaning

The bill acceptor is the single most common source of problems in home slot machines. It has optical sensors, rubber transport rollers, and a mechanical stacking mechanism. All of these collect dust, paper lint, and residue that eventually cause jams and read failures.

Here is how to clean it properly.

Open the bill acceptor head (it usually hinges forward after pressing a release tab). You will see the transport rollers and the optical sensors.

Clean the rollers with a cotton swab dampened with isopropyl alcohol. Roll each roller with your finger while wiping to clean the entire surface. These rollers are what grip and feed bills into the machine. When they get glazed with residue, bills slip and jam.

Clean the optical sensors with a dry cotton swab or a swab lightly dampened with alcohol. These are the small glass or plastic lenses visible in the bill path. There are usually 2 to 4 pairs of sensors. Any dust or residue on these causes the acceptor to reject valid bills or misread denominations.

Blow out the entire bill path with compressed air to remove paper dust and debris.

Reassemble the bill acceptor head and test with a few bills of different denominations to verify proper operation.

If your machine is set to free play and you never use the bill acceptor, you can skip this cleaning. But if you ever want to switch to bill play, you will want a clean acceptor ready to go.

### Hopper Inspection (Coin Machines Only)

If your machine uses coins, the hopper is the bowl-shaped mechanism inside the bottom of the cabinet that stores and dispenses coins. Once a quarter, check the following.

Open the main door and locate the hopper. Look inside for any foreign objects (paper clips, broken coin blanks, or debris). Remove anything that should not be there.

Check that the hopper sits securely in its mounting position. Some hoppers sit in a cradle and can shift during enthusiastic play sessions where the machine gets bumped.

Manually rotate the hopper dispensing disk (the flat disk at the bottom with coin-sized notches). It should rotate freely without binding. If it feels gritty or stiff, remove the hopper and clean the disk area with compressed air and a dry cloth.

Verify the hopper electrical connector is fully seated. A loose connector causes coin-out failures and tilt errors.

### Fan Cleaning

The power supply fan and any case fans accumulate dust on the blades over time, reducing airflow and eventually causing the fan motor to strain. Remove dust from fan blades with compressed air or a cotton swab. If a fan is noticeably louder than when the machine was new, or if it wobbles visibly, it should be replaced. Replacement fans are inexpensive, usually $10 to $20, and are a standard size available at electronics suppliers.

## Semi-Annual Maintenance (60-90 Minutes)

Twice a year, do all the above plus these deeper tasks.

### Connection Check

Open the main door and visually inspect all cable connections you can see. Gently push each connector to verify it is fully seated. Vibration from the hopper motor, from speaker bass, and from excited players hitting buttons can slowly work connectors loose over months.

Pay particular attention to the edge connectors on the main logic board. These flat ribbon-cable style connections are prone to working loose. Push them firmly into their sockets.

### Reel Maintenance (Mechanical Reel Machines)

If you have an IGT S2000, S-Plus, or similar mechanical reel machine, the reels benefit from semi-annual attention.

Open the top of the machine and look at the reel assembly. Check that each reel spins freely by gently rotating it with your finger (with the machine powered off). They should spin smoothly without catching or grinding.

Look at the reel strips (the printed strips with the symbols). Check for any peeling at the edges or tears. Damaged reel strips affect gameplay and should be replaced.

Check the reel stop mechanisms. Each reel has a stepper motor that positions it precisely. Listen during play for any grinding or clicking that was not there before.

### Monitor Care

Your machine's monitor is either a CRT (older cathode ray tube) or an LCD (flat panel). Each has different maintenance needs.

For CRT monitors, check for any degradation in brightness or color. CRTs dim over time as the phosphors age. A CRT that has become noticeably dim or has developed a color tint (often reddish or greenish) is approaching end of life. There is no maintenance that reverses this; the tube eventually needs replacement, and we recommend an LCD conversion at that point.

For LCD monitors, check for any dead pixels, bright spots, or uneven backlighting. Clean the screen surface with an ammonia-free LCD cleaner and a microfiber cloth. Never press hard on an LCD panel. Never use paper towels as they can scratch the coating.

Regardless of monitor type, check that the mounting hardware is secure. The monitor sits in a bracket inside the cabinet, and vibration can loosen the bolts over many months.

### Touchscreen Calibration (Video Machines with Touchscreens)

If your video machine has a touchscreen, verify calibration by touching each corner of the screen and confirming the machine registers the touch in the correct location. If touches are registering in slightly wrong positions, you need to recalibrate. This is usually done through the operator menu under a "touchscreen" or "display" section. The calibration process asks you to touch a series of targets on screen, similar to calibrating a tablet.

Also clean the touchscreen surface with appropriate cleaner. Touchscreens work by detecting finger contact, and a dirty surface can cause missed touches or phantom activations.

## Annual Maintenance

Once a year, do everything above plus these critical annual tasks.

### Battery Check and Replacement

This is the single most important annual task. Every slot machine has at least one battery that maintains critical memory when the machine is powered off. When this battery dies, the machine loses its settings, its accounting data, and potentially the game software itself.

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

On IGT machines (S2000, S-Plus, Game King, and most others), the critical battery is a Dallas DS1287 real-time clock module. This is not a standard battery you can buy at the drug store. It is a specialized integrated circuit with an internal lithium cell. It is soldered onto the main logic board or plugged into a socket depending on the board revision.

The Dallas DS1287 has a typical lifespan of 7 to 10 years. If your machine is refurbished and we installed a new one, you have years of life ahead. But if you bought a used machine or it has been more than 5 years since the last replacement, check the date on the module.

When this battery gets low, the machine may show a "low battery" error code on startup. Do not ignore this. Once the battery dies completely, the machine will lose all RAM data. On some platforms this means a complete reconfiguration from scratch. On others it means you need new EPROM chips reprogrammed. Either way, it is expensive and avoidable.

On Bally machines, the backup battery is typically a standard 3.6V lithium cell that is easier to source and replace. On WMS machines, it varies by platform.

If you are uncomfortable replacing batteries yourself, this is a good time to contact us for guidance. We can walk you through it over the phone for your specific machine model, or sell you the correct replacement part.

### Power Supply Load Test

The power supply converts household AC power to the various DC voltages the machine needs (typically +5V, +12V, and +24V). Over years of use, power supply capacitors age and voltage regulation can drift.

If you have a multimeter (even a cheap one works), check the DC output voltages at the test points on the power supply board (if labeled) or at the connector going to the main board. The +5V rail should read between 4.9V and 5.1V. The +12V rail should read between 11.8V and 12.2V. If any voltage is more than 5% off specification, the power supply should be replaced.

If you do not have a multimeter or are not comfortable with this, skip it and just monitor for symptoms of power supply degradation: random resets, display flickering, or intermittent errors that clear on their own.

### Door Switch and Security Check

Verify that all door switches function correctly. When you open the main door, the machine should register a "door open" condition (usually shown on the display or heard as an alert tone). When you close the door, it should clear. Same for the top box door and the coin door if applicable.

These switches are a safety feature. In a casino they trigger security alerts. In your home they simply ensure the machine knows its doors are properly closed, which matters because some machines will not operate with a door registered as open.

## When to Call a Tech

Some problems are beyond basic home maintenance. Call for professional help if you encounter any of the following.

- The machine will not power on at all and you have verified the outlet has power
- The monitor displays a solid color or horizontal/vertical lines
- You smell burning or see smoke (unplug immediately)
- The machine shows persistent error codes that do not clear after a power cycle
- Mechanical reels do not spin or stop in wrong positions consistently
- The main logic board shows visible damage like burnt components or leaking capacitors
- You need to replace the Dallas DS1287 battery and are not comfortable soldering

If you purchased your machine from us, call our tech support line first. We resolve the majority of issues over the phone with our customers, and when parts are needed, we can ship them quickly with instructions.

## Common Maintenance Mistakes to Avoid

After decades of seeing what customers do to their machines, here are the mistakes we see most often.

### Using WD-40 on Everything

WD-40 is not a lubricant. It is a water displacement spray that leaves a residue attracting dust and grime. Do not spray it on reel mechanisms, coin mechanisms, or any internal components. Use proper light machine oil (a single drop where needed) or white lithium grease for mechanisms that need lubrication.

### Over-Cleaning with Harsh Chemicals

Bleach, ammonia-based cleaners, acetone, and harsh degreasers destroy plastics, strip printed graphics, and corrode metal contacts. Stick with isopropyl alcohol for electronics and mild soapy water (well wrung out) for exterior cabinet surfaces.

### Ignoring the Bill Acceptor Until It Fails

The bill acceptor gives you warning signs before it fails completely. It starts rejecting bills it used to accept. It becomes picky about bill condition. It takes multiple attempts to feed a bill. These are signs it needs cleaning. Do not wait until it stops working entirely.

### Running Without a Surge Protector

We see this constantly. Someone sets up the machine perfectly with a surge protector, then rearranges the game room six months later and plugs the machine directly into the wall because the surge protector does not reach. Then a power surge kills the logic board. A $400 to $800 repair bill because of a $40 item.

### Never Opening the Cabinet

Some owners treat the machine like a sealed appliance and never open it. Dust builds up for years. Connections slowly loosen. A battery dies and nobody notices until the machine loses all its settings. Opening the cabinet quarterly for inspection and cleaning prevents all of these scenarios.

### Using Coins from Unknown Sources

If you run a coin-operated machine, use only standard US coins of the correct denomination. Foreign coins, arcade tokens, and worn or damaged coins cause jams, damage the coin comparator mechanism, and can trigger tilt conditions that lock the machine.

### Leaving the Machine Powered On 24/7

Unlike casino machines that run continuously, home machines should be powered off when not in use. This extends the life of the monitor (especially CRTs), reduces wear on the power supply, and lowers your electricity bill. The boot-up sequence only takes 30 to 90 seconds, so there is no practical reason to leave it running all the time.

## Seasonal Considerations

If your machine is in a room that experiences temperature or humidity changes with the seasons, take extra care.

In winter, if the room where your machine lives gets very dry from heating, static electricity becomes a concern. Static discharge can damage sensitive electronics. Consider a small humidifier in the room if you notice frequent static shocks.

In summer, if the room gets warm, ensure the machine's ventilation is not obstructed. Slot machines are designed to operate in casino environments (typically 68 to 72 degrees Fahrenheit). They can handle warmer temps up to about 85 degrees, but prolonged heat shortens component life.

If you lose power for an extended period (days), the machine will be fine. The backup battery maintains critical memory. When power returns, just turn it on normally. If the power was out for weeks and the room experienced extreme temperatures, follow the temperature acclimation protocol (let the machine reach room temp for several hours before powering on).

## Maintenance Log

Keep a simple written log of your maintenance activities and any issues you notice. This sounds old-fashioned but it is genuinely useful. Note the date, what you did, and anything that seemed different or concerning. This history helps if you ever need to troubleshoot a problem or if you eventually call for technical support. We can diagnose issues much faster when the owner can tell us when something first started versus discovering it only today.

A simple notebook kept near the machine works perfectly. Date, task, notes. Takes 30 seconds to jot down after each maintenance session.

## The Bottom Line

Slot machine maintenance is not complicated or time-consuming. Fifteen minutes monthly, 45 minutes quarterly, and one focused session annually is all it takes to keep your machine running reliably for 10, 15, even 20 or more years.

The machines we refurbish and sell were often running on casino floors for a decade or more before coming to us. With proper home maintenance at a fraction of the use intensity, there is no reason your machine will not provide entertainment for the rest of your life.

And remember, we are always here to help. Free lifetime tech support means exactly that. Call us anytime with questions about maintaining your machine. We have been keeping these machines running since 1992 and we have seen it all.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Coin vs. Coinless (TITO) Slot Machines: Which Is Right for Your Home?
excerpt: Compare coin-operated and coinless TITO slot machines for home use, plus learn about free play mode and what most buyers actually choose.
body:
One of the first decisions home slot machine buyers face is whether they want a coin-operated machine, a coinless TITO (Ticket In, Ticket Out) machine, or something else entirely. Each system has genuine advantages and trade-offs, and the right choice depends on what kind of experience you want in your game room.

Having sold machines configured every possible way over the past 33 years, we can tell you what most people choose, why they choose it, and the factors that should influence your decision.

## How Coin-Operated Machines Work

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

A coin-operated slot machine is what most people picture when they think of a slot machine. You insert quarters (or whatever denomination), the coin drops through a coin comparator that verifies it is genuine, your credits increase, you play, and when you cash out the hopper at the bottom spits coins into the metal tray with that satisfying clinking sound.

The internal components that make this work include the coin comparator (which uses size, weight, and electromagnetic properties to validate coins), the coin diverter (which routes accepted coins to the hopper or to a drop box when the hopper is full), and the hopper itself (a bowl with a motor-driven disk that picks up and dispenses individual coins).

This is the original system that slot machines used for over a century. It is proven, it is mechanical, and it delivers a specific sensory experience that many people love.

## How TITO (Coinless) Machines Work

TITO stands for Ticket In, Ticket Out. This system replaced coins in most casinos starting in the early 2000s. Instead of inserting coins, you insert paper currency into a bill acceptor. Instead of coins paying out, the machine prints a voucher ticket with a barcode when you cash out.

In a casino, you take that ticket to another machine or to a cashier. In your home, the ticket is essentially just a receipt showing your final credit balance. It is a souvenir of your session.

The internal components are simpler than a coin machine. There is a bill acceptor for input, a thermal ticket printer for output, and no hopper, coin comparator, or coin-handling mechanisms at all. This means fewer moving parts, fewer potential failure points, and significantly less noise during payout.

## The Nostalgia Factor: Why People Love Coins

Let us be honest about this. The number one reason people want coin-operated machines is the experience. The sound of coins dropping into the tray is iconic. It is the sound of winning. It is the sound of Las Vegas in the 1980s and 1990s. It triggers memories and creates atmosphere in a way that a printed ticket simply cannot match.

There is also a tactile element. Feeding coins into the machine, hearing them validated and accepted, watching your bucket of coins grow or shrink. It makes the money feel real in a way that credits on a screen do not.

For home use, some people set up their machine with a coin bucket and treat it like a mechanical piggy bank. Feed in spare change, play until it runs out, cash out what you win. It gamifies saving money in a fun way, especially for parties.

## The Practical Case Against Coins

Coins come with real drawbacks for home use.

The hopper is the most failure-prone component in a slot machine. The dispensing disk can jam. Coins can bridge (get wedged together) and cause the hopper to stall. Foreign coins or damaged coins cause problems. The hopper motor wears out. In a casino, technicians clear hopper jams multiple times per shift. In your home, that is your problem.

Coins are heavy and messy. A full hopper of quarters weighs 25 to 30 pounds. You need to keep a supply of coins on hand to operate the machine. Those coins end up on the floor, behind furniture, and in couch cushions. If you have small children, coins on the floor are a choking hazard.

Coin machines are loud during payout. The hopper motor, combined with 50 to 200 quarters dropping into a metal tray, creates significant noise. This can be an issue in shared living spaces, during late-night play, or if your game room is not acoustically isolated from the rest of the house.

Maintenance is higher. The coin comparator needs periodic cleaning. The hopper needs inspection for jams and foreign objects. Coin dust (fine metallic particles) accumulates inside the cabinet and coats other components.

## The Practical Case for TITO

TITO machines solve all of the above problems. No coins to buy, store, or clean up. No hopper to jam. Dramatically quieter operation. Less internal dust and wear. Fewer moving parts means less maintenance and fewer failures.

The bill acceptor is the only mechanical input device, and modern bill acceptors are highly reliable with proper quarterly cleaning. The ticket printer is simple thermal printing technology that works reliably for years with no ink to replace (thermal printers use heat, not ink).

For casual home use, TITO is simply more convenient. Insert a bill to get credits, play as long as you like, and when you are done the machine prints a ticket. The ticket does not need to go anywhere; it is just how the machine ends your session.

## Free Play Mode: The Third Option

Here is what most people do not realize until they start shopping. Your slot machine does not have to require any money input at all.

Free play mode means the machine always has credits available. You never insert anything. You just walk up, press the button, and play. Credits never expire and never run out. When you press cash out, the credit meter simply resets.

Free play mode is a software setting, not a hardware modification. Almost any slot machine made after 1995 can be set to free play through the operator configuration menu. It takes about 30 seconds to enable.

In **free play mode**, the coin mechanism and bill acceptor are essentially disabled. You do not need them. The machine operates purely as an entertainment device without any money handling at all.

## What Most Home Buyers Choose

After selling thousands of machines for home use, here is the breakdown of what customers actually configure.

Approximately 65 percent choose coinless with free play. They want the machine for entertainment without any money handling whatsoever. No coins, no bills, no tickets. Just press the button and play.

About 20 percent choose coinless with bill play. They insert bills to get credits and get a ticket at cash-out. This gives a more authentic casino-like experience without the hassle of coins.

About 15 percent want coin operation. These are typically enthusiasts who specifically want the classic coin-drop experience, people who use the machine at parties as a conversation piece with real quarters, or collectors going for complete authenticity.

The trend has been steadily moving toward free play over the past decade. As people get further from the era of coin-operated casinos, the nostalgia factor diminishes and convenience wins.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

## Converting Between Systems

If you buy a coin machine and later want to go coinless, or vice versa, conversion is possible but involves different levels of difficulty.

### Coin to Free Play (Easiest)

This is just a software setting change. Enter the operator menu, enable free play. Done. The coin mechanism still exists inside the machine but is simply not used. Five-minute job.

### Coin to TITO (Moderate)

This requires adding a bill acceptor (if one is not already installed) and a ticket printer, plus updating the machine's software configuration to recognize these components. On some platforms this is straightforward. On older platforms it may require a board upgrade. Typical cost for parts and labor runs $200 to $400.

### TITO to Coin (Most Complex)

Adding coin capability to a coinless machine means installing a coin comparator, coin diverter mechanism, and hopper. The mechanical mounting points may or may not exist in your specific cabinet depending on when it was manufactured. Some newer cabinets were built exclusively for coinless operation and do not have the physical space or mounting hardware for a hopper. In machines where conversion is possible, expect $300 to $500 in parts and labor.

### TITO to Free Play (Easiest)

Same as coin to free play. Software setting only. The bill acceptor and printer remain installed but unused.

## Factors to Consider for Your Decision

Here are the practical questions to ask yourself.

How often will you use the machine? Daily players benefit from the convenience of free play. Occasional or party-only use might favor the fun of coins.

Who else will use it? If guests at parties will play, free play eliminates the need to provide coins or explain the bill acceptor. Anyone can walk up and play immediately.

Do you have young children? Coins on the floor are a choking hazard. Free play or TITO eliminates this risk.

Is noise a concern? Coin payouts are loud. If the machine is in a shared living space or you play late at night, coinless is dramatically quieter.

Are you mechanically inclined? If you enjoy tinkering and do not mind occasional hopper jams, coin operation is manageable. If you want zero maintenance hassle, go coinless with free play.

Do you want to use the machine as entertainment for parties? Coins can be fun at parties. Give each guest a bucket of quarters and let them play. It creates engagement and competition. But free play with a simple "highest credit wins" competition works just as well with zero preparation.

Is authenticity important to you? If you are a collector or casino enthusiast who wants the most historically accurate experience, coin operation on a vintage machine delivers that perfectly.

## Our Recommendation

For the typical home buyer who wants a slot machine for personal entertainment and occasional party use, we recommend a coinless machine configured for free play. It is the lowest maintenance, lowest hassle, and most reliable configuration. You never run out of credits, never deal with jams, never sweep coins off the floor.

If you want a slightly more authentic feel, add bill acceptor play so you can insert a twenty and play until you win or lose it. This adds a mild stakes element that some people enjoy without the mechanical complexity of coins.

If you specifically want coins because that experience is important to you, we absolutely support that choice. Just understand the maintenance trade-off and keep your quarterly bill acceptor and hopper cleaning schedule.

Every machine we sell can be configured any of these ways. Tell us your preference when you order and we will set it up before shipping. And if you change your mind later, we can walk you through reconfiguring over the phone. That is what [free lifetime tech support](/warranty) means.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Best Slot Machines for a Home Game Room (2026 Picks)
excerpt: Our top slot machine picks for home game rooms in 2026, with recommendations by category from a 33-year veteran dealer.
body:
After 33 years selling slot machines for home use, we know exactly which models deliver the best experience, the fewest headaches, and the most smiles. Not every machine that was great on a casino floor translates well to home use. Some are too complicated to maintain. Some have proprietary parts that are impossible to source. Some just are not fun when you are playing alone rather than in a crowd.

These are our honest recommendations for 2026, organized by category so you can find exactly what fits your space, your budget, and your style.

## Best Classic Reel Machine: IGT S2000 Double Diamond

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

Price range: $800 to $1,200 refurbished

If you ask us which single machine we have sold more of than any other for home use, the answer is the IGT S2000 running Double Diamond. There is a reason this game has been a casino staple since the mid-1990s and remains on casino floors today. It is simple, it is satisfying, and it is built like a tank.

The S2000 platform is IGT's workhorse stepper machine. The cabinet is all steel construction. The reel mechanism is robust and precisely engineered. The platform is so well understood that any competent slot technician can service it blindfolded, and parts remain widely available and affordable.

Double Diamond itself is the perfect home game. Three reels, one payline (or three paylines in the deluxe version), and straightforward diamond-themed symbols. The double diamond symbol acts as both a wild and a multiplier, doubling any win it appears in. Two diamonds in a winning combination quadruple the payout. It is simple enough that anyone can understand it in 30 seconds.

Why we recommend it for home use: Mechanical reels deliver the authentic casino sound and feel that video screens cannot replicate. The clunk-clunk-clunk of reels stopping is what most people think of when they imagine a slot machine. The S2000 cabinet is compact enough for any game room at 20 inches wide, 20 inches deep, and 66 inches tall. Maintenance is straightforward. Parts are cheap and available. And the game never gets old because its simplicity is its appeal.

Alternatives in this category: IGT S2000 Triple Diamond, Red White and Blue, or Five Times Pay. All run on the same platform with different game themes.

## Best Video Slot: WMS Bluebird 2

Price range: $1,200 to $1,800 refurbished

When people think of modern casino video slots with bonus rounds, animated features, and immersive sound, they are usually thinking of games that ran on the WMS Bluebird 2 platform. This machine dominated casino floors from roughly 2008 to 2016 and the game library includes some of the most popular video slots ever made.

The Bluebird 2 cabinet features a large 22-inch widescreen LCD main display with a secondary display in the top box. The displays are bright, vivid, and designed for extended viewing without fatigue. The cabinet itself is a distinctive curved design that looks modern and impressive in any game room.

The game library is what makes this platform special. Titles available include Zeus (the most popular video slot of its era), Jungle Wild, Goldfish, Bruce Lee, Bier Haus, and dozens more. Many of these games feature elaborate bonus rounds with free spins, pick-em features, and cascading reels that keep play sessions entertaining for hours.

Why we recommend it for home use: The Bluebird 2 delivers the complete modern casino experience. The bonus rounds are genuinely exciting and varied. The sound design is excellent with actual music and voice acting rather than simple beeps and chimes. The displays are LCD so there is no CRT burn-in to worry about. The platform is reliable and well-documented with available parts.

Multi-game conversion kits exist for the Bluebird 2 that can give you dozens of games on a single machine, though the availability and legality of these kits varies. We sell machines with the original licensed game software.

The one downside: Bluebird 2 machines are larger than classic reel machines. The cabinet with the top box stands about 76 inches tall and weighs 275 to 325 pounds. Make sure your space can accommodate it.

## Best Multi-Game Machine: IGT Game King

Price range: $1,400 to $2,200 refurbished (depending on game count and configuration)

If variety is what you want, nothing beats the IGT Game King. This platform offers multiple games in a single machine, including slots, video poker, blackjack, and keno, all selectable from a touchscreen menu.

The Game King comes in several configurations. The most common for home use is the multi-game video poker version with 31, 55, or even 80+ game variants. You can play Jacks or Better, Deuces Wild, Double Bonus, and dozens more video poker variants, then switch to a selection of video slot games or table games without leaving your seat.

The platform uses a 17-inch or 19-inch LCD touchscreen (or CRT in older versions, though we convert most to LCD during refurbishment). The interface is intuitive: touch the game you want, play it, touch the menu button to switch games.

Why we recommend it for home use: One machine, dozens of games. If you only have room (or budget) for a single machine, the Game King delivers the most entertainment value per dollar. Video poker enthusiasts particularly love this machine because it offers every poker variant they could want. The variety prevents the boredom that can set in with a single-game machine.

The Game King is also an excellent party machine. Different guests prefer different games, and the multi-game format means everyone finds something they enjoy without needing multiple machines.

Available in both upright and slant-top (sit-down bar-top style) configurations. The slant-top version is excellent for game rooms with a bar counter.

## Best Conversation Piece: Bally S6000

Price range: $900 to $1,500 refurbished

The Bally S6000 is our pick for the machine that gets people talking. This platform ran some of the most visually distinctive games of the late 1990s and early 2000s, and the cabinet itself has a presence that commands attention.

The S6000 is a wide-body cabinet that is noticeably larger than standard machines. It uses a unique top-mounted spinning wheel (on models like Wheel of Gold) or an oversized bonus display that makes it immediately eye-catching. When someone walks into your game room and sees an S6000, they immediately want to know about it.

Popular S6000 titles include Blazing 7s (the classic Bally game with its distinctive red, white, and blue sevens), Cash Spin with its physical bonus wheel, and various themed games with elaborate top-box bonus features.

Why we recommend it for home use: This is the machine for someone who wants a showpiece. The S6000 makes a statement. It is large, it is loud (in the best way), and it looks like something that belongs in the center of a casino floor. If your game room is a source of pride and you want a machine that impresses every guest who sees it, this is the one.

The Bally platform is well-engineered and reliable. Parts availability is good. The main consideration is size: the wide-body cabinet is approximately 36 inches wide rather than the standard 24 to 30 inches. Plan your space accordingly.

## Best Value Under $1,000: IGT S-Plus

Price range: $599 to $899 refurbished

The IGT S-Plus is the predecessor to the S2000 and remains one of the best values in the used slot machine market. This platform ran from the late 1980s through the mid-1990s, making it vintage but not unreliable. IGT built these machines to last, and they have proven it by still running perfectly 30-plus years later.

The S-Plus is a three-reel mechanical machine available in hundreds of game themes. Double Diamond, Red White and Blue, Five Times Pay, Wild Cherry, and many more all run on this platform. The gameplay is identical to what you would experience in a casino with genuine IGT software and reel strips.

Cosmetically, the S-Plus shows its age compared to the S2000. The cabinet design is more angular and 1980s in styling. The displays are simpler. But mechanically, these machines are equally robust and deliver the exact same gaming experience.

Why we recommend it for home use: The S-Plus delivers 90 percent of the S2000 experience at 60 to 70 percent of the price. If your budget is under $1,000 and you want a genuine casino slot machine (not a toy or replica), the S-Plus is your best option. We refurbish these to the same standard as our more expensive machines, including new batteries, rebuilt bill acceptor, tested and verified electronics, and full **1-year warranty**.

The S-Plus is also slightly more compact than the S2000 and lighter, making it easier to move and place in tight spaces.

Parts are still available though becoming less common. The most critical replacement part, the Dallas DS1287 battery, is the same across the S-Plus and S2000 platforms.

## Best for Small Spaces: IGT Slant-Top (Various Models)

Price range: $1,000 to $1,800 refurbished (depending on platform and game)

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

Not everyone has room for a full-height upright machine. If your game room is compact, or if you want to place a machine at a bar counter, a slant-top is the answer.

Slant-top machines sit on a desk, counter, or dedicated stand at roughly table height. The player sits in a chair or bar stool rather than standing. The total height with the machine on a standard stand is approximately 48 to 52 inches, dramatically lower than a 72 to 76 inch upright.

The footprint is smaller too. Most slant-tops are 24 to 26 inches wide and 22 to 24 inches deep. They can sit on an existing counter or desk that can support approximately 100 to 175 pounds (slant-tops are significantly lighter than uprights since there is no tall cabinet structure).

We carry slant-top configurations of the IGT Game King (multi-game), IGT video slots, and some WMS platforms. The most popular slant-top for home use is the Game King multi-game poker machine, which pairs perfectly with a home bar setup. Sit at the bar, have a drink, play some video poker. It recreates the Vegas bar experience perfectly.

Why we recommend it for home use: When space is limited, a slant-top lets you have a real slot machine without dedicating the floor space that an upright requires. They are easier to move, lighter, and can integrate into existing furniture arrangements rather than dominating a room. The seated play position is also more comfortable for extended sessions.

The trade-off: slant-tops lack the visual impact of a full upright machine. They do not have the imposing presence of a standing cabinet with a lit top box. If you want a showpiece, go upright. If you want practical entertainment that fits your space, go slant-top.

## Honorable Mentions

A few other machines worth considering that did not fit neatly into the categories above.

### Konami Podium (Video Slots)

Konami makes excellent video slots with unique math models and bonus features. Their Podium cabinet is reliable and visually appealing. Popular titles include China Shores and Lotus Land. Price range: $1,000 to $1,600 refurbished.

### Aristocrat Viridian (Video Slots)

Aristralian gaming giant Aristocrat makes some of the most mathematically interesting slot games in the industry. Their Buffalo series (Buffalo, Buffalo Gold, Buffalo Grand) is massively popular. The Viridian cabinet is well-built and reliable. Price range: $1,200 to $1,900 refurbished.

### Ainsworth A600 (Video Slots)

Ainsworth is less well-known than the major brands but makes quality machines with engaging game themes. Good value for buyers who want something different from the standard IGT/Bally/WMS offerings. Price range: $800 to $1,300 refurbished.

## Factors That Should Influence Your Choice

Beyond the specific recommendations above, consider these factors.

### Maintenance and Parts Availability

IGT machines (S-Plus, S2000, Game King) have the best parts availability and the most technicians who know how to service them. If long-term maintainability matters to you, IGT is the safest choice. WMS and Bally are close behind. Konami, Aristocrat, and Ainsworth parts are available but less abundant.

### Your Play Style

Do you enjoy the simplicity of three reels and a single payline? Classic reel machines are your match. Do you want elaborate bonus rounds and animated features? Video slots deliver that. Do you think strategically and enjoy optimal play decisions? Video poker on a Game King rewards skill. Know yourself and buy accordingly.

### Who Else Will Play

If the machine is primarily for you, buy what you love. If it is for entertaining guests, consider accessibility. Classic three-reel games are immediately understood by anyone. Complex video slots with 50 paylines and multiple bonus triggers can confuse casual players. Multi-game machines let different guests find their preference.

### Budget for the Complete Setup

Remember that the machine itself is not your only cost. Budget for a quality surge protector ($40), possibly an appliance dolly for moving it ($80 to rent, $150 to buy), and a small maintenance supply kit ($30). If you are buying a slant-top, you may need a stand or need to verify your existing furniture can support the weight.

## Our Guarantee On All Picks

Every machine on this list, regardless of price point, comes with our standard offering: full refurbishment to our specifications, 1-year warranty on all mechanical and electronic components, and free lifetime technical support. We do not sell a budget machine with less care than a premium one. The refurbishment process is identical.

We have [334 machines in stock](/shop) at any given time across all these platforms and more. If a specific model or game title interests you, call us. We can tell you immediately what we have available, show you photos, and discuss configuration options.

We ship nationwide with proper crating and freight carrier delivery. Your machine arrives ready to plug in and play.

Thirty-three years in Kingman, Arizona. Same location, same commitment to quality. That is the Used Slot Shop difference.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: IGT vs. Bally vs. Aristocrat: Comparing Slot Machine Brands
excerpt: A 33-year veteran dealer breaks down the major slot machine brands, comparing build quality, parts availability, maintenance, and resale value.
body:
After 33 years selling used slot machines out of Kingman, Arizona, I have handled thousands of machines from every major manufacturer. The question I get asked most often is some variation of: which brand should I buy? The answer depends on what you want out of the machine, your budget, your technical comfort level, and whether you care about resale value down the road.

Let me walk you through the big three, then cover the secondary manufacturers that deserve attention.

## IGT: The Industry Standard

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

### Company History

International Game Technology started in 1975 as a small Reno operation and grew into the largest slot manufacturer in the world. They merged with GTECH in 2015 and now operate as IGT PLC out of London, but their slot manufacturing roots are pure Nevada. If you have ever walked into a casino anywhere in North America, you have played an IGT machine. They dominated the mechanical reel market for decades and transitioned into video slots without missing a beat.

### Cabinet Types

IGT has produced more cabinet styles than any other manufacturer. The ones you will encounter most often on the secondary market include the S2000 (the upright mechanical reel workhorse), the Game King (the multi-game video platform that revolutionized bars and taverns), the AVP (Advanced Video Platform, their premium video cabinet from the mid-2000s), the G20 and G23 (sleek curved-screen uprights), and the Crystal Dual and Crystal Curve (their modern premium cabinets with massive displays).

The S2000 is the machine most people picture when they think of a slot machine. It is a 19-inch upright with three mechanical reels, a coin hopper, and a pull handle. We have sold more S2000s than any other single cabinet in our 33 years. They are the Toyota Camry of slot machines: reliable, parts are everywhere, and any tech can work on them.

The Game King platform deserves special mention. This multi-game video machine lets you offer poker, slots, keno, and blackjack all in one cabinet. The 17-inch upright version was the backbone of every bar and tavern route in Nevada for 15 years. We keep dozens in stock at all times because demand never stops.

### Build Quality

IGT machines are built like tanks. The S2000 cabinet is heavy-gauge steel with a powder coat finish that holds up for decades. Internal components are modular and clearly labeled. Wire harnesses are color-coded and use standard connectors. The engineering philosophy is clearly meant for field service: everything is designed to be accessed, diagnosed, and replaced by a technician who might be working on 200 different machines across 30 locations.

The newer IGT cabinets (G20, G23, Crystal series) use more plastic and proprietary components, but they are still well-engineered. The trend toward larger displays and curved screens means more delicate components, but IGT builds them to withstand casino environments where machines run 24/7.

### Parts Availability

This is where IGT absolutely dominates. Because they have the largest installed base in the world, parts are available everywhere. Bill acceptors, power supplies, hopper motors, reel mechanisms, logic boards, button panels, harnesses, and every other component you might need are stocked by multiple suppliers. You can find S2000 parts in five minutes of searching. Even for newer platforms, IGT parts availability outpaces every other manufacturer.

### Maintenance Complexity

IGT machines are the easiest to maintain. Period. The S2000 can be diagnosed by a motivated homeowner with a multimeter and the service manual. Error codes are logical. Dip switches are labeled. The Game King requires a bit more software knowledge, but nothing exotic. We provide [free lifetime tech support](/warranty) with every purchase, and most IGT issues can be resolved over the phone in under 15 minutes.

### Resale Value

IGT holds its value well on the low end and depreciates predictably on the high end. An S2000 that sold for $500 ten years ago still sells for $400-600 today depending on the game. They do not spike in value, but they do not crater either. Game Kings hold steady. The newer video platforms depreciate faster because technology moves on, but there is always a buyer for a clean IGT machine.

### Best For

First-time buyers. Home game rooms. Anyone who wants zero hassle with parts and service. People who love classic reel games. Bar or restaurant owners who want proven reliability.

## Bally: The Premium Choice

### Company History

Bally Manufacturing started in 1932 making pinball machines and entered the slot market in the 1960s. They were acquired by Alliance Gaming, then Scientific Games (now Light and Wonder). The Bally brand has been through corporate ownership changes, but their engineering DNA remained consistent through their peak production years. Bally machines from the 1990s through 2010s represent some of the finest slot engineering ever produced.

### Cabinet Types

The Bally cabinets you will find on the secondary market include the Pro Series (S6000, 6000, and 5500 mechanical reel uprights), the Alpha platform (video uprights with the distinctive bow-tie top box), the V32 (a wide-body video cabinet with a 32-inch main display), the Pro V22 and V22HD (the standard video workhorse), and the U-Spin series (featuring the iconic physical spinning wheel on top).

The Pro Series mechanical reel machines are beautiful pieces of engineering. The S6000 in particular has a fit and finish that makes IGT's S2000 look industrial by comparison. The cabinet doors seal tighter, the chrome accents are actual chrome, and the glass is held with precision hardware rather than simple clips.

The Alpha platform is where Bally really made their mark in video slots. Hot Shot, Quick Hit, and dozens of other legendary titles ran on Alpha hardware. These machines have a distinctive look with their wide top box and crisp displays.

### Build Quality

Bally built premium machines. The materials are higher grade, the tolerances are tighter, and the finish work is noticeably better than the competition. A Bally Pro Series machine sitting next to an IGT S2000 looks like a luxury car next to a work truck. The internal wiring is immaculate, with harnesses bundled and routed with obvious care.

The downside of premium build quality is premium complexity. Bally used more proprietary connectors, custom-designed components, and brand-specific solutions than IGT. This is not a problem when the machine is working perfectly. It becomes a factor when something breaks.

### Parts Availability

Here is where Bally loses ground to IGT. Parts are available, but the supply is thinner. Bally-specific bill acceptors, power supplies, and logic boards have fewer sources. The Pro Series parts are still findable because enough machines were produced, but you might wait a week for something that would ship overnight for an IGT. The Alpha and V-series video platform parts are more specialized, and some components are genuinely hard to source.

We keep a deep inventory of Bally parts specifically because we know our customers will need them and cannot always find them elsewhere.

### Maintenance Complexity

Bally machines are moderately complex to maintain. They are not difficult if you have experience, but they are less forgiving than IGT for a first-timer. Error codes require the manual (they are not as intuitive). Some adjustments require Bally-specific tools or knowledge. The Pro Series mechanical reel machines are actually quite serviceable, but the video platforms can be challenging for non-technicians.

### Resale Value

Bally machines hold their value extremely well, often appreciating in certain cases. The Quick Hit series has become iconic and machines in good condition command a premium. U-Spin machines (with the physical spinning wheel) are highly sought after for home game rooms and consistently sell at the top of their price range. Clean Bally Pro Series machines are collector items.

### Best For

Buyers who want a premium machine and are willing to pay more upfront. People who value aesthetics and build quality. Collectors. Anyone who specifically wants Quick Hit, U-Spin, or other iconic Bally titles.

## Aristocrat: The Innovation Leader

### Company History

Aristocrat Leisure Limited is an Australian company founded in 1953. They have been making gaming machines longer than most people realize. They entered the US market aggressively in the 2000s and have steadily gained market share with innovative game design and mathematics. If you ask a casino floor manager which brand players gravitate toward, the answer is increasingly Aristocrat.

### Cabinet Types

Aristocrat cabinets on the secondary market include the MK5 (Mark 5, their older video platform from the early 2000s), the MK6 and MK6 XP (the workhorse platform that runs Buffalo and most of their major titles), the VIRIDIAN (a wide-body upright with premium displays), the Helix (a curved-screen premium cabinet), and the Arc (double-curved ultrawide displays).

The MK6 is the platform you will encounter most often if you are looking for Aristocrat games. It is a solid upright cabinet with good displays and reliable hardware. Not as heavy-duty as an IGT S2000, not as refined as a Bally Pro Series, but purposeful and well-designed.

### Build Quality

Aristocrat build quality is good but not exceptional on the cabinet side. What sets Aristocrat apart is game design, not hardware engineering. The cabinets are competent, well-built machines that do their job without calling attention to themselves. The MK6 platform uses standard components where possible, and the internal layout is logical if not elegant.

The newer Aristocrat cabinets (Helix, Arc) are premium pieces with excellent build quality, large displays, and impressive visual presence. But these rarely hit the secondary market at accessible prices yet.

### Parts Availability

Aristocrat parts are moderately available. Their growing US market share means more parts are entering the supply chain, but they still lag behind IGT significantly. The MK5 platform parts are getting scarce because those machines are aging out. MK6 parts are available through specialty suppliers and directly from Aristocrat's service division.

One advantage: Aristocrat uses some standard industry components (like JCM bill acceptors and standard power supplies) that are easy to source regardless of brand.

### Maintenance Complexity

Aristocrat machines fall in the middle. They are not as straightforward as IGT, but not as proprietary as Bally. The MK6 platform uses a standard PC-based architecture that any competent technician can navigate. The game software is loaded from a proprietary cartridge system, which simplifies game changes but means you need the right cartridge for the title you want.

The most common issue we see with Aristocrat machines is CMOS battery failure, which is an easy fix once you know what to look for. Our tech support team walks customers through it regularly.

### Resale Value

Aristocrat machines have the strongest resale value in the industry right now, driven almost entirely by demand for Buffalo and its variants. A clean MK6 running Buffalo will sell for more today than it sold for three years ago. That is unusual in this market. Other popular Aristocrat titles (5 Dragons, Queen of the Nile, Indian Dreaming, 50 Lions) also hold value well.

### Best For

Anyone who wants specific game titles, particularly Buffalo. Players who care more about the game experience than the cabinet. Buyers looking for investment value (Aristocrat machines are appreciating). People who want games their friends will recognize from the casino.

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

## The Secondary Brands

### WMS (Williams/Scientific Games)

WMS made some of the most beloved slot games ever produced. Monopoly, Wizard of Oz, Jackpot Party, Zeus, and dozens of others. Their Bluebird and Bluebird 2 cabinets are distinctive with their round top displays and excellent sound systems. WMS machines are great for game rooms because the titles are recognizable and fun.

The challenge with WMS is parts. After Scientific Games acquired them, support for older platforms became spotty. The Bluebird cabinet uses some components that are genuinely difficult to source. If you buy a WMS machine, buy from a dealer who can support it long-term. We stock WMS parts specifically for this reason.

Build quality is good. Resale value is moderate to strong depending on the title. Monopoly machines always sell.

### Konami

Konami makes excellent video slot machines with distinctive Asian-themed games (China Shores, Dragon's Law, Lotus Land) and strong all-around titles. Their cabinets are well-built and use mostly standard components. The Podium and Concerto platforms are reliable workhorses.

Parts availability is fair. Konami has a smaller installed base in the US, so parts suppliers carry less inventory. But the machines themselves are reliable and rarely need major service.

Konami is a solid choice for someone who wants something different from the big three without sacrificing reliability.

### Ainsworth

Ainsworth is another Australian manufacturer (actually founded by Aristocrat's original founder, Len Ainsworth, after he sold Aristocrat). They make good machines with solid games. The A600 platform is their workhorse. Build quality is comparable to Aristocrat.

Parts can be challenging to find because their US installed base is smaller. But the machines are well-engineered and rarely need anything beyond routine maintenance.

Popular titles include Mustang Money and Eagle Bucks. These are good machines at usually lower prices than comparable Aristocrat units.

### Aruze

Aruze (now Universal Entertainment) is a Japanese manufacturer that makes distinctive machines often featuring innovative bonus round mechanics. Their cabinets are well-built with good displays. The Muso and Innovator platforms are solid.

Parts availability is the weakest of any brand we carry. Aruze machines are great when they work and frustrating when they need parts. We sell them, we support them, but we make sure buyers understand the parts situation upfront.

## Head-to-Head Comparison

### Reliability (Best to Acceptable)

IGT leads reliability because of simple, proven designs and massive field testing across hundreds of thousands of installations. Bally is close behind with excellent engineering. Aristocrat is solid with occasional quirks. WMS and Konami are reliable. Ainsworth and Aruze have smaller sample sizes but perform well.

### Parts Availability (Best to Most Difficult)

IGT is unmatched. Bally is good but thinner. WMS is getting harder. Aristocrat is moderate and improving. Konami is fair. Ainsworth is challenging. Aruze is the hardest.

### Ease of Service (Simplest to Most Complex)

IGT S2000 is the easiest machine in the industry to service. IGT Game King requires moderate knowledge. Bally Pro Series is straightforward for mechanicals. Aristocrat MK6 is middle of the pack. Bally video platforms require experience. WMS Bluebird can be tricky. Konami and Ainsworth are moderate. Aruze requires manufacturer-specific knowledge.

### Resale Value (Strongest to Weakest)

Aristocrat leads right now due to Buffalo demand. Bally holds well due to collector interest and iconic titles. IGT is stable and predictable. WMS holds on title strength. Konami, Ainsworth, and Aruze are softer but reasonable.

### Game Library (Most Recognizable Titles)

IGT has Double Diamond, Triple Diamond, Wheel of Fortune, Red White and Blue, Cleopatra, and Texas Tea. Bally has Quick Hit, Blazing 7s, U-Spin, and Cash Wizard. Aristocrat has Buffalo, 5 Dragons, Queen of the Nile, and 50 Lions. WMS has Monopoly, Wizard of Oz, Zeus, and Jackpot Party. Each brand has titles that players recognize instantly.

## Making Your Decision

### Budget Under $1,500

You are looking at IGT S2000 reel machines or older IGT Game Kings. This is where IGT dominates the value proposition. You get a reliable, serviceable machine with parts everywhere and games people love. Double Diamond and Red White and Blue are classics for good reason.

### Budget $1,500 to $2,500

The sweet spot opens up considerably. Newer IGT video machines, Bally Alpha platforms, base-model Aristocrat MK6 units, and WMS Bluebirds all compete in this range. Choose based on which games you want and how comfortable you are with maintenance.

### Budget $2,500 to $4,000

Premium territory. Aristocrat Buffalo variants, Bally U-Spin machines, loaded multi-game setups, and newer video platforms from all manufacturers. At this price point, buy the game you love and the brand becomes secondary.

### Budget Over $4,000

You are looking at rare titles, premium cabinets, multi-machine setups, or the newest technology available on the secondary market. Collector pieces and conversation starters.

## Our Recommendation

After 33 years and over 10,000 machines sold, here is my honest advice: buy the game, not the brand. If you love Buffalo, buy an Aristocrat. If you love Wheel of Fortune, buy an IGT. If you love Quick Hit, buy a Bally. The machine you play most is the one with your favorite game on it.

But if you have no strong preference and just want a solid machine for your game room, IGT is the safest choice. Parts are everywhere, any tech can work on them, they hold value, and Double Diamond on a mechanical reel machine is still one of the most satisfying experiences in gaming.

We carry all seven brands, we stock parts for all of them, and our free lifetime tech support covers every machine we sell regardless of manufacturer. Come see our [334 machines in stock](/shop) at our Kingman showroom, or call us to talk through which brand and model fits your situation.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: How Much Electricity Does a Slot Machine Use? (Real Numbers)
excerpt: Real wattage measurements and monthly cost calculations for slot machines at home, from classic reel games to multi-screen video cabinets.
body:
One of the first questions buyers ask after "where do I put it" is "how much is this going to cost me in electricity?" After 33 years of selling machines and helping customers plan their game rooms, I can give you real numbers based on actual measurements, not guesses.

## Actual Wattage by Machine Type

Slot machines are not all created equal when it comes to power consumption. The range is significant depending on what type of machine you own.

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

### Mechanical Reel Machines (200-300 Watts)

A classic three-reel machine like the IGT S2000 running Double Diamond pulls between 200 and 300 watts during active play. The main power draws are the fluorescent backlight behind the glass (about 80W), the reel motors during spin (brief surges to 60W total, but only for a few seconds), the logic board and electronics (about 40W constant), and the bill acceptor and hopper motor (intermittent, 30-50W when active).

When the machine is idle (powered on but not being played), it drops to about 150-180 watts because the reels are not spinning and the hopper is not running. The backlight stays on, which is the biggest constant draw.

### Standard Video Slots (350-500 Watts)

A video slot machine with one or two LCD displays (like a Bally Alpha, Aristocrat MK6, or IGT AVP) pulls 350-500 watts during play. The displays are the primary consumers here. A 22-inch LCD panel draws 80-120 watts on its own. Add a second display for the top box and you are at 160-240 watts just for screens. The rest goes to the computer board, amplified speakers, LED lighting, and bill acceptor.

Idle draw on video machines is 280-380 watts because those displays stay lit.

### Multi-Screen and Premium Cabinets (450-600 Watts)

The newer premium cabinets with large curved displays, multiple screens, and enhanced lighting packages (think IGT Crystal, Aristocrat Helix, or Bally U-Spin with the mechanical wheel) pull 450-600 watts. The U-Spin wheel motor adds about 50 watts during bonus rounds. The larger displays (32-inch and up) draw 130-150 watts each.

## Monthly Cost Calculations

Let me do the math for you with two common scenarios: casual home use and heavy use.

### Casual Use: 2 Hours Per Day

This is the typical home game room owner who plays a couple rounds after dinner or when friends come over.

At the national average rate of $0.12 per kilowatt-hour:

- Reel machine (250W average): 250W x 2 hours x 30 days = 15,000 Wh = 15 kWh x $0.12 = $1.80 per month
- Video slot (425W average): 425W x 2 hours x 30 days = 25,500 Wh = 25.5 kWh x $0.12 = $3.06 per month
- Premium cabinet (525W average): 525W x 2 hours x 30 days = 31,500 Wh = 31.5 kWh x $0.12 = $3.78 per month

At a higher rate of $0.20 per kilowatt-hour (California, New England, Hawaii):

- Reel machine: 15 kWh x $0.20 = $3.00 per month
- Video slot: 25.5 kWh x $0.20 = $5.10 per month
- Premium cabinet: 31.5 kWh x $0.20 = $6.30 per month

### Heavy Use: 8 Hours Per Day

This covers party situations, dedicated game rooms that stay on all day, or small business installations.

At $0.12 per kilowatt-hour:

- Reel machine (250W): 250W x 8 hours x 30 days = 60 kWh x $0.12 = $7.20 per month
- Video slot (425W): 425W x 8 hours x 30 days = 102 kWh x $0.12 = $12.24 per month
- Premium cabinet (525W): 525W x 8 hours x 30 days = 126 kWh x $0.12 = $15.12 per month

At $0.20 per kilowatt-hour:

- Reel machine: 60 kWh x $0.20 = $12.00 per month
- Video slot: 102 kWh x $0.20 = $20.40 per month
- Premium cabinet: 126 kWh x $0.20 = $25.20 per month

## Comparison to Common Appliances

To put these numbers in perspective, here is how a slot machine compares to things already running in your home:

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

- A slot machine at 2 hours per day uses about the same electricity as a 60-watt light bulb left on 8 hours per day
- A desktop gaming computer during active use pulls 400-600 watts, the same as a video slot
- A 55-inch LED television draws 80-120 watts, about half of what powers the displays in a video slot
- A refrigerator uses about 100-200 watts continuously (40-60 kWh per month), making it a bigger electricity user than a casually-used slot machine
- A space heater at 1,500 watts draws three to five times more power than any slot machine

The bottom line: a slot machine used recreationally in your home adds less to your electric bill than your refrigerator. It is not a meaningful expense.

## Tips to Reduce Power Consumption

### Turn It Off When Not Playing

This sounds obvious, but many people leave their machines on 24/7 because they look cool lit up in the game room. A video slot left on around the clock at idle consumes about 200-250 kWh per month, which is $24-50 depending on your rate. Just flip the switch when you walk out of the room.

Slot machines are designed to be powered on and off. Unlike a computer that needs a graceful shutdown, a slot machine handles cold starts without any issue. The only consideration is the CMOS battery that maintains settings, which we will cover in a moment.

### Upgrade to LED Backlighting

Older reel machines use fluorescent tubes for the backlit glass and reel strips. These draw 60-100 watts and generate heat. LED replacement kits are available for most IGT and Bally cabinets, dropping that draw to 15-25 watts while producing brighter, more even light. The kits cost $30-60 and take about 20 minutes to install. Over a year of daily use, the LED upgrade pays for itself in electricity savings.

### Use a Smart Plug or Power Strip Timer

If you want your machine to be on when you walk into the game room at 6 PM and off after midnight, a $15 smart plug handles this automatically. Set a schedule and forget about it. The machine boots in under 30 seconds, so there is no warmup time to worry about.

### Keep It Clean

A slot machine with clogged ventilation fans runs hotter and the power supply works harder to compensate. Vacuum the intake vents every few months. A machine that runs cool runs more efficiently.

## The Hidden Power Cost Nobody Mentions

Here is something most people overlook: heat. Every watt a slot machine consumes ultimately becomes heat in your room. A 425-watt video slot running for four hours dumps about 1,700 watt-hours of heat into your space. In winter, that is free heating. In summer, your air conditioner has to remove that heat, effectively doubling the electricity cost.

If your game room is in a basement, this is rarely a problem. If it is in an uncooled garage in Arizona (I live here, I know), you probably will not play in summer anyway. But if your game room is in a climate-controlled part of your house, factor in a modest AC cost during hot months.

## What About the Startup Surge?

Slot machines do draw a brief surge when first powered on, as the power supply capacitors charge and the displays initialize.
