This surge lasts less than one second and is about double the normal wattage. It is completely insignificant for your electric bill and will not trip a household circuit breaker. A standard 15-amp household circuit can handle two slot machines simultaneously without any issues.

## Bottom Line

A slot machine in your home will cost you between $2 and $25 per month in electricity depending on the type of machine and how much you use it. For most home owners running a reel machine or video slot a couple hours per day, you are looking at $3-5 per month. That is less than a single trip to Starbucks. It should not be a factor in your buying decision.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Aristocrat Buffalo Slot Machine: The Complete Buyer's Guide
excerpt: Everything you need to know before buying a Buffalo slot machine for your home, including variants, platforms, pricing, and what makes it hold value.
body:
Buffalo is the single most requested slot machine title we sell. It has been that way for years and the demand keeps growing. Every week we get calls from people who played Buffalo at a casino and need one in their game room. I get it. The game is genuinely great. But there is a lot to know before you buy, and not every Buffalo machine for sale is worth your money.

## Why Buffalo Is the Most Popular Slot Machine in America

Aristocrat released the original Buffalo in 2008 and it became a phenomenon. The game hits a sweet spot that other titles miss: the volatility is high enough to create genuine excitement, the free games feature triggers often enough to keep you engaged, and the stacked wilds create those "big win" moments that players chase.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

The sunset-and-prairie aesthetic is iconic at this point. The animals (buffalo, eagle, wolf, mountain lion, and moose) are instantly recognizable to anyone who has walked a casino floor in the last 15 years. When people visit your game room and see Buffalo, they light up because they know the game.

From a technical standpoint, Aristocrat's mathematics on Buffalo are brilliant. The 1024-ways-to-win format (replacing traditional paylines) gives players more winning combinations per spin while maintaining the house edge. It feels more generous without actually being more generous. That is world-class game design.

## Buffalo Variants

Aristocrat has released multiple versions of Buffalo over the years. Here is what you need to know about each:

### Buffalo (Original)

The original 2008 release running on the MK5 or early MK6 platform. This is the pure classic experience with 1024 ways, free games triggered by three or more scatter coins, and the sunset wild that multiplies wins by 2x or 3x during free games. Many purists prefer the original because the math model feels the most balanced.

### Buffalo Gold

Adds a gold coin collection mechanic to the base game. As you play, gold buffalo symbols appear and fill a grid. Once you collect enough, you enter a special free games round with enhanced features. This adds a layer of progression to the session that keeps players going longer.

### Buffalo Grand

The premium version designed for high-traffic casino floor positions. It features a progressive jackpot element and enhanced free games. The cabinet was typically a larger Aristocrat frame with a prominent top box displaying the progressive meters. These are the most expensive Buffalo machines on the secondary market.

### Buffalo Extra Reel Power

Expands the reel set from 5 reels to 6, increasing ways-to-win from 1024 to 4096. This changes the game dynamics noticeably: wins hit more frequently but individual pays are smaller to compensate. Some players love the increased action. Others prefer the original 5-reel volatility.

### Buffalo Gold Revolution

A newer variant with even more features layered onto the Gold formula. These are harder to find on the secondary market because they are still relatively new and casinos have not retired many of them yet.

## MK5 vs. MK6 Platform: What It Means For You

This is the most important technical distinction when shopping for a Buffalo machine.

### MK5 (Mark 5)

The MK5 is Aristocrat's older platform from the early 2000s. It uses a proprietary processor board, older display technology, and a specific game cartridge format. The original Buffalo was first deployed on MK5 hardware in some markets.

Pros: Lower price point, proven hardware, simpler electronics.

Cons: Parts are getting scarce as the platform ages out. Display quality is noticeably lower than MK6. The cabinet styling looks dated. Finding replacement MK5 game boards is increasingly difficult.

My recommendation: Unless you are specifically buying on a tight budget and understand the parts risk, go MK6.

### MK6 (Mark 6) and MK6 XP

The MK6 is Aristocrat's workhorse platform that was current through most of Buffalo's prime years. It uses a more modern processor architecture, better displays, and a cartridge-based game loading system that is straightforward to work with.

Pros: Better displays, readily available parts, more supported by the industry, cleaner cabinet styling, handles all Buffalo variants, more reliable long-term.

Cons: Higher price than MK5 units. Still a proprietary system that requires Aristocrat game cartridges.

The MK6 XP is an enhanced version with slightly better hardware. For practical purposes as a home buyer, MK6 and MK6 XP are interchangeable in terms of experience.

## Pricing on the Secondary Market

As of today, here is what clean, tested, warrantied Buffalo machines sell for:

### MK5 Buffalo (Original): $2,495 - $2,995

The lower end of the range gets you a working machine that shows its age cosmetically. The higher end is a clean example with good displays and fresh maintenance.

### MK6 Buffalo (Original or Gold): $2,995 - $3,495

This is the sweet spot for most buyers. A solid MK6 cabinet with a Buffalo variant, tested and warrantied, ready to plug in and play.

### MK6 Buffalo Grand: $3,495 - $3,995

The premium variant in the better cabinet commands a premium price. Worth it if you want the Grand experience specifically.

### MK6 Buffalo Extra Reel Power: $2,995 - $3,495

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

Priced similarly to the standard MK6 Buffalo. Some buyers prefer the 4096-ways format, others want the classic 1024.

These prices have been stable or rising over the past three years. Buffalo machines are not depreciating. Demand outpaces supply consistently.

## What Should Be Included With Your Purchase

When you buy a Buffalo machine from a reputable dealer, the following should be included:

- Complete cabinet in tested, working condition
- Buffalo game cartridge installed and verified
- Bill acceptor (JCM or MEI) accepting $1, $5, $10, $20 bills at minimum
- Functioning displays (main game and top box if applicable)
- Printer or hopper for cashout (most use ticket printers now)
- Power cord
- Cabinet keys (belly door, main door, and reset key)
- Operator manual or at minimum setup instructions
- Warranty (we include one year parts and labor)
- Tech support for setup and ongoing questions

If a seller cannot confirm all of these items, ask questions. A machine sold "as-is" without testing or warranty is a gamble. The game board alone can cost $800-1,200 to replace if it fails.

## Game Features That Make Buffalo Special

For buyers who have not played extensively, here is what makes the gameplay compelling:

The 1024-ways format means any matching symbol on adjacent reels (left to right) pays. You do not need symbols on specific paylines. This creates more winning combinations and more visual excitement per spin.

The free games feature triggers when three or more gold coin scatter symbols land anywhere on the reels. Three coins give 8 free games, four give 15, and five give 20. During free games, the sunset wild symbol carries a 2x or 3x multiplier. When multiple wilds appear in a single spin, the multipliers multiply together. Two 3x wilds on one spin means 9x your win. This is where Buffalo creates those huge payoffs that players remember.

The free games retrigger indefinitely. I have seen sessions where players retrigger four or five times, accumulating 50 or more free spins. These extended runs are where the game builds its reputation.

## Why Buffalo Holds Its Value

Three factors keep Buffalo machines at stable or rising prices:

First, demand never slows down. Buffalo is still one of the top-performing games on casino floors 18 years after release. That is almost unheard of. As long as people play it in casinos, they want it at home.

Second, supply is limited. Aristocrat does not sell MK6 cabinets to the home market. Every machine on the secondary market came from a casino that retired it. As casinos upgrade to newer Aristocrat platforms, MK6 Buffalo machines enter the used market, but it is a trickle, not a flood.

Third, the game is genuinely excellent. Some slot titles are popular for a moment and fade. Buffalo has proven itself over nearly two decades. It is not going out of style.

## Common Issues and What to Watch For

The most common issues we see with used Aristocrat Buffalo machines:

CMOS battery failure causes the machine to lose its settings on power-off. This is a $3 battery replacement that takes 10 minutes. We replace these as part of our reconditioning process, but if you buy from another source, ask.

Display degradation on older MK6 units manifests as dim spots or color shifting. The LCD panels are replaceable but cost $200-400 for the panel alone. Check that the displays look bright and even before buying.

Bill acceptor issues are the most common service call on any machine. The JCM iVizion and MEI units in Aristocrat cabinets are reliable but need cleaning every few months for heavy use. A can of compressed air and a cleaning card keep them working perfectly.

Game cartridge errors are rare but serious. If the machine cannot read the game cartridge, that cartridge may need replacement at significant cost. Always buy from a dealer who has verified the game loads and runs correctly.

## Our Stock and Recommendation

We typically have multiple Buffalo variants in stock at any given time. Our recommendation for most buyers is an MK6 cabinet running Buffalo Gold. It gives you the classic Buffalo experience with the added gold coin collection mechanic that extends play sessions and adds variety. The MK6 platform ensures parts availability for years to come.

Every Buffalo machine we sell is fully reconditioned with new CMOS battery, cleaned and tested bill acceptor, verified game operation, and a full one-year warranty backed by our [free lifetime tech support](/warranty). We ship nationwide and can usually have your machine on a truck within a few days of purchase.

If Buffalo is your game, you will not regret the purchase. It is the one title we sell where buyers call back just to tell us how much they love it.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: What Is Free Play Mode on a Slot Machine?
excerpt: Free play mode lets you enjoy your slot machine without inserting money. Here is how it works, how to enable it, and why most home owners use it.
body:
Free play mode is the single most important feature for home slot machine owners, and it is also the most misunderstood. Every week we explain it to new buyers, answer questions about legality, and walk customers through enabling it on their machines. Let me clear up everything in one place.

## What Free Play Mode Actually Does

Free play mode (also called free play, freeplay, or auto-credit) automatically loads credits onto the machine without requiring money. When you power on the machine, it starts with a predetermined number of credits. As you play and the credits decrease, the machine periodically replenishes them back to the set amount. Some machines replenish when credits hit zero. Others replenish at a set threshold (for example, when credits drop below 100, it auto-loads back to 1000).

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

The result: you sit down, press the spin button, and play. No coins, no bills, no tickets. Just pure entertainment. When you are done, you walk away. The next time you (or a guest) sits down, the credits are there waiting.

## How It Works Technically

Slot machines have an internal accounting system that tracks every credit in and every credit out. In a casino, this system is critical for regulatory compliance, tax reporting, and revenue tracking. In your game room, it is irrelevant.

Free play mode works by setting a configuration option in the machine's operator menu (also called the setup menu, service menu, or attendant menu). This is a password-protected area that casino technicians use to configure the machine for their floor. One of the configurable options is the credit mode.

The exact implementation varies by manufacturer:

On IGT machines, you access the operator menu by opening the main door and pressing the service button (or a specific button combination). Navigate to the accounting or credit menu and look for "free play" or "auto-credit" settings. You set the number of credits to auto-load and the replenishment trigger.

On Bally machines, the setup menu is accessed similarly through the service panel. The free play option is typically under game configuration or credit parameters. Bally calls it "**free play mode**" explicitly.

On Aristocrat machines, the operator menu (accessed with the attendant key and reset button combination) has a clear free play toggle. Aristocrat machines handle free play cleanly with a simple on/off setting and a credit amount field.

## How to Enable Free Play on Your Machine

Every machine we sell ships with free play already enabled and configured. You plug it in, turn it on, and play. We set the credit amount based on the denomination (typically 1000 credits for a penny machine, 500 for a nickel, 200 for a quarter, and 100 for a dollar).

If you buy a machine elsewhere or need to change the free play settings, here is the general process:

### Step 1: Access the Operator Menu

This requires opening the main door of the machine with the included key. Inside, there is either a service button, a specific key switch, or a button combination that enters the setup screen. The exact method depends on your manufacturer and model.

### Step 2: Navigate to Credit Settings

Use the buttons on the service panel or screen to navigate through the menu structure. You are looking for sections labeled credits, free play, auto-credit, or game configuration.

### Step 3: Enable Free Play and Set Amount

Toggle free play to ON (or YES or ENABLED, depending on the machine). Set the credit amount you want loaded. Set the replenishment behavior.

### Step 4: Save and Exit

Confirm your changes and exit the operator menu. Close the door. The machine should now display your set credit amount.

This sounds straightforward written out, but the menu systems on these machines were designed for trained technicians, not home users. The interface can be confusing if you have never seen it before. This is exactly why we offer [free lifetime tech support](/warranty). Call us and we walk you through it step by step for your specific machine. Most calls take under 10 minutes.

## Legal Implications: Why Free Play Matters

Here is the critical legal point: a slot machine operating in free play mode is not a gambling device. Gambling requires three elements: consideration (you risk something of value), chance (the outcome is random), and prize (you receive something of value if you win). Remove any one of those elements and it is legally not gambling.

Free play mode removes both consideration and prize. You are not inserting money, and you cannot cash out for money. The machine is purely entertainment, like a video game. This distinction is what makes it legal to own a slot machine in your home in most states.

Some states have additional regulations about slot machine ownership (age requirements, restrictions on certain machine types, or registration requirements), but the gambling element is moot when the machine is in free play mode. You are not operating an illegal gambling device. You are operating an entertainment device.

We always recommend checking your specific state and local laws before purchasing. We can help point you in the right direction for your jurisdiction.

## Why Most Home Owners Use Free Play

The vast majority of our customers, probably 95 percent, use their machines exclusively in free play mode. Here is why:

### No Hassle

You do not need to keep coins or bills around. You do not need to explain to guests how to insert money and cash out. You just point them at the machine and say "press the button." It eliminates all friction between your guest and a good time.

### No Accounting

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

Coin-operated machines fill up their hoppers and need to be emptied. Bill acceptor cash boxes need to be counted and cleared. Ticket printers need paper rolls. In free play mode, none of this matters. The machine just runs.

### Social Simplicity

Imagine having a poker night and your buddy wants to try the slot machine. In credit mode, do you charge him? Does he keep the winnings? What about the friend who puts in $20 and loses it in three minutes? Free play eliminates all social awkwardness. Everyone plays for fun, no money changes hands, no hard feelings.

### Family Friendly

With free play enabled, your slot machine is no different from an arcade game or pinball machine. Kids can press buttons without any concern about money. It is entertainment, full stop.

## Common Misconceptions

### Misconception: Free Play Damages the Machine

False. The machine does not know or care whether the credits came from a bill acceptor or from auto-credit. The game plays identically. The random number generator works identically. The reels spin identically. Free play is a built-in feature, not a hack.

### Misconception: You Need Special Software for Free Play

False. Free play is a standard configuration option on every commercial slot machine manufactured in the last 30 years. It exists because casinos use it for demonstration purposes, trade shows, and testing. It is not an aftermarket modification.

### Misconception: Free Play Means the Machine Never Pays

False. The machine plays exactly like it would in a casino. The random number generator produces the same results regardless of credit source. You will hit bonus rounds, free spins, and jackpots just like normal. The only difference is that the credits are not tied to real money. You still get the excitement of a big win, you just cannot cash it out for dollars.

### Misconception: Free Play Mode Is Illegal

False (in most jurisdictions). As explained above, free play mode removes the gambling element entirely. However, slot machine ownership laws do vary by state, so always verify your local regulations. The mode itself is not illegal. Whether you can own the machine at all depends on where you live.

### Misconception: The Machine Needs Internet for Free Play

False. Slot machines do not use the internet. They are self-contained. Free play is configured locally on the machine itself and requires no external connection of any kind.

## Can You Switch Between Free Play and Credit Mode?

Yes. The setting is reversible at any time by accessing the operator menu. Some customers use free play for everyday casual use and switch to credit mode for special occasions (poker night, parties where they want to set up a mini casino experience). This is a matter of personal preference and local law.

If you do use credit mode for parties, the machine accepts bills and pays out in coins (for machines with hoppers) or printed tickets (for machines with printers). It functions identically to how it operated in a casino.

## Our Setup Process

When you buy a machine from us, here is what happens regarding free play:

Before shipping, we configure free play mode with an appropriate credit amount for the denomination. We test it to make sure credits load properly on power-up and replenish correctly during play. We verify that the game functions normally in free play mode.

When your machine arrives, you plug it in and it is ready to play immediately. No setup required on your end. If you ever want to change the credit amount, switch to credit mode, or adjust any settings, you call us and we walk you through it.

Free play mode turns a slot machine from a complex gambling device into a simple entertainment appliance. Plug it in and press spin. That is the experience we deliver with every machine we sell.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Where to Find Slot Machine Parts: Bill Acceptors, Hoppers, and More
excerpt: A dealer's guide to sourcing slot machine replacement parts, typical costs, when to DIY, and what your warranty covers.
body:
Slot machines are mechanical and electronic devices with moving parts, and moving parts eventually wear out. After 33 years in this business, I can tell you exactly which parts fail, how often, what they cost, and where to find them. Most repairs are straightforward enough for a motivated home owner. Some are not. Here is the complete guide.

## Parts That Wear Out (In Order of Frequency)

### Bill Acceptors (Bill Validators)

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

The bill acceptor is the number one service item on any slot machine. It is a precision optical and mechanical device that must identify, validate, and stack paper currency at speed. The rollers get dirty, the optical sensors get dusty, and the stacking mechanism can jam.

Most machines use one of two brands: JCM (models iVizion, WBA, or older DBV series) or MEI (now CPI, models SC66, SC83, or APEX series). Both are excellent products that last for years with basic maintenance.

Common failures: Bill rejection (machine will not accept bills), stacking jams (bill gets stuck inside), communication errors (acceptor loses connection to the logic board).

Maintenanceschedule: Clean the optical path with a cleaning card every 1-3 months depending on usage. Blow dust out with compressed air. Check the rollers for debris.

Replacement cost: A new JCM iVizion runs $300-500. A refurbished unit costs $150-250. MEI/CPI units are similar. Older models (JCM DBV-200, MEI SC66) can be found for $75-150 refurbished.

### CMOS Batteries

Every slot machine has a small lithium battery (usually CR2032 or 1/2 AA) on the logic board that maintains memory when the machine is powered off. These batteries last 3-7 years and then die without warning. When they fail, the machine loses its settings, game configuration, and audit data on every power cycle.

Symptoms: Machine powers on with error codes about memory or NVRAM. Machine asks for setup configuration every time you turn it on. Machine will not boot past the initial screen.

Replacement cost: The battery itself is $2-5 at any electronics store or online. It is a 10-minute replacement once you know which battery your board uses and where it is located.

This is the single most common call we get on our tech support line. We walk people through it multiple times per week. It is always an easy fix.

### Power Supplies

Slot machine power supplies convert household AC power (120V) to the various DC voltages the machine needs (typically +5V, +12V, +24V, and sometimes +48V). They run hot, and heat kills capacitors over time. A failing power supply causes random shutdowns, display flickering, boot failures, or complete failure to power on.

Most machines use standard ATX-style or proprietary switching power supplies. IGT S2000 machines use a specific IGT power supply that is widely available. Bally machines often use Astec or similar branded supplies. Aristocrat uses standard form factor supplies that are easy to cross-reference.

Replacement cost: $50-150 for most models. IGT S2000 supplies are $60-80. A universal replacement for Bally or Aristocrat runs $80-120. Premium or hard-to-find models can hit $200.

### Hopper Motors and Assemblies

If your machine pays out in coins (rather than printing tickets), it has a coin hopper with a motor that spins a disk to dispense coins. The motor runs hard every time the machine pays, and the coin-moving mechanism wears over time.

Symptoms: Machine will not pay out (hopper jam error), loud grinding noise during payout, intermittent payouts (starts then stops).

Hoppers come in different sizes (500 coin, 1000 coin, 2000 coin) and different coin diameters. Make sure you get the right one for your machine and denomination.

Replacement cost: Complete hopper assemblies run $75-200 depending on the manufacturer and size. Just the motor is $30-60. The coin disk (the part that actually contacts coins) is $20-40.

Note: Many home owners switch to a ticket printer instead of a hopper, eliminating this wear item entirely. A printer setup costs $200-350 but saves hassle long-term.

### Reel Mechanisms (Mechanical Reel Machines Only)

On mechanical reel machines, each reel is driven by a stepper motor with an optical encoder that tells the machine exactly where the reel is positioned. The motors are reliable but the optical sensors can get dirty, and the reel strips (the paper or mylar band showing symbols) can fade or tear.

Symptoms: Reel stops in wrong position (tilt error), reel will not spin (motor failure), faded or torn symbols (cosmetic, not functional).

Replacement cost: Complete reel mechanism $100-200. Stepper motor alone $40-80. Reel strip (the symbol band) $15-30. Optical sensor board $30-60.

### Buttons and Touch Screens

Buttons on a slot machine get pressed thousands of times. The spin button and max bet button take the most abuse. Eventually the microswitch inside fails or the button lamp burns out.

Most machines use standard IGT or Bally button assemblies with T3-1/4 wedge base lamps. The button housing lasts forever but the microswitch and lamp are consumables.

Replacement cost: Complete button assembly $10-25. Microswitch only $2-5. Button lamp $1-3. LED replacement lamps (never burn out) $3-8 each.

Touch screens can develop dead spots or lose calibration. Replacement touch screen panels cost $100-300 depending on size and type (resistive vs capacitive vs infrared).

### Monitors and Displays

LCD panels and older CRT monitors degrade over time. LCDs develop backlight failure (dim or dark areas), dead pixels, or color shift. CRTs (found in older machines) lose brightness, develop geometry issues, or fail entirely.

Replacement cost: LCD panel replacement $150-400 depending on size. CRT monitors are increasingly hard to find; LCD conversion kits ($200-350) are usually the better option for older machines.

## OEM vs. Aftermarket Parts

OEM parts are manufactured by or for the original machine manufacturer (IGT, Bally, Aristocrat, etc.). Aftermarket parts are made by third parties to fit the same machines.

### When OEM Matters

Logic boards, game software, and proprietary electronics should always be OEM. These are precisely engineered for specific platforms and aftermarket alternatives either do not exist or are unreliable. A game cartridge must be genuine Aristocrat. An IGT processor board must be genuine IGT.

### When Aftermarket Is Fine

Power supplies, lamps, batteries, generic switches, cooling fans, and similar commodity components work perfectly in aftermarket versions. A 12V cooling fan is a 12V cooling fan regardless of who made it. A CR2032 battery from Energizer works the same as one from any other brand.

Bill acceptors are a middle ground. OEM refurbished units are preferred, but aftermarket-rebuilt acceptors from reputable rebuilders work fine. Avoid cheap no-name imports.

## Where to Source Parts

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

### From Us (Used Slot Shop)

We stock the most common parts for every brand we sell. Bill acceptors, power supplies, hoppers, buttons, batteries, reel mechanisms, and more. When you buy a machine from us, parts availability is part of the relationship. We keep inventory specifically so our customers are not hunting for parts.

Advantage: We know exactly what part your machine needs because we sold you the machine. No guessing, no compatibility questions.

### Manufacturer Direct

IGT, Aristocrat, and Light and Wonder (Bally) all have parts departments that sell to licensed operators. Access can be difficult for home owners since manufacturers typically sell to licensed gaming businesses. However, some parts (particularly consumables like cleaning kits, lamps, and paper rolls) are available through their authorized distributors without licensing requirements.

### Specialty Suppliers

Several companies specialize in used and refurbished slot machine parts. They buy machines for parts inventory and sell individual components. Names you will encounter include Slot Machines Unlimited, Slot Machine Parts Direct, and various eBay sellers who specialize in this niche.

Advantage: Wide selection, competitive pricing. Disadvantage: You need to know exactly what part number or specification you need. There is no tech support to help you diagnose the problem.

### Online Marketplaces

eBay has a large selection of slot machine parts from various sellers. Prices range from excellent to absurd depending on the seller and how rare the part is. Quality ranges from tested-and-warrantied to pulled-from-a-junk-machine-no-returns.

Tips for buying parts on eBay: Buy from sellers with high feedback scores and slot-specific inventory (not general electronics sellers). Look for listings that say "tested working" or "refurbished." Avoid listings with stock photos instead of actual photos of the part. Check the return policy before buying.

## When to DIY vs. Call a Tech

### DIY-Friendly Repairs

These repairs require no special skills beyond basic mechanical ability and willingness to follow instructions:

- Battery replacement (10 minutes, just swap a coin cell)
- Button lamp replacement (5 minutes per button, unplug old lamp, plug in new one)
- Bill acceptor cleaning (5 minutes, insert cleaning card, blow with compressed air)
- Bill acceptor replacement (15 minutes, unplug old unit, plug in new unit, mount with two screws)
- Power supply replacement (20 minutes, note wire positions, swap unit, reconnect)
- Hopper replacement (15 minutes, disconnect, remove two bolts, reverse with new unit)
- Reel strip replacement (30 minutes, requires patience but no special skills)

### Call a Tech

These repairs require specialized knowledge, equipment, or software:

- Logic board diagnosis and replacement (firmware matching, configuration)
- Game software issues (requires manufacturer-specific knowledge)
- Monitor/display replacement on older CRT machines (high voltage, dangerous)
- Touch screen calibration that will not hold (often indicates controller failure)
- Intermittent errors that do not produce clear error codes
- Any repair involving the progressive controller or networked components

### Call Us First Either Way

Before you order parts or call a local tech, call our tech support line. Many times what seems like a hardware failure is actually a configuration issue, a loose connection, or a simple reset. We troubleshoot over the phone at no charge for every machine we sell. At least half the time, we resolve the issue without the customer spending a dime on parts.

## What Your Warranty Covers

Our standard one-year warranty covers all mechanical and electronic failures under normal home use. This includes:

- Power supply failure
- Logic board failure
- Bill acceptor failure (not caused by foreign objects)
- Display failure
- Reel mechanism failure
- Hopper mechanism failure
- Button and switch failure

The warranty does not cover cosmetic damage, physical abuse, liquid damage, unauthorized modifications, or consumable items (lamps, batteries, cleaning supplies, printer paper). It also does not cover failures caused by improper electrical supply (surge damage from not using a surge protector, for example).

Warranty service works like this: Call us, describe the problem. We diagnose over the phone. If it is a parts issue, we ship the replacement part at no charge with instructions for installation. If you are not comfortable installing it yourself, we can arrange a local tech visit or accept the machine back for repair (customer pays shipping to us, we pay return shipping).

After the one-year warranty expires, our tech support remains free for life. We will still diagnose problems over the phone, help you identify parts, and walk you through repairs. You just pay for the parts themselves and any shipping.

## Preventive Maintenance: Keep Parts From Failing

The best parts strategy is preventing failures in the first place:

- Clean the bill acceptor every 1-3 months (cleaning cards cost $2 each)
- Replace the CMOS battery every 5 years proactively (do not wait for failure)
- Use a quality surge protector to prevent power supply damage from electrical spikes
- Keep the machine in a climate-controlled environment (humidity and temperature extremes kill electronics)
- Vacuum the ventilation openings every few months (dust buildup causes overheating)
- Exercise the machine regularly (components that sit unused degrade faster than ones that run periodically)

A slot machine that gets basic preventive maintenance will run for decades without major repair. We have customers still playing machines they bought from us 15 or 20 years ago with nothing more than a battery swap and occasional cleaning. These are robust commercial devices built to run 24 hours a day in a casino. In a home environment with proper care, they last essentially forever.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: The 25-Year Rule: Understanding Antique Slot Machine Laws
excerpt: Learn which states allow slot machines 25+ years old, what qualifies as antique, and which classic machines are now legal to own.
body:
If you have ever looked into buying a slot machine for your home, you have probably encountered a confusing patchwork of state laws. Some states ban them outright. Some allow any machine regardless of age. And roughly 28 states fall somewhere in the middle with what collectors call the 25-year rule. After 33 years selling slot machines from our shop in Kingman, Arizona, I have helped thousands of customers navigate these laws. Let me break it down clearly.

## What the 25-Year Rule Actually Says

The 25-year rule is not a single federal law. It is a common threshold adopted by many individual states that says a slot machine manufactured 25 or more years before the current date may be legally owned by a private citizen for personal use. The logic behind it mirrors antique firearm laws and classic vehicle exemptions. Once a machine reaches a certain age, legislators generally view it as a collectible or antique rather than an active gambling device.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

The key word here is manufactured. Not purchased. Not retired from a casino. Manufactured. The date that matters is when the machine rolled off the assembly line, not when it hit a casino floor or when you bought it.

As of 2026, any slot machine manufactured in 2001 or earlier qualifies under the 25-year threshold in states that use this rule. That opens up a massive catalog of classic machines that are now legally available to private buyers in most of the country.

## Which States Use the 25-Year Rule

Approximately 28 states allow private ownership of slot machines that are 25 or more years old. These include Alabama, Arizona, Arkansas, Colorado, Connecticut, Georgia, Idaho, Illinois, Iowa, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Maine, Michigan, Minnesota, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, New Hampshire, New Mexico, North Carolina, North Dakota, Ohio, Oklahoma, Oregon, Rhode Island, and West Virginia.

Some states set the bar at different ages. Texas uses 25 years. South Dakota allows machines manufactured before 1941. Wisconsin and Wyoming allow any slot machine regardless of age. Nevada, where the machines originate, has no restrictions on ownership.

A handful of states prohibit slot machine ownership entirely for private citizens. These include California (with some county exceptions), New York, South Carolina, Tennessee, and Hawaii. The laws change, so always verify current regulations in your state before purchasing.

## What Qualifies a Machine as Antique

For the purposes of these laws, the qualification is straightforward. The machine must have been manufactured 25 or more years ago. There is no requirement that it look old, be non-functional, or have any particular aesthetic. A fully operational IGT S2000 from 1999 in perfect working condition qualifies just as much as a rusty mechanical reel machine from the 1960s.

The machine does not need to be in original condition either. A refurbished machine that has been given new buttons, a fresh power supply, or updated bill acceptors still qualifies based on its original manufacture date. The chassis and motherboard determine the age, not the replacement parts.

Some buyers confuse antique status with collector value. They are not the same thing. A machine can be legally antique under the 25-year rule while having modest collector value, and vice versa. Legal status is purely about the manufacture date.

## Which Machines Now Qualify in 2026

With the cutoff now at 2001, an enormous range of beloved casino machines falls into legal territory for 25-year-rule states. Here are some of the most popular models that now qualify.

IGT S-Plus machines manufactured from the late 1980s through the mid-1990s. These are the workhorses of the used slot world. Reliable, easy to maintain, and available with hundreds of game chips.

IGT Game King multi-game machines from the late 1990s. These units offer video poker, video slots, and keno all in one cabinet. The early Game King models from 1997 through 2001 are now antique-eligible.

IGT S2000 machines from 1997 through 2001. The successor to the S-Plus with improved displays and expanded game options. A favorite among home collectors.

Bally GameMaker machines from the late 1990s. Multi-game video platforms that were extremely popular on casino floors.

WMS Bluebird machines from 2000 and 2001. The original Bluebird cabinet was revolutionary with its large LCD display. Early production units now qualify.

Aristocat MK series machines from the late 1990s. These Australian-designed machines brought a different flavor to American casinos and are now collectible.

Williams reel machines from the 1990s including popular titles like Monopoly, Jackpot Party, and Reel Em In.

Every year that passes, more machines cross the threshold. By 2027, machines from 2002 will qualify, opening up even more modern-feeling games to buyers in 25-year-rule states.

## Why This Matters for Buyers

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

The 25-year rule dramatically expands your options if you live in one of these states. Ten years ago, qualifying machines were mostly mechanical reels and early video units from the 1980s. Now the rule encompasses sophisticated video slot machines with bonus rounds, multi-game platforms, and machines that feel very similar to what you would find on a casino floor today.

This also affects pricing. As machines cross the 25-year threshold, demand increases because a larger pool of buyers can legally purchase them. Popular titles from the late 1990s have seen price increases as they become available to buyers in restrictive states. If you have your eye on a specific game from 2002 or 2003, buying it now and storing it until it qualifies might save you money compared to waiting for the rush.

## How to Verify a Machine's Manufacture Date

Every slot machine has identification that establishes its manufacture date. Here is how to verify.

The serial number plate is typically located on the inside of the main door or on the cabinet frame. IGT machines use a format that includes the manufacture date directly. Bally machines have production codes that correspond to manufacture quarters.

The game EPROM or software chips inside the machine contain version dates. While these can be updated, the original hardware platform date is what matters for legal purposes.

Manufacturer records exist for all machines produced for regulated casino markets. IGT, Bally, and other manufacturers kept detailed production records. As a dealer, I can provide manufacture documentation for any machine in our inventory.

Cabinet style and hardware generation also help identify era. An experienced dealer can tell you within a year or two when a machine was made just by looking at the cabinet design, button layout, and display type.

When you buy from us, every machine ships with documentation including its manufacture date and serial number verification. This gives you proof of antique status if it is ever questioned.

## Common Misunderstandings

The biggest misunderstanding is that the 25-year rule means you can operate the machine for gambling. It does not. In virtually every state, private ownership for personal use and entertainment is what these laws allow. You cannot charge people to play, offer real payouts, or operate the machine commercially without a gaming license.

Another common confusion is thinking the rule applies to when the machine was removed from service. A machine manufactured in 2005 but retired from a casino in 2010 is still only 21 years old in 2026. Manufacture date is the only date that matters.

Some people believe they need to disable the machine or make it non-functional to own it legally. In 25-year-rule states, this is not the case. The machine can be fully operational. It just cannot be used for commercial gambling.

Finally, some buyers think they need a special license or permit to own an antique slot machine. In most 25-year-rule states, no license is required for personal ownership. You buy it, you own it, you enjoy it at home. Some states require you to register the machine or notify local authorities, but this is rare and straightforward when it applies.

## The Bottom Line

The 25-year rule has opened up slot machine collecting to millions of Americans who previously thought it was illegal to own one. With machines from 2001 and earlier now qualifying, you have access to sophisticated video slots, multi-game platforms, and classic reel machines that deliver an authentic casino experience at home.

If you are unsure about your state's laws, give us a call. We have been shipping machines to all 50 states since 1992 and we know exactly what is legal where. We will never sell you a machine that you cannot legally own in your state. That is part of the service when you buy from a dealer who has been doing this for over three decades.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: How to Build a Home Casino: Slot Machines, Decor, and Setup Tips
excerpt: Plan your dream home casino from room layout to lighting, flooring, slot placement, and budget. A complete guide from setup to finishing touches.
body:
Building a home casino is one of the most rewarding projects you can take on as a homeowner. I have helped thousands of customers plan their setups over the past 33 years, and I can tell you that the difference between a slot machine sitting awkwardly in a corner and a proper home casino comes down to planning. Let me walk you through everything from room selection to final touches.

## Room Planning and Space Requirements

The minimum space you want for a dedicated home casino is 10 feet by 12 feet. That gives you enough room for two slot machines, a small bar area or high-top table, and comfortable movement space. Most customers work with spare bedrooms, finished basements, bonus rooms, or converted garages.

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

A basement is ideal for several reasons. Temperature stays consistent which is good for electronics. Sound is naturally dampened by being below grade. You typically have fewer windows to deal with for light control. And basements often have the structural floor support to handle heavy machines without any concern.

A standard upright slot machine weighs between 250 and 400 pounds. A two-car garage conversion can comfortably hold four to six machines plus entertainment space. A spare bedroom typically fits two machines and a small seating area.

Measure your doorways before ordering. Standard interior doors are 30 to 32 inches wide. Most slot machine cabinets are 24 to 28 inches wide, so they fit through standard doors. Taller upright cabinets may need to be tilted, and you should measure the height of any stairways if the room is on a different level.

Plan your electrical before anything else. Each slot machine draws between 2 and 4 amps on a standard 120-volt circuit. A typical 15-amp household circuit can safely run three machines plus ambient lighting. If you plan to run more than three machines, lighting, a TV, and a sound system, have an electrician run a dedicated 20-amp circuit. Never use extension cords for slot machines. Use surge protectors rated for at least 1500 watts.

## Lighting Design

Lighting transforms a room with slot machines into a casino. The goal is to eliminate overhead fluorescent flatness and create atmospheric depth.

Start with your ambient base layer. LED color wash strips along the ceiling perimeter or under bar counters create that unmistakable casino glow. Purple, blue, and red are the classic casino palette. RGB LED strips are inexpensive at around $30 to $50 for enough to ring a room, and they come with remote controls to change colors and intensity.

Add accent lighting with neon signs or LED neon flex. Classic phrases like Lucky Seven, Jackpot, or a simple bar sign add character. Vintage brewery signs or Las Vegas replica signs work perfectly. Budget $50 to $200 per sign depending on size and whether you go vintage or reproduction.

Keep overhead lighting dimmable. Install a dimmer switch on whatever ceiling fixture exists. You want the option to bring up full light for cleaning or maintenance but keep it low during play. Recessed can lights on a dimmer are ideal if you are doing any ceiling work.

The slot machines themselves provide significant light. Their attract modes cycle through colorful displays, and the button panels glow. In a properly darkened room, three slot machines provide enough ambient light to navigate comfortably.

Avoid direct sunlight on machine screens. UV exposure over time damages LCD displays, and glare makes screens hard to read. If your room has windows, use blackout curtains or cellular shades.

## Flooring Considerations

Casinos use low-pile commercial carpet for good reasons. It absorbs sound, hides wear patterns, it is comfortable to stand on, and it does not show every coin or token that hits the ground. For your home casino, low-pile carpet or carpet tiles are the best choice.

Carpet tiles are particularly smart for home casinos. They cost $2 to $5 per square foot, come in casino-appropriate dark colors and patterns, and individual tiles can be replaced if one gets stained or worn. A 10x12 room costs roughly $240 to $600 to floor with quality carpet tiles.

Rubber matting or interlocking foam tiles work well for garage conversions. They dampen vibration, insulate against cold concrete, and are extremely durable. Anti-fatigue mats in front of each machine add comfort for longer play sessions.

Hardwood and laminate floors work but create more noise. Every coin drop, every chair scoot, and the general hum of machines reflects off hard surfaces. If you already have hardwood, add area rugs in front of machines and in seating areas.

Avoid placing machines directly on carpet pad or thick plush carpet. The machines are heavy enough to compress thick padding unevenly over time, potentially causing the machine to tilt slightly. Low-pile or commercial grade avoids this issue.

## Slot Machine Placement

Where you position your machines matters for both aesthetics and longevity.

Keep machines at least 6 inches from any wall. They need air circulation around the back panel where the power supply and main board generate heat. Pushing a machine flush against a wall restricts airflow and shortens the life of electronic components.

Never place a machine near a source of moisture. This means away from exterior doors where rain might blow in, away from sump pump areas in basements, and away from any plumbing. Humidity is the enemy of circuit boards. If your basement has humidity issues, run a dehumidifier and aim for 40 to 50 percent relative humidity.

Avoid heat sources. Do not place machines next to radiators, heating vents, or in direct sunlight. The electronics are designed to operate in climate-controlled casino environments between 65 and 75 degrees Fahrenheit.

Angle your machines slightly rather than lining them up flat against a wall. A 10 to 15 degree angle creates a more inviting arrangement and lets multiple people play without bumping elbows. This mirrors how casinos arrange banks of machines.

Leave enough space in front of each machine for comfortable play. Three feet of clearance allows for a stool or chair and room to get in and out without squeezing.

## Complementary Items and Decor

A home casino is more than slot machines. The surrounding elements complete the experience.

A poker table is the most popular addition. Folding poker table tops that sit on any dining table cost $100 to $300. Dedicated poker tables with padded rails run $300 to $1,500. If you have the space, an 8-person table creates a social hub that complements the solo slot experience.

A bar area elevates the room significantly. This does not need to be elaborate. A 4-foot countertop with a mini fridge underneath, some shelving for bottles, and two or three bar stools creates a proper casino bar feel. Budget $300 to $800 for a simple home bar setup. Add a speed rail, a bar mat, and some rocks glasses and you are in business.

Sports memorabilia and casino collectibles add personality. Framed vintage casino chips, old Las Vegas postcards, boxing event posters, or classic rat pack photos all work. Check estate sales and antique shops for authentic vintage casino signage.

A wall-mounted TV lets you run sports or classic casino movies during gatherings. A 55-inch TV mounted above a bar area or between machines creates that sportsbook atmosphere.

Card table accessories round out the gaming options. A craps felt layout for a tabletop, a roulette wheel, or even a simple blackjack mat gives guests options beyond the slot machines.

Consider a vintage jukebox or at minimum a quality Bluetooth speaker system. Music matters in creating atmosphere. A used jukebox runs $500 to $2,000 and becomes a focal point.

## Sound Considerations

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

Slot machines are not quiet. The attract mode on a typical machine cycles through music and sound effects at moderate volume. When someone is actively playing, win celebrations can hit 80 decibels or more. If your casino room shares walls with bedrooms or neighbors, address sound.

Most machines have internal volume controls. IGT machines have a volume setting in the operator menu accessible with the jackpot reset key. You can turn machines down to 30 to 40 percent volume and still enjoy the sound effects without shaking the walls.

Soft surfaces absorb sound. Carpet, upholstered furniture, acoustic panels, and curtains all reduce sound transmission. If you are converting a room with hard parallel walls, even two or three acoustic panels at $30 each can dramatically reduce echo.

For garage conversions, insulate shared walls with the house. Standard fiberglass batt insulation in the wall cavity between garage and living space makes a meaningful difference.

Timing matters too. Most home casino owners learn quickly to power down machines or mute them during quiet hours. Modern machines remember their volume settings so you only set it once.

## Climate Control

Slot machines generate heat. A single machine adds roughly the same heat as a 200-watt space heater running continuously. Three machines in an enclosed room will noticeably raise the temperature.

Ensure your room has adequate HVAC coverage. A room with three machines may need a slightly oversized vent or a supplemental mini-split unit, particularly in summer months. Basements tend to stay cool naturally, making them ideal.

In garages, a ductless mini-split air conditioning system is the gold standard for conversions. They run $800 to $2,000 installed and keep the space comfortable year-round while also controlling humidity.

Never operate machines in an unheated space during winter if temperatures drop below 50 degrees. LCD displays can be damaged by extreme cold, and thermal cycling causes solder joint stress on circuit boards.

## Budget Breakdown

Here is what a home casino project typically costs at three levels.

### Budget Build: $3,000 to $5,000

- 1-2 refurbished slot machines: $1,600 to $3,000
- LED strip lighting: $50
- Carpet tiles for the room: $300
- Bar stools and small table: $200
- Neon sign or two: $100
- Surge protectors and electrical: $100
- Decor and accessories: $200

### Mid-Range Build: $6,000 to $10,000

- 2-3 refurbished slot machines: $3,000 to $5,500
- Full lighting design with color wash and accents: $300
- Professional flooring: $600
- Home bar setup with mini fridge: $800
- Poker table: $400
- Wall-mounted TV: $400
- Acoustic treatment: $200
- Decor, signage, memorabilia: $500
- Dedicated electrical circuit: $300

### Premium Build: $12,000 to $15,000

- 4-6 refurbished slot machines: $5,000 to $9,000
- Full room renovation with proper flooring and paint: $1,500
- Custom bar with refrigeration: $1,500
- Quality poker table and accessories: $800
- Professional lighting installation: $500
- Sound system: $400
- Large screen TV: $600
- Mini-split HVAC: $1,500
- Decor package: $800

## Planning Your Order

When you are ready to start, here is the sequence I recommend.

First, plan your electrical and verify your circuit capacity. Second, address flooring and paint while the room is empty. Third, install lighting fixtures and any ceiling work. Fourth, order your machines. We can hold machines for up to 30 days while you prepare your space. Fifth, arrange delivery and have your placement spots clear with surge protectors ready. Sixth, add furniture, decor, and finishing touches.

We ship machines nationwide on pallets via freight carrier. Delivery to your door is included in our pricing. You will need one or two helpers to move a machine from the delivery point to your casino room, or we can arrange white-glove delivery in most areas for an additional fee.

## The Payoff

Customers tell me all the time that their home casino becomes the most-used room in the house. It becomes where friends gather for fight nights, where the family plays poker on holidays, where Dad disappears for 20 minutes of decompression after work. A well-planned home casino is not just a collection of machines in a room. It is a destination within your home that brings people together.

Give us a call and tell us about your space. After 33 years and thousands of home casino setups, we can help you plan the perfect layout and choose the right machines for your room and budget.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Slot Machine Error Codes: What They Mean and How to Fix Them
excerpt: Decode common error codes on IGT, Bally, and Aristocrat slot machines. Learn which fixes you can handle yourself and when to call for help.
body:
Every slot machine owner will eventually see an error code on their display. The machine locks up, a message appears, and suddenly your evening entertainment is staring back at you with a cryptic code. After 33 years in this business and tens of thousands of tech support calls, I can tell you that about 80 percent of home-use error codes have simple fixes you can handle yourself. Let me walk you through the most common codes by manufacturer.

## Understanding Error Codes Generally

Slot machines are computers with extensive self-diagnostic systems. Every time you power on a machine, it runs through a series of checks on its memory, mechanical components, security switches, and peripherals. When something does not pass muster, the machine generates an error code and locks itself to prevent operation until the issue is resolved.

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

This is actually a feature, not a flaw. In a casino environment, these codes alert technicians to issues before they become expensive problems. In your home, they serve the same purpose but can feel frustrating when you just want to play.

Most errors fall into a few categories: door and security switch errors, hopper and payout errors, bill acceptor problems, memory errors, and communication faults. Knowing which category your error falls into tells you immediately whether it is a quick fix or something requiring technical support.

## IGT Error Codes

IGT machines are the most common in home collections, so I will start here. Whether you have an S-Plus, S2000, Game King, or newer IGT cabinet, these codes are consistent across platforms.

### Door Open or Door Error

This is the single most common call we get. The machine displays MAIN DOOR OPEN, BELLY DOOR OPEN, or a door switch error even though all doors appear closed.

What it means: Every door on an IGT machine has a magnetic reed switch or microswitch that tells the computer whether the door is open or closed. If the switch is misaligned, dirty, or the magnet has shifted, the machine thinks a door is open.

DIY fix: Open the main door and look along the door frame edge. You will see small switches mounted to the cabinet frame with corresponding magnets on the door itself. Make sure the magnet aligns within a quarter inch of the switch when the door closes. If a switch has physically shifted, loosen its mounting screw, realign it, and retighten. Clean the switch contact area with compressed air. Close the door firmly and turn the jackpot reset key to clear the error.

If the switch itself has failed, a replacement reed switch costs about $8 and takes 10 minutes to swap with a screwdriver.

### RAM Cleared or RAM Reset Required

You see CMOS RAM CLEARED or RAM ERROR on the display after a power event.

What it means: The machine's battery-backed memory lost its contents. This happens when the internal lithium battery dies, when there was a power surge, or when the machine was unplugged for an extended period without a healthy battery.

DIY fix: For a simple RAM clear after a power outage, open the main door, locate the jackpot reset key switch, turn it to the reset position, then turn it back. Some IGT models require you to press and hold the service button while resetting. The machine will re-initialize its RAM with default settings.

If this happens repeatedly, the CMOS battery needs replacement. It is a standard CR2032 lithium coin cell on most IGT boards. Pop the old one out, put the new one in observing correct polarity, and the machine will hold its memory through future power outages. Battery costs about $3 at any drugstore.

### Hopper Empty or Hopper Error

Displays HOPPER EMPTY, HOPPER JAM, or HOPPER RUNAWAY.

What it means: The coin hopper at the bottom of the machine either has no coins to pay out, has coins jammed in the payout mechanism, or paid out more coins than expected.

DIY fix for hopper empty: If you are running your machine on coin play, simply add coins to the hopper. The hopper holds between 1,000 and 2,500 coins depending on the model. Fill it three-quarters full for best operation. Turn the jackpot reset key to clear the error.

DIY fix for hopper jam: Open the belly door, pull the hopper out (it sits in a cradle and lifts straight up). Look in the payout chute for any coins turned sideways or foreign objects. Coins that are slightly bent or damaged frequently cause jams. Remove the obstruction, replace the hopper, and reset.

Note: If you run your machine on **free play mode**, hopper errors should never occur because the machine is not attempting to pay coins. If you see hopper errors in free play mode, there may be a configuration issue in the machine's setup menu.

### Bill Acceptor Errors

Displays BILL ACCEPTOR ERROR, STACKER FULL, STACKER REMOVED, or BILL JAM.

What it means: The bill validator unit is reporting a problem. These are common because bill acceptors have moving parts and optical sensors that can get dirty.

DIY fix for stacker full: The stacker is the removable cassette that holds accepted bills. Pull it out, empty the bills, replace it. Reset the error.

DIY fix for bill jams: Open the bill acceptor head (it usually hinges open with a thumb latch). Look for paper stuck in the transport path. Gently remove any jammed bill being careful not to tear it and leave fragments inside. Use a bill acceptor cleaning card ($5 for a pack of 10) to clean the optical sensors. Run the cleaning card through three times. This solves 90 percent of bill acceptor issues.

DIY fix for stacker removed: Make sure the stacker cassette is fully seated. It should click into place. If the machine still shows stacker removed, the microswitch that detects the stacker presence may need cleaning or adjustment.

### Coin In Meter Error

Displays COIN IN TILT or OPTIC ERROR.

What it means: The coin comparitor or optic sensor that reads coins entering the machine is dirty or malfunctioning.

DIY fix: Locate the coin comparitor in the coin head assembly at the top of the coin chute. Use compressed air to blow out dust. Wipe the optical sensors with a dry lint-free cloth. Do not use liquid cleaners on optical sensors unless they are specifically designed for that purpose. Reset and test with a clean coin.

## Bally Error Codes

Bally machines use a slightly different error reporting system. Many Bally errors display as two-digit codes on a seven-segment LED display inside the cabinet rather than on the main screen.

### CMOS Error (Bally)

The cabinet LED shows alternating characters or displays CMOS on the VFD.

What it means: Same as IGT RAM errors. The battery-backed memory has been compromised.

DIY fix: Locate the CMOS battery on the main processor board. On most Bally machines from the 1990s and 2000s, this is a 3.6V lithium cell, sometimes soldered in place rather than socketed. If it is socketed, replace it with the correct voltage replacement. If it is soldered, this is a job for someone comfortable with a soldering iron. After battery replacement, you will need to reinitialize the machine using the RAM clear procedure in the operator manual.

Call for help if: The battery is soldered and you are not comfortable with soldering on circuit boards. This is a common repair we handle via our tech support line with step-by-step guidance.

### Hopper Errors (Bally)

Bally hopper codes typically show as ERROR 30 through ERROR 39 depending on the specific issue.

Error 30 means hopper empty. Error 31 means hopper jam. Error 32 means hopper runaway, meaning the hopper paid out more coins than it should have.

DIY fix: Same general approach as IGT. Fill the hopper for empty errors, clear jams by removing the hopper and inspecting the payout path, and for runaway errors check that the hopper optic sensor is clean and properly detecting coins as they exit.

### Tilt Codes (Bally)

Bally machines have numerous tilt codes that relate to the physical orientation and door security of the machine.

Error 10 through Error 19 are typically door and security switch tilts. Error 10 is usually the main door. Error 11 is the drop door. Error 12 is the top box.

DIY fix: Same approach as IGT door switches. Verify switch alignment, clean contacts, ensure magnets are properly positioned. Reset by turning the jackpot reset key or pressing the attendant reset button depending on your model.

### Logic Door Error (Bally)

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

This is a specific Bally code that indicates the logic compartment (where the CPU board lives) was opened.

DIY fix: Open and close the logic door firmly, ensuring the switch engages. Reset with the attendant key. If you did not intentionally open the logic area, check that vibration has not caused the door to work loose from its latch.

## Aristocrat Error Codes

Aristocat machines use alpha-numeric codes that are fairly descriptive. They tend to display the error clearly on the main screen.

### RAM Error or Memory Fault (Aristocrat)

Displays MEMORY FAULT or NVRAM ERROR.

What it means: Non-volatile memory has been corrupted, usually from power loss or battery failure.

DIY fix: Aristocrat machines have a RAM clear procedure that typically involves opening the logic door, pressing a specific button combination, and cycling power. The exact sequence varies by model. On MK series machines, hold the service button while powering on to enter diagnostic mode, then select RAM clear from the menu. You will need the attendant and jackpot keys.

Note: RAM clear on an Aristocrat resets all game settings to factory defaults. You may need to reconfigure denomination, payout percentage, and other operator settings afterward.

### Door Errors (Aristocrat)

Aristocat displays MAIN DOOR OPEN, LOGIC DOOR OPEN, or CASH DOOR OPEN clearly on screen.

DIY fix: Same principles as other manufacturers. Check switch alignment and magnet placement. Aristocrat uses a combination of reed switches and microswitches depending on the model and door location. The main door switch is typically at the bottom left of the cabinet frame.

### Printer Errors (Aristocrat)

Displays PRINTER ERROR, PAPER OUT, or PRINTER DISCONNECTED.

What it means: If your machine has a thermal ticket printer (TITO system), it is reporting a paper or communication fault.

DIY fix: If you do not use the printer for anything (most home users do not), you can often disable it in the operator setup menu, which eliminates these errors entirely. If you want the printer functional, check that the paper roll is loaded correctly with the thermal (shiny) side facing the print head. Check the ribbon cable connection between the printer and the main board.

### Communication Errors (Aristocrat)

Displays COMM FAULT or PROTOCOL ERROR.

What it means: The machine is looking for a connection to a casino network or progressive system that does not exist in your home.

DIY fix: Enter the operator setup menu and disable any network communication or SAS protocol settings. The machine does not need network connectivity for home use. Once disabled, these errors will stop appearing. On some models, you may need to set the machine to standalone mode.

## Universal Troubleshooting Steps

Regardless of manufacturer, here is a universal approach when you see any error code.

First, write down the exact error message or code. This helps if you need to call tech support.

Second, try the simple reset. Turn the jackpot reset key or press the attendant reset button. Many errors are transient and clear with a simple reset, especially after power events.

Third, power cycle the machine. Turn it off, wait 30 seconds, turn it back on. The reboot process runs all diagnostics fresh and many issues resolve on their own.

Fourth, check all doors and connections. Open each door and close it firmly. Check that all cable connections inside are secure. Vibration from shipping or even from the machine's own speakers can work connectors loose over time.

Fifth, check your environment. Is the room too humid? Is there a new source of electrical interference nearby? Did you recently plug something else into the same circuit?

## When to Call for Help

Some issues are beyond DIY regardless of your comfort level with electronics.

Main board failures where the machine shows no display at all or displays garbage characters need professional diagnosis. Power supply failures where the machine will not turn on but has confirmed good wall power should be handled by someone with multimeter experience. EPROM or software corruption where games behave erratically even after RAM clear may require chip replacement. Mechanical reel motor failures on physical reel machines involve precision alignment that is difficult without experience.

## Our Free Lifetime Tech Support

Every machine we sell comes with free lifetime technical support. That means you can call us any time you see an error code and we will walk you through the fix. We have seen every code on every machine made in the last 40 years. Most calls take five to ten minutes to resolve.

We also stock replacement parts for every machine we sell. Door switches, batteries, bill acceptor parts, hopper motors, power supplies, and boards are available and ship quickly. About 95 percent of home-use errors can be resolved with a phone call and at most a $10 to $30 part.

Keep your operator keys in a safe place, keep the owner's manual we provide with every machine, and do not hesitate to call when something comes up. That is what we are here for.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Used Slot Machines vs. New: Is Buying Refurbished Worth It?
excerpt: Compare refurbished slot machines at $800-$3,500 versus new at $15,000-$25,000. Learn why casinos retire working machines and what you actually sacrifice buying used.
body:
One of the most common questions I get from first-time buyers is whether a used or refurbished slot machine is worth it compared to buying new. After 33 years in this business, my answer is always the same: for home use, refurbished is not just worth it, it is the only option that makes financial sense. Let me show you why with real numbers and real facts.

## The Price Gap Is Enormous

A brand new slot machine from IGT, Aristocrat, or any major manufacturer costs between $15,000 and $25,000. That is not a typo. The base cabinets start around $15,000, and popular titles with licensed themes can push $25,000 or more.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

A professionally refurbished version of the same machine, often the exact same model, costs between $800 and $3,500 from our shop. The most popular machines for home use fall in the $1,200 to $2,500 range.

That is an 85 to 95 percent savings for a machine that does the exact same thing. It plays the same games, makes the same sounds, has the same bonus features, and delivers the same experience. The electrons do not know whether the machine is one year old or fifteen years old.

## Why Casinos Replace Perfectly Good Machines

Here is what most people do not understand about the used slot machine market. Casinos do not retire machines because they are broken. They retire them because newer games earn more revenue per square foot of floor space.

A casino floor is real estate. Every square foot needs to generate maximum revenue. When a new game comes out that earns $300 per day compared to an older game earning $200 per day, the older game gets pulled even if it is in perfect working condition. The decision is purely economic.

Most machines are retired after 3 to 7 years on a casino floor. They have been maintained by full-time technicians their entire lives. Casinos employ teams of slot techs who perform preventive maintenance on strict schedules. These machines get better care during their casino life than most cars get from their owners.

When a casino refreshes its floor, hundreds of perfectly functional machines become available to dealers like us. We buy them in bulk, refurbish them, and pass the savings on to home buyers.

## What Professional Refurbishment Includes

Not all refurbished machines are equal. Here is what we do to every machine that comes through our shop before it goes to a customer.

We completely disassemble the cabinet interior. Every component comes out for individual inspection and testing.

The power supply is tested under load. If it does not meet factory voltage specifications on all rails, it gets replaced. Power supplies are the number one failure point on older machines, so we do not take chances here.

All mechanical components are inspected. Hopper motors, coin acceptors, bill validators, reel motors, door hinges, and locks. Anything worn beyond acceptable tolerance gets replaced with new parts.

The main CPU board is tested and the CMOS battery is replaced regardless of remaining life. This ensures years of reliable operation without memory errors.

All lighting is checked and replaced as needed. Bulbs, fluorescent tubes, and LED arrays are verified. Many machines get upgraded from incandescent bulbs to LEDs during refurbishment, which run cooler and last essentially forever.

The bill acceptor gets rebuilt with new belts and cleaned optics, or replaced entirely with a current-generation unit that accepts new bill designs.

The cabinet exterior is cleaned, touched up, and any damaged trim pieces are replaced. The machine should look presentable in your home.

Finally, the machine runs on our test bench for a minimum of 24 hours with automated play cycles to verify no intermittent issues exist.

## What You Sacrifice Buying Used

Let me be honest about what you give up choosing refurbished over new for home use.

You will not have the absolute latest game titles. The newest releases from major manufacturers go exclusively to licensed casinos. You will not find a machine that hit casino floors six months ago on the used market yet. Games typically become available used two to five years after their casino debut.

The cabinet style may look slightly dated. A machine from 2010 has a different aesthetic than one from 2023. The screens may be slightly smaller, the cabinet lines less sleek. For most home users, this is irrelevant because you are after the gaming experience, not the industrial design trophy.

That is genuinely it. The game play is identical. The math is identical. The features work identically. A refurbished machine does not hit differently or pay differently or play differently than it did on day one in the casino.

## What You Do NOT Sacrifice

Game quality. The software running on a refurbished machine is the same software that ran in the casino. The random number generator, the bonus features, the paytables, the animations, the sounds. All identical.

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

Reliability. A properly refurbished machine with new critical components is arguably more reliable than a new machine in its first few months. The burn-in period where infant failures occur happened years ago in the casino. What remains are the components that survived and proved their reliability.

Fun factor. This is the one that matters most. When you sit down at a refurbished Game King and hit four aces on double bonus poker, the excitement is not diminished by the age of the cabinet. The game plays exactly as designed.

## Warranty Comparison

New machines sold to casinos come with a one-year manufacturer warranty. Parts and labor for that first year are covered by IGT, Aristocrat, or whoever made the machine.

Our refurbished machines come with a one-year warranty. We cover parts for any failure that is not caused by physical damage or modification. We also provide free lifetime technical support by phone, which no manufacturer offers to casino operators.

So the warranty coverage is equivalent in duration, and our ongoing tech support actually exceeds what a casino gets from the manufacturer. When your machine has an issue in year three or year five, you can still call us and we will help you fix it at no charge for the support time.

## The Math for Home Use

Let me frame this differently. A new machine at $18,000 versus a refurbished machine at $2,000 leaves you $16,000 ahead. With that savings you could buy seven additional refurbished machines and have a full casino room. Or you could buy one machine, build out an entire home casino with bar, lighting, poker table, and decor, and still have $10,000 in your pocket.

The new machine depreciates catastrophically the moment it enters a private home. It cannot be resold back to a casino. Its resale value on the private market drops to used pricing almost immediately because private buyers will not pay casino pricing.

A refurbished machine holds its value well. A machine purchased from us for $2,000 can typically be resold for $1,200 to $1,500 years later because the used market is liquid and demand is consistent. Your percentage loss on resale is actually lower with refurbished than new.

## Who Should Buy New

New machines make sense for licensed casino operations that need the latest titles to attract players and generate maximum revenue. They make sense for commercial route operators who place machines in bars and restaurants and need the newest games to compete. They do not make sense for home use under any realistic scenario.

If money is truly no object and you want the absolute newest title that just hit Vegas, you will need to go through a licensed distributor and you will pay full price. But understand that you are paying a $15,000 premium for a game that is six months newer than what you could get refurbished.

## Resale Value

Slot machines hold value better than most entertainment purchases. A home theater system from 2015 is worth almost nothing today. A slot machine from 2015 is worth 60 to 75 percent of its refurbished purchase price.
