The multi-game format also reduces the chance that any guest will get bored with a single title during their stay.

**Price range:** $1,495 to $2,195.

### Best for Upscale Properties: Bally Alpha Series

![Bally slot machine from Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879c0e257dd60bea48569a9.jpeg)

For luxury rentals where presentation matters as much as function, the **Bally Alpha** series offers stunning visual impact. Large screens, cinematic bonus rounds, and a sleek cabinet design photograph beautifully for listings. These machines make a statement.

**Price range:** $2,195 to $3,295.

## Setting Up Free Play Mode

This is critical for rental properties. Your machine must be set to **free play mode**. You cannot have guests inserting money or receiving payouts in a rental property. Free play eliminates legal concerns and removes the hassle of coin handling.

In free play mode, guests press a button to add credits to the machine. They play for entertainment only. There is no money in and no money out. This is the standard configuration we recommend for all rental properties, and it is how we set up every machine we sell for this purpose.

Setting up free play is straightforward on most machines. On the IGT S2000, it involves accessing the setup menu through the main door and toggling the free play option. We walk every customer through this during our [setup process](/blog/how-to-set-up-your-slot-machine-at-home), and our free lifetime tech support means you can call us anytime if you need to reconfigure.

Some hosts get creative with free play. They leave a laminated card next to the machine explaining how to add credits and play. Others include a brief mention in their welcome book. The simpler you make it, the more guests will use it.

## Legal Considerations

Legality is the first thing you need to verify. Slot machine ownership laws vary by state, and you need to confirm that your rental property is in a state that allows private ownership. Our [state-by-state legal guide](/blog/is-it-legal-to-own-a-slot-machine-state-by-state-guide) covers this in detail.

The key points for rental property owners are as follows.

**No gambling can occur.** The machine must be in free play mode. Guests cannot wager real money, and you cannot offer real payouts. This is true in every state regardless of ownership laws.

**Personal use vs. commercial operation.** A slot machine in a rental property game room that guests play for free as part of their stay is generally treated as personal property used for entertainment. You are not operating a casino. You are not charging admission to play the machine. It is an amenity, like a hot tub or pool table.

**Verify your specific state.** Thirteen states allow unrestricted ownership. Twenty-eight states allow machines over 25 years old. Nine states prohibit ownership entirely. If your rental is in a prohibited state, you cannot put a machine in it. Period.

**Consult local ordinances.** Some municipalities have their own restrictions beyond state law. A quick call to your local code enforcement office can clarify any local rules.

We recommend keeping your purchase documentation and machine age certification with your property records. In the unlikely event anyone ever questions the machine, you have proof of legal compliance on hand.

## Durability Rankings by Brand

Rental machines take more abuse than home machines. Different guests with different experience levels play them multiple times per week. Here is how the major brands stack up for rental durability based on our experience and customer feedback.

### Tier 1: Built to Survive Anything

- **IGT S2000** — The cockroach of slot machines. These things simply do not die. The mechanical reel mechanism is overengineered and the cabinet is built like a tank. Best choice for high-turnover rentals.
- **IGT S-Plus** — The predecessor to the S2000. Slightly less refined but equally durable. A good budget option for rentals.

### Tier 2: Excellent Durability

- **Bally Alpha Series** — Well-built cabinets with quality components. The video displays may need attention after several years of heavy use, but the core platform is very solid.
- **Aristocrat Mark VI** — Australian engineering at its finest. Built tough with quality materials throughout.

### Tier 3: Good Durability with Some Caveats

- **WMS Bluebird** — Excellent machines but the more complex bonus hardware (spinning wheels, moving parts) can develop issues with heavy use. The core cabinet is solid but bonus features need periodic attention.
- **Konami** — Good overall build quality. LCD screens are generally reliable. Worth considering for rentals.

### Tier 4: Better for Light-Use Environments

- **Ainsworth** — Quality machines but less proven in high-use rental environments. We have less long-term data on these in rental settings.
- **Aruze** — Unique designs but parts can be harder to source. Better suited for personal use where you can call us for [tech support](/warranty) and take your time with repairs.

## Insurance Considerations

Your rental property insurance should cover the slot machine as part of your property contents. Here is what to do.

**Notify your insurance carrier.** Add the machine to your property contents schedule with its purchase price. Most standard rental property policies cover contents without specific exclusions for gaming machines used for entertainment.

**Document the purchase.** Keep your receipt, photos of the machine, serial numbers, and our warranty documentation. This is standard for any valuable property asset.

**Liability concerns.** The most common question hosts ask is whether they have liability exposure from the machine. In free play mode with no money changing hands, the liability profile is similar to any other game room equipment. A guest could theoretically claim injury from the machine (tripping over it, for example), but this falls under your general premises liability — the same coverage that protects you if someone trips over your coffee table.

**Consider an umbrella policy.** If you do not already have an umbrella liability policy for your rental properties, get one. This is general advice for any rental host and not specific to slot machines. An umbrella policy provides an additional layer of protection for $200 to $400 per year.

## Maintenance for Rental Properties

Rental machines need a slightly more rigorous maintenance schedule than home machines because of higher use frequency. Here is what we recommend.

### Between Guest Stays (or Weekly)

- Wipe down the cabinet exterior, buttons, and handle with a disinfecting wipe
- Check that the machine powers on and plays correctly
- Verify free play credits are working
- Quick visual check for any obvious issues

### Monthly

- Open the main door and blow out dust with compressed air
- Check all light bulbs and replace any that are out
- Clean the glass panels
- Verify the bill acceptor is disabled (it should be in free play mode, but double-check)

### Quarterly

- Full cleaning of interior components
- Check all cable connections
- Test all buttons for responsiveness
- Clean or replace the air filter if your machine has one

### Annually

- Replace the backup battery preemptively
- Inspect the coin mechanism even if it is not in use
- Check the power supply under load
- Full cosmetic detail of the cabinet

If you use a property management company, include the weekly and monthly checks in their service checklist. The quarterly and annual items can be done during your own property visits or by scheduling a maintenance window.

## Guest Communication Tips

How you present the slot machine to guests matters for both enjoyment and machine care.

### In Your Listing

Feature the machine prominently in your listing photos. A well-lit photo of the game room with the slot machine as the centerpiece is one of the most effective images you can include. Mention it in your amenities list and description. Something simple like "Real casino slot machine in the game room — play for free all night long" is enough to generate interest.

### In Your Welcome Book

Include a short section on the slot machine. Cover how to turn it on (if you turn it off between stays), how to add free play credits, and basic gameplay. Keep it to one page. Mention that it is a real casino machine and note the [warranty and support](/warranty) backing. Guests appreciate knowing it is a quality piece of equipment, not a toy.

### House Rules

Add a simple machine rule to your house rules. Something like: "The slot machine is for entertainment use only. Please do not insert coins or attempt to open the cabinet. Press the green button to add credits and enjoy." This sets clear expectations and prevents guests from trying to force quarters into a machine configured for free play.

## Maximizing the Guest Experience

A few small touches turn a slot machine from an amenity into a memorable experience.

**Lighting matters.** Install some ambient lighting around the machine — LED strip lights behind or beneath the cabinet create an authentic casino atmosphere. The machine's own lights do most of the work, but a darkened game room with a glowing slot machine is irresistible.

**Sound management.** Set the volume to a moderate level. Too quiet and it loses its atmosphere. Too loud and it disturbs guests in other rooms. Most machines have adjustable volume — check our [noise management guide](/blog/how-to-manage-slot-machine-noise-volume-control-muting-soundproofing) for detailed instructions.

**Pair it with other game room amenities.** A slot machine alongside a pool table, dartboard, or card table creates a complete entertainment space. The combination is greater than the sum of its parts.

## The Bottom Line

A slot machine in a vacation rental is one of the highest-ROI amenities you can install. The upfront cost is modest relative to property improvements like hot tubs or theater rooms. The maintenance is minimal. The guest response is overwhelmingly positive. And the revenue impact — in higher nightly rates, better occupancy, and standout reviews — pays for the machine many times over.

[Browse our inventory](/shop) for rental-ready machines, or [contact us](/contact) to discuss which machine makes the most sense for your property. We have helped dozens of rental hosts choose the right machine and we will walk you through every step from purchase to guest-ready setup.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: How to Manage Slot Machine Noise: Volume Control, Muting, and Soundproofing
excerpt: Practical solutions for controlling slot machine noise at home — from built-in volume controls and speaker mods to room-level soundproofing techniques.
body:
This is one of the most common concerns we hear from buyers, especially those whose significant other is not entirely sold on the idea of a casino machine in the house. Slot machines are designed to be heard across a noisy casino floor. That level of audio output is impressive in a casino and excessive in a living room.

The good news is that noise management is a solved problem. Every machine we sell has some form of volume control, and there are additional steps you can take to tame the sound to whatever level works for your household. After 33 years of helping customers set up machines in homes, apartments, condos, and yes, even a few bedrooms, here is everything you need to know about managing slot machine noise.

## How Loud Is a Slot Machine?

Before we talk about solutions, let us establish what we are dealing with. **Slot machine noise** comes from several sources, each at different volume levels.

### Sound Sources and Decibel Levels

**Speaker audio (music, sound effects, win celebrations):** 70 to 85 decibels at full volume. This is the primary noise source and the one you have the most control over. For reference, 70 dB is comparable to a vacuum cleaner, and 85 dB is similar to a food blender. At medium volume, most machines run around 60 to 70 dB — normal conversation level.

**Mechanical reel spin (reel machines only):** 45 to 55 decibels. The whir of the stepper motors and the click of reels stopping into position. This is a relatively quiet, mechanical sound that most people find pleasant rather than annoying. You cannot eliminate it without disabling the reels, which defeats the purpose.

**Coin hopper payout (coin-operated machines only):** 75 to 85 decibels during payout. This is the loudest component — metal coins dropping into a metal tray. A large payout of 100 coins sounds like a brief burst from a coffee grinder. This noise is intermittent and only occurs during wins.

**Button presses:** 35 to 45 decibels. A light mechanical click. Not a concern.

**Cooling fans:** 30 to 40 decibels. Barely audible. Some machines have small internal fans; others rely on passive ventilation.

The total noise picture depends on your configuration. A reel machine in **free play mode** with volume at medium produces about 55 to 65 dB during play — comparable to a television at moderate volume. That same machine in coin mode at full volume during a big payout can hit 85 dB momentarily.

## Built-In Volume Controls by Manufacturer

Every major manufacturer includes some form of volume adjustment. Here is how to access it on the platforms we sell most often.

### IGT S2000 and S-Plus

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

The IGT S2000 has a volume control accessible through the operator menu. Open the main door with your key, and you will find a small potentiometer (dial) on the speaker amplifier board, usually located on the inside of the main door panel. Turn it counterclockwise to reduce volume, clockwise to increase.

Some S2000 models also have a software volume setting accessible through the setup menu on the main board. Press the setup button inside the door to enter the configuration menu and look for the audio or volume option.

The S-Plus uses a similar potentiometer on the door panel. It is a simple analog control — no menus needed.

**Range:** Full mute to casino-floor loud. Most home users set it to about 25 to 35 percent of maximum.

### Bally Alpha Series

Bally machines typically have software-controlled volume accessed through the operator menu. Open the main door, press the service button to enter the menu, and navigate to Audio Settings. You will find separate controls for base volume, win celebration volume, and attract mode volume.

The ability to control attract mode volume separately is valuable. You can mute the attract sounds (the music and animations that play when nobody is actively playing) while keeping gameplay sounds at a comfortable level. This means the machine stays quiet when nobody is using it.

**Range:** 0 to 100 percent in increments of 5 or 10 percent.

### WMS Bluebird and Bluebird 2

WMS machines offer volume control through the operator setup screen. Access it via the service panel inside the main door. The volume setting is under System Settings or Audio. WMS also allows you to set different volume levels for gameplay, bonus rounds, and attract mode.

**Range:** 0 to 100 percent. Bonus round audio can be set independently, which is useful because bonus celebrations tend to be the loudest part of gameplay.

### Aristocrat

Aristocrat machines (Mark VI, Viridian) have volume control in the operator menu. Look for Audio under the Configuration section. Like Bally, you can often set gameplay and attract mode volumes independently.

### Konami

Konami machines use a similar software-based volume control through the operator menu. Access via the service door and navigate to Sound Settings.

### General Rule

If you cannot find the volume control on your specific machine, call us. We provide [free lifetime tech support](/warranty) and can walk you through the exact steps for any machine we sell. It usually takes less than five minutes over the phone.

## Muting the Machine While Keeping the Lights

Some customers want a completely silent machine — all the visual spectacle with zero audio. This is common in bedrooms, home offices, or apartments with thin walls. Here is how to achieve it.

### Software Mute

The simplest approach is setting the volume to zero through the operator menu as described above. On most machines, this eliminates all speaker output while keeping every visual element fully functional. Lights flash, reels spin, screens animate — all in silence.

### Physical Speaker Disconnect

For a more permanent mute, you can disconnect the speaker wires from the amplifier board. This is a simple operation that involves unplugging one or two connectors inside the main door. The advantage is that no accidental menu change will restore the volume. The disadvantage is that you need to open the door and reconnect to get sound back.

We generally recommend the software mute over physical disconnect unless you are certain you will never want sound. The software option is reversible in seconds.

### The Volume Knob Solution

If your machine has a potentiometer (physical dial), you can turn it all the way down. On IGT machines, counterclockwise until it stops. This provides a true analog zero that eliminates any residual hum or static that some machines produce even at the lowest software setting.

## External Volume Solutions

If the built-in controls do not provide the range or precision you want, external solutions can help.

### Inline Volume Attenuator

An **inline volume attenuator** is a small device (typically $10 to $25) that wires between the amplifier and the speaker. It gives you an external dial to control volume without opening the machine. Some customers mount the attenuator dial on the outside of the cabinet for easy access.

This is particularly useful on older machines where the internal volume control is inconvenient to reach or has a narrow adjustment range.

### Speaker Replacement

The factory speakers in most slot machines are designed for casino environments — meaning they prioritize volume and durability over audio quality. Replacing them with smaller, lower-wattage speakers reduces maximum volume while often improving sound clarity at low volumes.

A pair of 3-inch, 5-watt speakers from any electronics supplier ($10 to $20) can replace the factory 10 to 25-watt speakers. The result is a machine that sounds better at living-room volumes and physically cannot get as loud as it could with factory speakers.

### Headphone Jack Modification

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

This is a popular modification for apartment dwellers or late-night players. Adding a **headphone jack** to a slot machine involves wiring a 3.5mm audio jack to the speaker output with a switching circuit that mutes the internal speakers when headphones are plugged in.

The parts cost about $5 to $15 and the installation takes about 30 minutes if you are comfortable with basic soldering. We can walk you through it over the phone if you have basic electronics skills, or any local electronics repair shop can do it for $30 to $50.

With headphones, you get the full casino audio experience without any external sound. This is the best solution for players who want immersive audio at midnight without waking the household.

### Bluetooth Transmitter

A wireless alternative to the headphone jack. Attach a **Bluetooth audio transmitter** ($20 to $35) to the speaker output and pair it with Bluetooth headphones or a Bluetooth speaker. This gives you wireless audio control and the ability to route machine sound to any Bluetooth device.

The slight audio delay with Bluetooth (typically 40 to 100 milliseconds depending on the codec) is generally not noticeable during slot play since there is no precise audio-to-action synchronization required.

## Eliminating Coin Noise

If your machine is set up for coin operation and the hopper payout noise is the issue, you have two options.

### Switch to Free Play Mode

The simplest solution. In [free play mode](/blog/what-is-free-play-mode-on-a-slot-machine), no coins are involved at all. Credits are added by pressing a button, and wins simply add to the credit meter. Zero coin noise because there are zero coins. This eliminates the single loudest sound a slot machine makes.

### Hopper Dampening

If you want to keep coin play for the authentic experience but reduce the payout noise, you can dampen the coin tray. Line the metal tray with a thin layer of felt or rubber matting. The coins land on the soft surface instead of clanging against bare metal. This reduces payout noise by roughly 50 to 60 percent. The coins still accumulate in the tray, but the metallic clatter becomes a softer, muffled sound.

Some customers also place a small rubber mat under the hopper mechanism inside the cabinet to reduce the vibration transmitted to the cabinet shell.

## Room-Level Soundproofing

If you want to go beyond machine-level controls and soundproof the room where your machine lives, here are the most effective approaches ranked by cost and effectiveness.

### Level 1: Soft Furnishings ($0 to $200)

The cheapest soundproofing is stuff you probably already have. Carpeted floors, curtains, upholstered furniture, and bookshelves all absorb sound and reduce transmission to adjacent rooms. A game room with hardwood floors and bare walls will echo and amplify machine noise. The same room with carpet, curtains, and a couch will be noticeably quieter from outside.

If your game room has hard floors, a thick area rug under and around the machine makes a measurable difference. Adding heavy curtains to any windows further dampens sound transmission.

### Level 2: Acoustic Panels ($100 to $400)

**Acoustic foam panels** or fabric-wrapped acoustic panels mounted on the walls absorb mid and high-frequency sound — exactly the frequency range where slot machine audio lives. A set of 12 to 16 panels covering 20 to 30 percent of the wall area will reduce perceived volume from outside the room by 30 to 50 percent.

These panels are available from Amazon or any music supply store for $1 to $4 per square foot. They come in various colors and can be arranged attractively. Some customers use them as decorative elements in their casino-themed game room.

### Level 3: Door Seal and Upgrade ($50 to $300)

Sound leaks through gaps, and the biggest gap in most rooms is under and around the door. An **automatic door sweep** ($20 to $40) seals the gap under the door when it closes. Adhesive weatherstripping around the door frame ($10 to $15) seals the perimeter. Together, these reduce sound transmission through the door by 40 to 60 percent.

If your game room has a hollow-core interior door, replacing it with a solid-core door ($100 to $250) is one of the most effective single upgrades you can make. Solid-core doors block significantly more sound than hollow-core models.

### Level 4: Serious Soundproofing ($500 to $2,000+)

For dedicated game rooms or basement casinos where you want near-complete sound isolation, professional-grade solutions include adding a second layer of drywall with **Green Glue** sound-dampening compound between layers, installing resilient channel to decouple the wall surface from the framing, and adding insulation to the wall cavities.

This level of soundproofing is typically done during construction or renovation and reduces sound transmission by 70 to 90 percent. It is overkill for most home slot machine installations but worth considering if you are building a dedicated casino room from scratch or if you live in a condo with noise-sensitive neighbors. Check out our [home casino build guide](/blog/how-to-build-home-casino) for more on creating a dedicated gaming space.

## Machine Placement Tips

Where you put the machine in the room affects perceived noise more than you might expect.

**Corner placement amplifies bass frequencies.** Putting a machine in a corner creates a natural bass trap that makes low-frequency sounds (the hum of the machine, the rumble of the hopper) louder. Pull the machine at least 18 to 24 inches from any corner.

**Wall placement reflects sound.** A machine flush against a wall reflects sound off that wall and back into the room, increasing perceived volume. Leave at least 6 inches behind the machine — which you should do anyway for ventilation, as we discuss in our [setup guide](/blog/how-to-set-up-your-slot-machine-at-home).

**Carpet absorbs impact noise.** Place the machine on carpet or a thick rubber mat rather than bare hard flooring. This reduces vibration transmission and eliminates the resonance that hard floors create.

**Face the machine away from bedrooms.** Speakers are typically located behind the top glass or in the door panel — both facing the player. Orienting the machine so the speaker side faces away from bedrooms or shared walls reduces transmission to those spaces.

## Noise Levels by Configuration

Here is a quick reference for expected noise levels based on how you set up your machine.

### Quietest: Free Play, Volume Off
- 40 to 50 dB (mechanical reels only)
- Comparable to a quiet library or gentle rain
- Only sounds are reel mechanics and button clicks

### Quiet: Free Play, Low Volume
- 50 to 60 dB
- Comparable to a normal conversation
- Comfortable for late-night play in most homes

### Moderate: Free Play, Medium Volume
- 60 to 70 dB
- Comparable to a television at normal volume
- The sweet spot for most home users

### Loud: Coin Mode, Medium Volume
- 65 to 75 dB during play, 75 to 85 dB during payouts
- Comparable to a loud television or running dishwasher
- Payouts are the noisy part

### Casino Authentic: Coin Mode, Full Volume
- 80 to 90 dB
- Comparable to a food blender or busy restaurant
- Appropriate for dedicated game rooms with soundproofing

## The Bottom Line

Slot machine noise is completely manageable. Between built-in volume controls, free play mode, speaker modifications, and basic room treatment, you can dial the noise from casino-floor loud to near-silent depending on your needs.

The most common setup among our customers is free play mode with volume set to about 30 percent — enough to enjoy the sound effects and win celebrations without overpowering conversation or carrying to other rooms. Start there and adjust to taste.

If noise is your primary concern about buying a slot machine, do not let it stop you. We have helped customers in studio apartments get their machines to acceptable levels. A house with a dedicated game room is an even easier problem to solve.

[Browse our inventory](/shop) and when you are ready, [contact us](/contact) for specific guidance on noise management for the machine you choose. We have been solving this exact problem for 33 years.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: CRT to LCD Monitor Upgrade: Modernizing Your Slot Machine Display
excerpt: Everything you need to know about replacing a failing CRT monitor with an LCD kit in your IGT S2000, S-Plus, or Bally S6000 slot machine — costs, compatibility, and step-by-step overview.
body:
The CRT monitor inside your slot machine was built to last a decade of 24/7 casino use. For a home machine that runs a few hours a week, it might last 20 or 30 years. But eventually, every CRT reaches the end of its life. When that happens, you have two choices: find another aging CRT to install, or upgrade to a modern LCD panel that will outlast the rest of the machine.

After 33 years of servicing slot machines in Kingman, Arizona, we have seen thousands of CRT failures and helped hundreds of customers through the upgrade decision. This guide covers everything you need to know — why CRTs fail, which LCD kits fit which machines, what it costs, and whether you should tackle it yourself or have it done professionally.

## Why CRT Monitors Fail

CRT (cathode ray tube) monitors are analog devices with finite lifespans. Understanding how they fail helps you recognize the warning signs and decide when to act.

### Capacitor Degradation

The most common failure mode in slot machine CRTs is capacitor aging. Electrolytic capacitors in the monitor chassis dry out over time, losing their ability to filter and regulate voltage. Symptoms include a shrinking picture (image pulls away from the edges), brightness fluctuations, and eventual failure to display an image at all. A technician can sometimes recap the monitor board — replacing all electrolytic capacitors — for $50 to $100 in parts. But this is a temporary fix on a monitor that is fundamentally aging out.

### Phosphor Burn-In

Slot machine CRTs display the same image elements for years — pay tables, credit displays, and game graphics in fixed positions. Over time, these static elements literally burn into the phosphor coating on the inside of the tube. You see ghost images even when the display content changes. Burn-in is irreversible.

### Flyback Transformer Failure

The flyback transformer generates the high voltage needed to accelerate electrons toward the screen. When it fails, the monitor goes completely dark. Flyback replacements are becoming increasingly difficult to source for slot machine monitors, particularly the Wells Gardner and Kortek units commonly found in IGT and Bally machines.

### Yoke and Convergence Issues

Color CRTs use three electron guns (red, green, blue) that must converge precisely on the screen. As components age, convergence drifts and colors bleed or separate. Adjustments help temporarily, but progressive drift indicates end-of-life.

### The Supply Problem

Here is the bigger issue: CRT manufacturing ended globally in the early 2010s. No one makes new CRT monitors. The supply of replacement tubes is finite and shrinking. Refurbished CRTs are available today at $50 to $100, but in five years, they will be scarce and expensive. An LCD upgrade is future-proofing your machine.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

## Compatible LCD Kits by Machine Model

Not all LCD kits are equal, and not all fit all machines. Here is a breakdown by the most common platforms.

### IGT S2000

The S2000 uses a 9-inch CRT (some variants use a 10-inch) for the upper information display. This is the small screen that shows credits, bet amounts, and game messages — not the reels themselves (those are mechanical). The monitor is typically a Wells Gardner U2000 or equivalent.

**Compatible LCD kits:** 9-inch or 10-inch direct-fit LCD replacements are available from aftermarket suppliers. These kits include the LCD panel, a driver board, mounting bracket, and wiring harness that connects to the existing video signal from the game board. The most common kits output the same resolution the game board expects — typically 15kHz at 240p or 480i.

**Resolution and aspect ratio:** The original S2000 CRT is 4:3 aspect ratio at low resolution. LCD kits designed for this application match the 4:3 ratio perfectly. Do not buy a widescreen panel — it will stretch the image or leave black bars on the sides.

**Difficulty:** Moderate. Requires removing the old CRT (careful — they hold high voltage even when unpowered), mounting the LCD panel and driver board in the same space, and connecting the video and power harness. A competent DIYer with basic electronics comfort can do this in 2 to 3 hours.

### IGT S-Plus

The S-Plus predates the S2000 and uses an even simpler display system. Many S-Plus machines use a VFD (vacuum fluorescent display) rather than a CRT for the credit/bet display, and the upper display is a small CRT for attract modes.

**Compatible LCD kits:** Similar 9-inch kits work for the S-Plus, though mounting may require minor bracket modifications since the S-Plus cabinet has slightly different internal dimensions than the S2000.

**Resolution and aspect ratio:** Same 4:3, 15kHz signal as the S2000. The game boards are similar enough that the same LCD driver boards work.

**Difficulty:** Moderate. Similar to the S2000 process with minor mounting differences.

### Bally S6000 Series

Bally machines from the S6000 era use larger CRT monitors — typically 15-inch or 17-inch units for video display machines, and smaller units for reel-spinner information displays. The video signal is typically standard-resolution analog.

**Compatible LCD kits:** 15-inch and 17-inch LCD kits are available for Bally video machines. These are more complex installations because the CRT is the primary game display (not just an information screen). The LCD panel must handle the full video output of the game board including game graphics, bonus screens, and attract modes.

**Resolution and aspect ratio:** Bally S6000 video machines output at 480i or 480p depending on the model. 4:3 aspect ratio. The LCD kit driver board must be able to sync to the specific signal the Bally board outputs — not all generic kits work. Buy a kit specifically marketed for Bally S6000 compatibility.

**Difficulty:** Moderate to difficult. The larger monitor is heavier to remove, the mounting is more complex, and the video signal compatibility is more critical. For Bally video machines, we recommend professional installation or at minimum a detailed service manual for your specific model.

### Video Slot Platforms (WMS Bluebird, Aristocrat, Konami)

Many video slot machines from the late 1990s and early 2000s use larger CRTs (17-inch to 22-inch) as their primary display. LCD upgrades for these platforms exist but vary widely by model. Some manufacturers used proprietary video interfaces that require specific driver boards.

**General guidance:** For video slot platforms, research your specific model before buying any kit. The game board's video output format, resolution, and connector type must match the LCD driver board exactly. A mismatch means no picture or a scrambled display.

## Resolution and Aspect Ratio Matching

This is where most DIY upgrades go wrong. Here are the rules.

### Match the Aspect Ratio

Old slot machine CRTs are 4:3. Your replacement LCD must be 4:3. A 16:9 widescreen panel will either stretch the image horizontally (making everything look fat) or display black bars on the sides (wasting screen space and looking wrong inside the bezel).

### Match the Signal Type

Slot machine game boards output analog video at specific frequencies. The most common are:

- 15kHz horizontal (standard NTSC/CGA timing) — most common in IGT machines
- 24kHz (EGA-style timing) — some Bally and WMS machines
- 31kHz (VGA timing) — newer platforms

Your LCD driver board must accept the frequency your game board outputs. A driver board designed for 31kHz VGA will not sync to a 15kHz signal. This is the most critical specification to verify before purchasing.

### Brightness and Viewing Angle

Slot machine monitors are viewed from close range at a specific angle. Choose an LCD panel with at least 250 nits brightness (more is better for the backlit casino look) and a wide viewing angle (178 degrees IPS panels are ideal). TN panels with narrow viewing angles look washed out from anything other than dead center.

![Bally slot machine from Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879c0e257dd60bea48569a9.jpeg)

## Cost Comparison: LCD Kit vs. CRT Refurbishment

### LCD Upgrade Kit: $150 to $400

- 9-inch to 10-inch kit for IGT S2000/S-Plus information display: $150 to $200
- 15-inch kit for Bally video machines: $200 to $300
- 17-inch to 19-inch kit for larger video slot platforms: $250 to $400
- Professional installation (if you hire a technician): Add $100 to $200 labor

### CRT Refurbishment: $50 to $150

- Recapping the monitor chassis (capacitor replacement): $50 to $100 in parts, $50 to $100 labor if you hire it out
- Replacement CRT monitor (used/refurbished): $75 to $150 depending on size and availability
- Flyback transformer replacement (if available): $30 to $60 part, $75 to $100 labor

### The Long-Term Math

A CRT refurb costs less today but buys you limited time. A recapped monitor might last another 5 to 10 years. A replacement used CRT is already aged and may last 3 to 7 years. Eventually, you will face the same decision again — and CRT parts will be harder to find.

An LCD upgrade costs more upfront but is essentially permanent. LCD panels last 50,000 to 100,000 hours. At 10 hours per week of home use, that is 96 to 192 years. The LCD will outlive you, your children, and probably the machine itself.

## Step-by-Step Overview: LCD Upgrade on an IGT S2000

This is a general overview. Your specific kit will include detailed instructions. Always follow the kit manufacturer's instructions over this general guide.

### Step 1: Power Down and Discharge

Unplug the machine and wait at least 30 minutes. CRT monitors store high voltage in their flyback capacitor even when powered off. This voltage can be lethal. If you are not comfortable working around high-voltage electronics, hire a professional.

To safely discharge: use an insulated screwdriver with a wire clipped from its shaft to the monitor chassis ground. Slide the screwdriver tip under the anode cap (the suction cup on the side of the tube) and touch the connector underneath. You may hear a snap. This discharges the stored voltage.

### Step 2: Remove the Old CRT

Disconnect the video cable, power connector, and degaussing coil connector from the monitor chassis. Remove the mounting bolts (typically 4 bolts securing the monitor frame to the cabinet). Carefully slide the CRT assembly out. CRTs are heavy and fragile — the glass envelope can implode if cracked. Wear safety glasses and work carefully.

### Step 3: Prepare the Mounting

Your LCD kit includes a mounting bracket or adapter plate designed to use the same bolt holes as the original CRT mount. Test-fit the bracket and confirm alignment with the bezel opening. You want the LCD panel centered in the visible area.

### Step 4: Install the LCD Panel and Driver Board

Mount the LCD panel to the bracket per kit instructions. Secure the driver board to the cabinet interior using the included standoffs or Velcro mounting. Connect the LVDS cable from the driver board to the LCD panel. Route cables neatly and away from any heat sources.

### Step 5: Connect Video and Power

Connect the video input cable from your kit's driver board to the game board's video output. This is typically a VGA-style connector or a pin header, depending on the kit and machine. Connect the power cable — most kits tap into the machine's existing 12V supply or include a small power adapter.

### Step 6: Power Up and Adjust

Power on the machine. The LCD should display the game's boot sequence and then the attract mode or main game screen. Use the driver board's OSD (on-screen display) buttons to adjust brightness, contrast, position, and scaling if needed. Some kits auto-detect and require no adjustment.

### Step 7: Secure and Close

Once the display looks correct, secure all cables with zip ties, close the cabinet, and test thoroughly. Play several rounds and verify all screens display correctly — attract mode, game play, bonus features, and error/tilt displays.

## When to Upgrade vs. When to Refurbish

Here is our honest recommendation based on decades of experience.

**Upgrade to LCD if:**

- Your CRT has failed completely (dark screen, no high voltage)
- You have already recapped the monitor once and it is failing again
- The CRT has significant burn-in affecting viewing enjoyment
- You want a brighter, sharper display with better color
- You plan to keep the machine long-term (10+ years)
- You have a video slot machine where the CRT is the primary game display

**Stick with CRT refurbishment if:**

- The CRT has minor issues (needs recapping, slight brightness reduction)
- You prefer the authentic warm glow and curvature of a CRT display
- You are on a tight budget and $50 to $100 solves the problem
- The machine is an [IGT S2000 reel spinner](/blog/igt-s2000-americas-most-popular-home-slot-machine) where the CRT is only the small information display (not the primary game view)
- You are a purist who values originality over longevity

## Important Safety Warnings

CRT monitors contain hazardous voltages and materials. Take these warnings seriously.

- **High voltage:** CRTs store 20,000 to 30,000 volts in the flyback/anode assembly. This voltage persists after power is removed. Always discharge before touching any monitor component.
- **Implosion risk:** The glass envelope is under vacuum. A cracked tube can implode violently, throwing glass shards. Handle with care and wear safety glasses.
- **Lead content:** CRT glass contains lead. Dispose of old CRTs properly through electronics recycling, not regular trash.
- **Weight:** A 17-inch CRT monitor assembly weighs 30 to 50 pounds. Support it properly when removing from the cabinet.

If any of this makes you uncomfortable, that is perfectly reasonable. A local TV repair shop, arcade technician, or slot machine service tech can do the upgrade for you. Or [contact us](/contact) — we can walk you through the process over the phone with our [free lifetime tech support](/warranty).

## Where to Find LCD Kits and CRT Replacements

For parts sourcing, check our guide on [where to find slot machine parts](/blog/where-to-find-slot-machine-parts). Specialized arcade and gaming monitor suppliers carry LCD kits designed specifically for slot machine applications. Avoid generic LCD controllers meant for PC monitors — they often do not support the 15kHz signal frequencies that slot machine game boards output.

If you are dealing with [error codes](/blog/slot-machine-error-codes-what-they-mean) related to a failing monitor, sometimes the issue is the monitor itself and sometimes it is the game board's video output. Diagnosing which component is at fault before ordering parts saves time and money.

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

## Our Machines Come Ready to Play

Every machine in our [inventory of 334 machines](/shop) is sold with fully functional displays — whether that is an original CRT in good condition or an LCD upgrade already installed. We test every display as part of our refurbishment process and will not ship a machine with a failing monitor.

If you are buying a machine from us and have a preference for LCD over CRT (or vice versa), let us know. We can often accommodate display preferences on machines that are still being prepped.

For machines you already own, our [free lifetime tech support](/warranty) covers walk-through assistance on LCD upgrades. Call us, tell us your machine model and the kit you purchased, and we will guide you through the installation step by step.

Whether you decide to upgrade or refurbish, the goal is the same — keeping your machine displaying bright, clear graphics for years to come. The CRT-to-LCD upgrade is one of the best modernizations you can make to a classic slot machine, preserving everything that makes it great while eliminating the one component most likely to fail.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Slot Machine Insurance: Protecting Your Investment at Home
excerpt: How to insure your slot machine under homeowners insurance, what scheduled property endorsements cover, liability considerations, and documentation tips from a 33-year dealer.
body:
You just spent $1,500 to $3,000 on a slot machine, plus shipping. It is sitting in your game room looking fantastic. Now ask yourself: what happens if a pipe bursts and floods the basement? What if someone breaks in and steals it? What if a guest trips over the power cord, falls into the machine, and breaks both the machine and their wrist?

Insurance is not exciting, but for an item that costs as much as a decent used car and weighs 275 pounds, it deserves five minutes of your attention. We have had customers over our 33 years in business deal with all of these scenarios. Here is what we have learned about protecting your investment.

## Does Standard Homeowners Insurance Cover Slot Machines?

The short answer is: probably, but with significant limitations.

Standard homeowners insurance (HO-3 policies, which are the most common in America) covers your personal property against named perils — fire, theft, vandalism, certain water damage, and other specified events. A slot machine sitting in your home qualifies as personal property, just like furniture, electronics, or art.

However, there are two major limitations.

### Coverage Limits on Individual Items

Most homeowners policies cap coverage on any single item at $1,000 to $2,500 unless you specifically schedule (itemize) it. If your machine is worth $2,500 and your policy limits individual items to $1,500, you are underinsured by $1,000.

### Category Exclusions

Some policies have specific exclusions or sub-limits for categories like electronics, gaming equipment, or collectibles. Read your policy declarations page carefully. If there is a sub-limit for electronics or hobby equipment, your slot machine may fall under that cap.

### The Deductible Factor

Remember that any claim is subject to your deductible. If your deductible is $1,000 and your $1,500 machine is damaged, you are only collecting $500 from the insurer (assuming no other policy limitations). For some owners, the math does not justify a claim on a lower-value machine.

## Scheduled Personal Property Endorsements

This is the right answer for most slot machine owners. A scheduled personal property endorsement (also called a floater or rider) adds specific items to your policy at their appraised or agreed-upon value. Here is why this matters.

### What Scheduling Gets You

- **Agreed value coverage:** The insurer agrees to pay the full scheduled amount in case of a total loss, no depreciation applied
- **Broader perils:** Scheduled items are typically covered for all risks (open peril) rather than just the named perils in your base policy — meaning accidental damage, mysterious disappearance, and other events not covered under standard terms
- **No deductible:** Many scheduled property endorsements have zero deductible or a very low one
- **No sub-limits:** Your item is covered at its full scheduled value regardless of category caps in the base policy

### What It Costs

Scheduling a slot machine typically costs $15 to $50 per year depending on the value, your location, and your insurer. For a $2,000 machine, expect roughly $25 to $35 annually. That is negligible insurance for full replacement value coverage.

### How to Get It

Call your homeowners insurance agent and tell them you want to schedule a personal property item. They will need:

- Description of the item (slot machine, manufacturer, model)
- Value (your purchase receipt is the best documentation)
- Serial number
- Photos

Some insurers require a formal appraisal for items over a certain value ($5,000 is a common threshold). For most slot machines in the $1,000 to $3,000 range, your purchase receipt from a dealer is sufficient documentation.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

## Liability: What If a Guest Gets Injured?

Slot machines are heavy, have sharp metal edges inside the cabinet, and sit in social spaces where alcohol is often consumed. Liability is a legitimate consideration.

### Scenarios That Create Liability

- A guest leans on the machine and it tips (unlikely with upright models, but possible with improperly secured slant-tops on stands)
- A child opens the unlocked cabinet door and touches internal components
- Someone trips over the power cord
- A guest reaches into the coin tray and cuts themselves on a sharp edge
- Electrical fault causes a shock

### Your Homeowners Liability Coverage

Standard homeowners policies include personal liability coverage, typically $100,000 to $300,000. This covers you if someone is injured on your property, including by your personal property. A slot machine injury would generally fall under this coverage the same way a guest tripping on your stairs would.

### Reducing Liability Risk

- Keep cabinet doors locked when the machine is not being serviced
- Secure slant-top machines to their stands (they should be bolted, not just resting)
- Route power cords along walls and under cable covers, never across walking paths
- Keep the machine away from high-traffic areas where someone could bump into it
- If children are present, consider keeping the machine powered off and locked when not in supervised use
- Maintain the machine properly — a well-maintained machine is a safe machine. Our [maintenance guide](/blog/slot-machine-maintenance-homeowners-guide) covers what to check regularly

## Theft and Burglary Coverage

Slot machines are not easy to steal — they weigh 275 pounds and do not exactly fit in a backpack. But break-ins happen, and a determined thief with a dolly could take one. More realistically, if your home is burglarized, the thief might damage the machine trying to break into it (thinking it contains cash) even if they cannot take it.

### Standard Theft Coverage

Homeowners policies cover theft of personal property. The same individual item limits and deductible apply. If your machine is scheduled, theft is covered at full agreed value with no deductible.

### Documentation Is Critical

After a theft, your insurer will ask you to prove you owned the item and its value. This is where documentation saves you. Keep these on file (cloud storage recommended — a house fire that destroys your machine also destroys paper records in the same house):

- Purchase receipt with price, date, and seller information
- Serial number from the manufacturer's plate inside the door
- Photos of the machine — exterior from all sides, interior with door open, close-up of the serial plate
- Any appraisals or certificates of authenticity
- Your original listing or advertisement if you bought online

We provide detailed receipts with every sale that include manufacturer, model, serial number, game theme, and purchase price. Keep yours.

## Damage Coverage: Fire, Water, and More

The most common damage scenarios for home slot machines:

### Water Damage

Basement flooding, burst pipes, or roof leaks can destroy a slot machine's electronics. The game board, power supply, and any CRT monitor are particularly vulnerable to water. If your machine is in a basement, consider its position relative to floor drains and sump pumps.

**Insurance note:** Standard homeowners covers sudden and accidental water damage (burst pipe, appliance overflow) but not gradual seepage or flood. If you are in a flood zone, you need separate flood insurance (NFIP or private) to cover your machine against rising water.

### Fire

Fire coverage is standard in all homeowners policies. A slot machine destroyed by house fire is covered under personal property provisions.

### Power Surge

Lightning strikes and power surges can fry electronics instantly. This is covered under most policies, but prevention is better — always use a quality surge protector. Our [setup guide](/blog/how-to-set-up-your-slot-machine-at-home) recommends surge protection as essential equipment.

### Accidental Damage

You bump the machine with a furniture dolly while rearranging. A child throws a ball through the glass panel. You drop a tool into the open cabinet during maintenance. Standard homeowners does not cover accidental damage by the owner. A scheduled property endorsement typically does.

## Documentation Tips: Building Your Insurance File

Create a simple file (digital, stored in the cloud) with the following for each machine you own:

- **Purchase receipt** — shows value, date, seller
- **Serial number** — from the manufacturer's plate (usually inside the main door). Take a clear photo.
- **Exterior photos** — front, back, both sides, with the machine powered on showing the display
- **Interior photos** — open the main door and photograph the inside, showing the game board, power supply, and any identifiable components
- **Manufacturer and model** — IGT S2000, Bally Alpha, etc.
- **Game theme** — Double Diamond, Wheel of Fortune, etc.
- **Any modifications or upgrades** — LCD monitor upgrade, LED lighting mods, etc. These add value.
- **Maintenance records** — any repairs or parts replaced, with receipts

Update this file whenever you make changes to the machine. If you ever need to file a claim, this documentation makes the process dramatically smoother.

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

## Multiple Machines and Collections

If you own three or more machines, you are moving into collector territory. The insurance math changes.

### Blanket Scheduled Coverage

Instead of scheduling each machine individually, some insurers offer blanket coverage for collections. You declare a total collection value (say $8,000 for four machines) and any individual item is covered up to the blanket limit. This is simpler to manage than individual riders.

### Collections Insurance Specialists

For large collections (8+ machines, $20,000+ total value), consider specialty insurers that focus on collectibles and hobby equipment. Companies like Collectibles Insurance Services or American Collectors Insurance understand gaming equipment and offer tailored policies.

### Inventory Management

Maintain a current inventory list with values. Update it when you buy, sell, or significantly modify machines. Inform your insurer of any additions — a new machine is not automatically covered under an existing schedule.

## Umbrella Policies: Extra Protection

An umbrella policy provides liability coverage above and beyond your homeowners and auto policies, typically in $1 million increments. If you regularly host parties or events in your game room — especially where alcohol is served — an umbrella policy provides an extra layer of protection against injury lawsuits.

Umbrella policies are relatively inexpensive: $150 to $300 per year for $1 million in coverage. For homeowners with a game room or [home casino setup](/blog/how-to-build-home-casino), it is cheap peace of mind.

## Commercial Coverage for Business Use

If you use slot machines commercially — in a bar, restaurant, private club, or rental property — homeowners insurance does not apply. You need commercial property and general liability coverage.

Commercial coverage for gaming equipment costs more ($200 to $500 per year per machine depending on the setting) but covers business use, patron injuries, and commercial theft. If you operate machines where the public interacts with them, do not rely on personal homeowners coverage — it will be denied if the insurer determines commercial use.

## Our Recommendation

For most customers who buy a single machine for home entertainment:

- **Call your homeowners agent** and ask about scheduling the machine as personal property. Cost is minimal ($25 to $40/year) and coverage is comprehensive.
- **Document everything** — receipt, serial number, photos. Store digitally in the cloud.
- **Maintain the machine** — a [well-maintained machine](/blog/slot-machine-maintenance-homeowners-guide) is less likely to cause problems and maintains its value for insurance purposes.
- **Use a surge protector** — prevents the most common electrical damage scenario.
- **Lock the cabinet** — reduces liability and prevents unauthorized access to internal components.

Your slot machine is an investment — in entertainment, in your home's atmosphere, and financially. A few dollars a month in insurance and five minutes of documentation ensure that investment is protected no matter what happens.

Browse our [current inventory of 334 machines](/shop) and know that every purchase includes a detailed receipt documenting the machine's identity, value, and specifications — exactly what your insurer needs. Questions about any of this? [Contact us](/contact) anytime.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Slot Machine Lighting Mods: LED Upgrades, Belly Glass Backlighting, and Ambient Effects
excerpt: Transform your slot machine's visual impact with LED strip upgrades, belly glass backlighting, topper mods, and RGB ambient effects — complete guide with power considerations and before/after results.
body:
Nothing transforms a slot machine's presence in a room like lighting. A stock machine with aging fluorescent tubes looks flat and washed out. The same machine with LED upgrades — crisp belly glass illumination, vibrant topper lighting, and ambient glow — looks like it was just pulled from a Las Vegas casino floor and plugged in five minutes ago.

Lighting mods are the single highest-impact, lowest-difficulty upgrade you can make to a slot machine. Most require no soldering, no specialized tools, and less than an hour of work. Here is everything you need to know about bringing your machine's lighting into the modern era.

## Stock Lighting: What Came From the Factory

Understanding what you are replacing helps you choose the right upgrade.

### CCFL (Cold Cathode Fluorescent Lamps)

Most slot machines manufactured from the mid-1990s through the mid-2000s use CCFL tubes for internal illumination. These thin fluorescent tubes backlight the belly glass, top glass, and internal signage. They produce a diffuse, even light but have significant drawbacks:

- They dim over time (losing 30 to 50 percent brightness over 10 to 15 years)
- They require an inverter board that can fail
- Color temperature shifts toward pink/yellow as they age
- They contain mercury and require special disposal
- They flicker when failing, which looks terrible

### Incandescent Bulbs

Older machines and certain accent positions use standard incandescent or halogen bulbs — particularly in button lamps, candle lamps (the light on top of the machine that signals attendant calls), and some topper positions. These burn hot, consume significant power, and burn out regularly.

### Fluorescent Tubes (T8/T12)

Some larger cabinet positions use standard fluorescent tubes similar to office lighting. These are found behind large backlit panels and in base cabinets. Same aging problems as CCFLs but larger.

## LED Strip Replacement: The Foundation Upgrade

Replacing internal CCFLs and fluorescent tubes with LED strips is the most impactful single mod you can make.

![Bally slot machine from Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879c0e257dd60bea48569a9.jpeg)

### What You Need

- **LED strip lights:** 12V DC LED strips in 6000K to 6500K (cool white) for belly glass and top glass backlighting. Buy strips with adhesive backing and at least 60 LEDs per meter for even illumination.
- **Power source:** Most slot machines have 12V DC available on the power supply. You can tap into this directly. Alternatively, use a separate 12V adapter plugged into the machine's internal power strip.
- **Wire connectors:** Quick-connect terminals or solder-free strip connectors for clean wiring
- **Mounting clips or adhesive:** LED strips with 3M adhesive backing stick directly to clean metal surfaces inside the cabinet

### Belly Glass Backlighting

The belly glass is the large decorative panel on the front lower portion of the machine. It displays the game theme artwork and is backlit from behind. This is the most visible lighting element on any slot machine.

**Stock:** One or two CCFL tubes or a single fluorescent tube running horizontally behind the glass.

**Upgrade:** Two to three rows of LED strip running horizontally behind the glass, spaced evenly for uniform coverage. The result is dramatically brighter, more even illumination that makes the artwork pop.

**Installation:**
1. Open the main door and locate the belly glass backing area
2. Disconnect and remove the old CCFL tubes and inverter board (or fluorescent tube and ballast)
3. Clean the mounting surface with isopropyl alcohol
4. Cut LED strips to length (cut only at marked points on the strip)
5. Peel adhesive backing and press strips into position — horizontal rows spaced 2 to 3 inches apart
6. Connect power leads to the machine's 12V supply
7. Close up and power on

Time: 30 to 45 minutes. Difficulty: Easy.

### Top Glass and Topper Backlighting

The top glass panel shows the game logo and pay table. The topper (if present) is the illuminated sign on top of the cabinet. Both use similar backlighting that benefits from the same LED strip treatment.

Some machines have elaborate toppers with spinning elements or additional displays. For these, keep the mechanical components intact and replace only the static backlighting elements with LED strips.

## Button LED Upgrades

Slot machine buttons (Spin, Bet Max, Cash Out, etc.) are individually lit from behind. Stock lighting is typically a small #555 or #194 wedge-base incandescent bulb. These burn out frequently and produce a warm, dim light.

### LED Button Bulb Replacement

Direct-replacement LED bulbs in #555 and #194 wedge base are available in multiple colors. Simply pull the old incandescent bulb and push in the LED replacement. No wiring changes needed.

**Color choices:**
- Cool white (6000K): Clean, modern look matching other LED upgrades
- Warm white (3000K): Closer to original incandescent appearance
- Color-matched: Red for Cash Out, green for Bet, blue for Spin — creates a color-coded button layout
- RGB color-changing: Fun for home use, changes colors on a cycle

**Benefits:** LED button bulbs last 50,000+ hours versus 2,000 hours for incandescent. They run cool (no heat buildup in the button assembly), use a fraction of the power, and are significantly brighter.

Time: 5 minutes per bulb, 20 to 30 minutes for all buttons. Difficulty: Extremely easy.

## RGB Ambient Lighting

This is where you move beyond restoration into customization. RGB LED strips mounted to the exterior or interior edges of the cabinet create a colored ambient glow that makes the machine a room centerpiece.

### Popular RGB Mod Positions

- **Under-cabinet glow:** Mount RGB strips along the bottom edge of the cabinet facing downward. Creates a pool of colored light on the floor beneath the machine. Dramatic in a dark room.
- **Rear cabinet wash:** Mount strips along the back edges facing the wall. Creates a halo of light around the machine's silhouette.
- **Interior accent:** Mount strips inside the cabinet behind tinted glass panels for a colored glow visible through the glass.
- **Topper accent ring:** Mount strips around the base of the topper for a crown-like glow effect on top.

### RGB Controller Options

- **Basic controller with remote:** $10 to $20. Offers preset colors, dimming, and simple effects (fade, flash, strobe). Good enough for most installations.
- **WiFi/Bluetooth smart controller:** $20 to $40. Control from your phone, set schedules, sync with music, choose from millions of colors. Brands like Govee and LIFX offer reliable options.
- **Sound-reactive controller:** $15 to $30. LEDs pulse and change color in response to the machine's audio output. Creates a dynamic light show synchronized with game sounds.

### Installation Tips for RGB

- Use LED strips with waterproof silicone coating (IP65 rated) for exterior positions — they resist dust and are easier to clean
- Run the RGB power supply off a separate plug rather than tapping the machine's internal power — this keeps machine electronics isolated and lets you control ambient lighting independently
- Use aluminum LED channels with diffuser covers for a professional look instead of bare adhesive strips
- Keep RGB effects subtle — a slow color fade or static accent color looks sophisticated. Rapid flashing looks cheap.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

## Candle Lamp and Tower Light Upgrades

The candle lamp is the light on top of many slot machines that flashes to indicate service needs (hopper empty, door open, etc.). On the [IGT S2000](/blog/igt-s2000-americas-most-popular-home-slot-machine), it is a small cylindrical light on a short stalk.

Stock candle lamps use incandescent bulbs. Replacing with an LED equivalent makes it brighter and eliminates bulb changes. Some owners replace the entire candle assembly with a custom RGB unit that can be set to any color — a nice personalization touch.

## Power Considerations

Adding LED lighting draws additional power from somewhere. Here is how to plan.

### Power Draw Comparison

- Stock CCFL belly glass setup: 15 to 25 watts (tubes plus inverter)
- Stock incandescent button bulbs (8 buttons): 40 watts total
- Stock fluorescent topper tube: 15 to 20 watts
- **Total stock lighting draw: approximately 70 to 85 watts**

- LED strip belly glass replacement: 5 to 10 watts
- LED button bulbs (8 buttons): 4 watts total
- LED topper strips: 3 to 6 watts
- RGB ambient strips (2 meters): 10 to 15 watts
- **Total LED lighting draw: approximately 22 to 35 watts**

LED upgrades actually reduce total power consumption by 50 to 70 percent while producing more light. You will not overload anything.

### Tapping the Machine's Power Supply

Slot machine power supplies typically provide 12V DC and 5V DC rails for internal components. The 12V rail powers most lighting. Check the power supply rating plate — if it provides 5A or more on the 12V rail (60 watts), you have plenty of headroom for LED strips that draw only 10 to 15 watts total.

### Using External Power

For RGB ambient lighting, a separate 12V power adapter is cleaner. It keeps the mod isolated from machine electronics, makes troubleshooting easier, and lets you turn ambient lighting on or off independently of the machine itself. A basic 12V 2A adapter ($8 to $12) powers several meters of RGB strip.

## Before and After: The Impact

The visual difference between a stock-lit machine and a fully LED-upgraded machine is striking.

**Before:** Dim, yellowed belly glass illumination. Half the button lights burned out. Topper barely visible. Cabinet blends into the wall.

**After:** Crisp, bright belly glass art that pops with saturated color. Every button glowing uniformly. Topper visible from across the room. Ambient under-glow creating a pool of light that draws the eye. The machine looks alive.

In a [home casino setup](/blog/how-to-build-home-casino) with multiple machines, LED lighting mods on every machine create a cohesive, immersive atmosphere that transforms a basement into a genuine gaming experience. The machines become visual anchors for the room.

## Total Cost Breakdown

Here is what a full lighting overhaul costs in parts:

- LED strips for belly glass and top glass: $15 to $25
- LED button bulbs (set of 8 to 10): $15 to $25
- LED candle lamp replacement: $5 to $10
- RGB ambient strip kit with controller (2 meters): $20 to $35
- Aluminum channels and diffusers (optional, for clean look): $15 to $25
- Connectors, wire, and mounting supplies: $10 to $15

**Total: $80 to $135 for a complete transformation.**

Compare that to replacing burned-out stock bulbs and CCFLs repeatedly over the years. The LED mod pays for itself in eliminated replacement costs within 2 to 3 years, then saves money indefinitely while looking dramatically better.

## Tips for Best Results

- **Match color temperature** on all white LEDs. Mixing 4000K and 6500K strips looks inconsistent. Pick one temperature (we recommend 6000K to 6500K cool white for that fresh casino-floor look) and use it everywhere.
- **Diffuse the light.** Bare LED strips behind glass can show individual LED dots (hot spots). Use diffuser film or frosted tape between the strip and the glass, or position strips far enough back that the light blends.
- **Clean the glass first.** Years of dust, nicotine residue, or grime on the inside of belly glass panels blocks light. Clean all glass panels with glass cleaner before installing new lighting — the difference is immediate.
- **Test before final mounting.** Power up your LED strips temporarily with alligator clips before committing to adhesive mounting. Verify brightness, color, and coverage are what you want.
- **Photograph before and after.** You will want to see the comparison, and the photos are useful for [insurance documentation](/blog/slot-machine-insurance-protecting-your-investment) too.

## Combining Lighting Mods With Other Upgrades

Lighting pairs well with other common slot machine upgrades. If you are already opening up the cabinet for an [LCD monitor upgrade](/blog/crt-to-lcd-monitor-upgrade-slot-machine-display), do your LED lighting at the same time — you are already in there with the tools out.

Regular [maintenance](/blog/slot-machine-maintenance-homeowners-guide) sessions are also a natural time to add or adjust lighting. Every time you open the cabinet to clean or inspect, you can add another strip or swap another bulb.

Browse our [inventory of 334 machines](/shop) — every machine we sell is a candidate for these lighting mods. And if you want a machine that already looks showroom-fresh out of the box, our refurbishment process includes replacing burned-out lighting as standard. Questions about any mod on any machine? Our [free lifetime tech support](/warranty) team has done them all. [Contact us](/contact) anytime.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Multi-Game Conversion Kits: Turn One Slot Machine Into 50+ Games
excerpt: A 33-year dealer explains multi-game conversion kits — what they are, which platforms support them, DIY vs. pre-loaded options, and whether they're worth the investment.
body:
One of the most common questions we get from customers who already own a single-game slot machine is this: can I add more games to it? The answer, in many cases, is yes. Multi-game conversion kits let you transform a single-game cabinet into a multi-game platform with 50, 100, or even 200+ titles on one machine.

After 33 years of selling, converting, and servicing slot machines out of our Kingman, Arizona showroom, we have seen the conversion kit market evolve from a niche modification into a mainstream upgrade path. Here is everything you need to know before deciding whether a conversion kit is right for your machine.

## What Exactly Is a Multi-Game Conversion Kit?

A multi-game conversion kit is a hardware and software package that replaces your machine's existing game board with a new board loaded with multiple game titles. Instead of playing one game — say, Double Diamond on your IGT S2000 — you install the kit and suddenly have access to 50 or more games selectable from an on-screen menu.

The kit typically includes a replacement game board (the main PCB that runs the game software), a wiring harness adapter if needed, new EPROM chips or a flash memory card containing the game library, and sometimes an updated touchscreen interface or button overlay for game selection.

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

Think of it like upgrading the operating system on a computer. The cabinet, power supply, display, buttons, and coin handling all stay the same. You are just swapping out the brain that determines what games are available.

The concept is not new. IGT's own Game King platform was designed from the ground up as a multi-game system. What conversion kits do is bring that multi-game capability to platforms that were originally designed for single-game operation.

## Compatible Platforms: Which Machines Can Be Converted?

Not every slot machine is a good candidate for conversion. The feasibility depends on the platform architecture, display type, and button layout. Here are the major platforms that support conversion kits.

### IGT Game King

The IGT Game King is already a multi-game platform, so conversion here means expanding the game library rather than adding multi-game capability from scratch. A base Game King might ship with 31 poker games. Conversion kits can expand that to 50, 70, or even 100+ games including both poker variants and slot titles.

The Game King uses a modular software architecture that makes game library expansion straightforward. You swap the game chip set — replacing the existing EPROMs with chips containing the expanded library — and the machine recognizes the new games on its next boot cycle. No wiring changes, no display modifications, no button alterations.

This is the easiest and most reliable conversion available. If you already own a [Game King](/blog/igt-vs-bally-vs-aristocrat-comparing-slot-machine-brands) and want more games, this is a no-brainer upgrade.

### IGT S2000 Platform

The IGT S2000 is [America's most popular home slot machine](/blog/igt-s2000-americas-most-popular-home-slot-machine), and for good reason. It is also one of the more limited platforms for conversion because it was designed as a mechanical reel machine. The physical reels can only display specific symbol combinations, which limits what games the machine can run.

That said, S2000 conversions do exist. They work by changing the game chip and reel strips simultaneously. You get a different game — Triple Diamond instead of Double Diamond, for example — but you are still limited to one game at a time. True multi-game conversion on a mechanical reel machine is not practical because you would need to physically swap reel strips for each game.

However, if your S2000 has a video display (some later models did), multi-game kits become viable. Video-based S2000 variants can run multi-game software similar to the Game King platform.

### Bally Game Maker

The Bally Game Maker was Bally's answer to the IGT Game King — a purpose-built multi-game platform. Like the Game King, expanding its library is a chip-swap operation. Base configurations typically include 10 to 20 games, and conversion kits can push that to 50 or more titles.

Bally Game Maker kits are less commonly available than IGT kits due to Bally's smaller installed base, but they do exist. The games tend to focus on video slots and video poker, with some keno and blackjack variants mixed in.

### WMS Bluebird

The WMS Bluebird platform is a video-based system that can theoretically run multiple game titles. Conversion kits for the Bluebird are available but less standardized than IGT offerings. The Bluebird uses a PC-based architecture (essentially a computer running proprietary gaming software), which makes game loading more of a software installation than a chip swap.

Bluebird conversions can add 20 to 40 games depending on the kit. The games are primarily WMS video slot titles with bonus features — think Wizard of Oz, Monopoly, and similar theme-heavy games with second-screen bonuses.

### Bally Alpha Series

![Bally Alpha slot machine cabinet](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879c0e257dd60bea48569a9.jpeg)

The Bally Alpha platform uses a similar PC-based architecture to the Bluebird. Multi-game kits exist for the Alpha series, though availability varies. These kits can load 30 to 60 Bally video slot titles onto a single machine.

The Alpha's large screen and advanced graphics make it an excellent multi-game platform. Games look fantastic on the high-resolution display, and the machine's hardware has no trouble running any title in the Bally library from that era.

## Game Counts: What to Expect

Conversion kits come in various library sizes. Here is what is available at each tier.

### 50-Game Kits ($300-$450)

These are the entry-level multi-game kits. They typically include a mix of video poker variants (Jacks or Better, Deuces Wild, Double Bonus, etc.) and basic video slot games. On an IGT Game King platform, a 50-game kit gives you an excellent variety of poker and slots without overwhelming the selection menu.

### 100-Game Kits ($450-$600)

The sweet spot for most home users. A 100-game kit adds more exotic poker variants, additional slot themes, and usually some specialty games like keno or multi-hand poker. You get enough variety that you will never feel limited, without so many options that the menu becomes unwieldy.

### 200+ Game Kits ($600-$800)

The maximum-variety option. These mega-kits pack everything available for the platform into one package. You get every poker variant, every slot theme, specialty games, and sometimes regional game styles. The sheer number means some games will rarely get played, but if you like having options, this is the way to go.

## DIY Installation vs. Buying Pre-Loaded

You have two paths to multi-game capability: install a kit yourself, or buy a machine that already has a multi-game configuration installed.

### DIY Kit Installation

Installing a conversion kit yourself is doable if you are comfortable opening up the machine and handling circuit boards. The process varies by platform but generally involves these steps.

Power down the machine completely and unplug it. Open the main door and locate the game board — it is usually mounted inside the upper cabinet area. Remove the existing game chips (EPROMs) from their sockets, noting orientation. Install the new chips from the kit in the same sockets, matching orientation marks. Close up, power on, and follow any initialization procedure included with the kit.

On IGT Game King machines, this is straightforward — a 20-minute job for someone who has done it before. On PC-based platforms like the Bluebird or Alpha, it may involve connecting a USB drive and running a software installation, which takes longer but is not technically difficult.

The risk with DIY installation is static damage to the chips during handling and incorrect chip orientation. Both can render the board non-functional until corrected. Using an anti-static wrist strap and double-checking orientation before powering on eliminates most risk.

### Buying Pre-Loaded

The alternative is to buy a machine that already has the multi-game kit installed. When you purchase a multi-game machine from our [shop](/shop), the kit is already installed, tested, and verified working. Every game in the library has been cycled through during our refurbishment process.

The premium for a pre-loaded multi-game machine versus a single-game machine on the same platform is typically $300 to $700 — roughly equivalent to the kit cost itself. So you are essentially getting free installation and the peace of mind that everything works.

## Cost Comparison: Kit vs. Buying a Multi-Game Machine

Let us break down the economics for a typical scenario.

### Scenario A: Convert Your Existing Machine

- You already own an IGT Game King with 31 poker games
- 100-game conversion kit: $500
- Your time to install: 30-60 minutes
- Total additional cost: $500
- Result: 100 games on your existing machine
