title: Antique & Vintage Slot Machine Collector's Guide: Eras, Values, and What Buyers Need to Know
excerpt: From Mills Novelty cast-iron machines of the 1920s to Bally electromechanicals of the 1970s, this guide covers the major eras, top manufacturers, value factors, and what to look for when buying vintage.
body:
A lot of buyers who contact us are not looking for a machine that just came off the Las Vegas casino floor. They want something older. Something with history. A mechanical one-armed bandit from the 1940s, or a classic electromechanical machine from the 1960s, or a Bally from an era when slot machines were built to last decades.

Collecting antique and vintage slot machines is a different pursuit from simply buying a modern used machine. The values are different. The legal considerations are different. The expertise required to evaluate a machine is different. And the satisfaction of owning a machine that has genuinely lived through eras of American history is different too.

This guide draws on 34 years in the used slot machine market to walk you through what you need to know: the major eras of slot machine manufacturing, who made the important machines, what drives value, and what to look for before you buy.

![Slot machine investment and collectible value — Used Slot Shop](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879c9f457dd601474857328.jpeg)

## A Brief History of Slot Machine Manufacturing

Slot machines were invented in the United States in the 1890s. Charles Fey, a San Francisco mechanic, is credited with creating the first true slot machine around the mid-1890s — a three-reel mechanical device he called the Liberty Bell, which paid out on combinations of playing card suit symbols and a cracked liberty bell.

Fey's invention spawned an entire industry. By the early 1900s, manufacturers in Chicago and the Midwest were producing machines in commercial quantities.

### The Golden Age Manufacturers: 1890s Through the 1950s

Four companies dominated the American slot machine industry through the mechanical era.

**Mills Novelty Company** (Chicago) was the largest and most prolific manufacturer of antique slot machines. Herbert Mills produced the Mills Liberty Bell around 1907, improving on Fey's original design and pushing the technology into mass production. Mills machines are known for their ornate cast-iron and later aluminum cabinets, their popularization of fruit symbols (which Mills used partly to distance the machines from gambling associations), and their mechanical precision. Collectors prize Mills machines above nearly all others from this era.

**Caille Bros. Manufacturing Company** (Detroit) was one of Mills' primary competitors through the early 20th century. Caille built elaborate, beautifully decorated upright machines that are highly sought by serious collectors. The company produced a wide range of coin-operated devices beyond slots, including fortune-telling machines and arcade games.

**Watling Manufacturing** (Chicago) produced machines from the early 1900s through the 1950s. Watling machines are recognized for quality construction and distinctive designs, including the "Rol-A-Top" models with their transparent spinning disk mounted above the cabinet — a striking visual element that made these machines stand out on a saloon floor.

**Jennings & Company** (Chicago) was a major slot machine manufacturer active through the mid-20th century. Jennings machines are known for their colorful painted cabinets and models like the "Sun Chief" that became collector favorites. Jennings produced machines in large enough quantities that examples are more accessible to today's collectors than some rarer Caille models.

### The Electromechanical Era: 1960s and 1970s

Bally Manufacturing changed slot machines permanently in 1963 with the **Money Honey** — the first fully electromechanical slot machine. Money Honey used electric motors to control reel stops while retaining some mechanical elements. It could pay out up to 500 coins automatically, compared to the 20-coin maximum of earlier mechanical machines. This shift opened the door to larger jackpots and more complex gameplay.

Bally machines from the 1960s and 1970s now occupy a sweet spot for collectors: genuinely historic, produced in quantities that keep prices accessible, and mechanical enough that skilled technicians can still service them.

### Early Electronic Machines: 1970s and 1980s

Fortune Coin Company introduced the first video slot machine in 1976. IGT (International Game Technology) acquired the technology and began mass-producing video slots, fundamentally changing what a slot machine was. By the mid-1980s, electronic slot machines were overtaking mechanical and electromechanical models on casino floors.

Machines from this transitional era — early IGT video slots, first-generation Bally electronic cabinets — are now approaching or crossing the 25-year ownership threshold in age-restricted states and are the subject of growing collector interest.

## The 25-Year Threshold and What It Means in 2026

Many US states allow private ownership of slot machines only if they are at least 25 years old. In 2026, that means any machine manufactured in 2001 or earlier qualifies. This threshold covers the entire antique and vintage machine spectrum, plus a substantial range of machines from the electronic era.

For a full breakdown of which states apply age restrictions and which allow unrestricted ownership regardless of machine age, see our [state-by-state legal guide](/blog/is-it-legal-to-own-a-slot-machine-state-by-state-guide) and our dedicated [25-year rule guide](/blog/25-year-rule-antique-slot-machine-laws).

## What Drives Value in Antique Slot Machines

Antique slot machine values range from a few hundred dollars to well over $100,000 for the rarest examples. Several factors determine where any specific machine falls on that spectrum.

### Age and Era

Generally, older machines in good condition command higher prices. Pre-1920s machines in working order are extremely rare and the most valuable. Machines from the 1920s through 1940s form the core of the serious collector market. 1950s and 1960s machines are more available and more accessible in price.

### Manufacturer and Model

Mills and Caille are typically the most valued names among antique collectors. A Mills Novelty machine in good original condition from the 1930s can sell for several thousand to tens of thousands of dollars depending on the specific model. Published auction results show Mills "War Eagle" models selling at approximately $40,000, and Mills "Horse Head Bonus" machines selling at approximately $75,000. More common Mills production models from the same era may be found for $2,500–$8,000 in good condition.

Specific model variations matter enormously. Rare denominations, unusual design elements, or limited-run models command premiums over standard production machines.

### Condition: Original vs. Restored

Condition is the most variable factor in antique slot machine pricing. Collectors distinguish between three levels:

**Original and unrestored:** All original parts, original paint or finish, original mechanics. Even with wear and patina, this is often the most valued state among serious collectors. An original machine tells its own story.

**Professionally restored:** Mechanics rebuilt and tested, cabinet refinished to original specifications, glass replaced or accurately reproduced. A quality professional restoration adds value over a worn, non-functional machine — but typically not over a good original.

**Amateur or parts-mix restoration:** The most problematic category. Non-original paint colors, components sourced from different machines, incorrect replacement parts. This can significantly reduce a machine's collector value, even if it looks superficially clean.

Before buying any antique slot machine, ask explicitly about what is original versus what has been replaced or restored. A reputable seller will know and will tell you.

### Originality of Specific Components

The **belly glass** — the decorative painted glass panel on the lower front of the cabinet — is often damaged over decades of use and is difficult to replace authentically. An intact original belly glass adds meaningful value. The same applies to the original cash box, original side castings, and original reel assembly. A machine that is complete with original components throughout is worth materially more than one with mixed or replacement parts.

### Working Condition

A machine that operates — accepting coins, spinning reels, and paying out as originally designed — is worth more than a display piece. However, some genuinely rare machines command significant prices even non-working, due to their historical rarity.

## Price Ranges by Era

These represent general market ranges based on published auction results and dealer pricing. Individual machines vary widely based on model, condition, and current demand.

**Pre-1920s mechanical machines (Caille, early Mills):**
Extremely rare in working condition with original components. Examples with documented provenance sell in the range of $15,000 to well over $100,000 for significant pieces. The Mills Novelty "Owl" machine from the 1890s is known to sell around $15,000–$18,000 in working original condition.

**1920s–1930s machines (Mills, Caille, Watling, Jennings):**
The heart of the antique collector market. Common production models in fair condition: $2,500–$8,000. Desirable models in good original condition: $8,000–$25,000. Trophy pieces like the War Eagle and Horse Head Bonus models: $40,000–$75,000 at auction.

**1940s machines:**
Similar premium for trophy models, with common production machines available in the $2,000–$8,000 range depending on model and condition.

**1950s mechanical machines:**
More plentiful, broadly accessible. Good condition examples: $1,500–$5,000.

**1960s–1970s electromechanical machines (Bally Money Honey era and similar):**
$800–$4,000 for common models in working order. Rare or mint-condition examples fetch more.

**1980s–early 1990s early electronic machines (early IGT, early Bally electronic):**
$500–$1,500 for well-maintained examples. These are at the entry end of the vintage collector market and are increasingly in demand.

## What to Look for When Buying an Antique Slot Machine

Given the values involved and the complexity of condition assessment, buying an antique slot machine requires more due diligence than buying a modern used machine.

**Ask for detailed photographs.** Specifically: both side castings, the belly glass, the interior mechanism, the cash box, and the reel assembly. Each component tells you something about the machine's history and originality.

**Confirm what is original versus replaced.** Ask directly which parts have been replaced or restored. A reputable seller will know and will answer. Be wary of any seller who cannot or will not tell you.

**Verify that the mechanism functions.** Ask to see a video of the machine operating — accepting a coin, spinning reels, making a payout. A working demonstration tells you more than any number of photos.

**Know the specific model.** Mills, Caille, Watling, and Jennings each produced many different models with significant variation in rarity and value. Collector communities have documented production histories and value ranges for most major models. Research the specific model before committing.

**Verify legal status in your state.** Pre-2001 machines qualify under the 25-year rule in most states, but some states restrict all slot machines regardless of age. Our [state legal guide](/blog/is-it-legal-to-own-a-slot-machine-state-by-state-guide) is a good starting point.

## Modern Vintage: 1990s and Early 2000s Machines

Not all vintage collecting focuses on pre-WWII mechanical machines. A growing segment of buyers are drawn to machines from the 1990s and early 2000s — the IGT S2000, early WMS platforms, classic Bally multi-line video games. These machines are now at or past the 25-year threshold, qualifying for legal ownership in age-restricted states, and they represent an accessible entry point into collecting without the price tags of true antiques.

A well-maintained IGT S2000 from the mid-1990s plays great, looks authentic, and costs a fraction of a genuine antique. These are the machines we specialize in.

## What We Carry

Used Slot Shop specializes in commercial-era used casino slot machines from primarily the 1990s through the 2010s. We carry IGT, Bally, Aristocrat, WMS, and Konami machines in a rotating inventory of 334+ units, all inspected and reconditioned in Kingman, Arizona.

We do not typically carry pre-war mechanical antiques — those machines require specialized restoration expertise and trade primarily through antique dealers and auction houses. What we do carry is a comprehensive selection of machines spanning the early electronic and modern video eras, including machines that now qualify as vintage under the 25-year rule.

If you are looking for a machine with authenticity, proven reliability, and a realistic price point — the kind of machine that has a genuine casino floor history behind it — that is our wheelhouse.

[Browse our current inventory](/shop) or [contact us](/contact) to discuss what you are looking for. We are happy to help you find a machine that fits your collection, your space, and your budget.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Bally Slot Machines for Home: Game Maker, Alpha, and Which Model Is Right for You
excerpt: Bally has been building casino-quality gaming machines for nearly a century. Here is everything you need to know about the Game Maker, Alpha, and other Bally platforms before you buy for your home.
body:
When people think of slot machines, IGT comes to mind first. But ask any serious home collector what brand rounds out a game room and the answer is almost always Bally. In 34 years of selling machines, we have watched Bally maintain a loyal following among buyers who want something a cut above the standard. The build quality is excellent. The game themes are memorable. And the Game Maker multi-game platform offers a variety that no other machine quite matches.

Here is everything you need to know about Bally slot machines before you buy.

## A Brief History of Bally

Bally Manufacturing has been in the coin-operated machine business since the 1930s, making it one of the oldest gaming companies in America. They helped define what a slot machine looks and feels like. Over the decades the company went through various corporate changes and is now part of Scientific Games, which also owns the Williams (WMS) gaming brand. Both Bally and Williams machines are sold under the Scientific Games umbrella today.

What that history means for home buyers is that Bally machines benefit from decades of engineering refinement. The cabinets are solid. The electronics are well-documented. Parts are available. And the game libraries include some of the most entertaining themes ever built into a casino machine.

## The Two Bally Product Lines That Matter for Home Buyers

Bally's lineup for the home market breaks down into two primary categories: the **Game Maker** series (multi-game platforms) and the **Alpha** series (dedicated video slot cabinets). Understanding the difference is the first step in choosing the right machine.

### The Bally Game Maker: Variety in a Single Cabinet

The Game Maker is Bally's answer to the question every game room owner eventually asks: do I buy one machine with one game, or do I find something with more variety? The Game Maker solves the problem elegantly by putting multiple games into a single cabinet accessible from a menu.

![IGT Game King multi-game video poker machine](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/687a200ece9e185839e98449.jpeg)

The Game Maker platform has gone through several generations. Here is what you will encounter in the used market.

#### Game Maker V7200

This is one of the most common Game Maker models in the home market. The V7200 is a space-saving upright cabinet that holds up to 10 games in a single machine. The game selection typically includes a mix of video poker, video slots, keno, and blackjack depending on the configuration. The cabinet is compact enough to fit in tight spaces without giving up the full casino feel.

The V7200 fits the 25-year rule in most age-restricted states since this platform was produced in the late 1990s and early 2000s. If you are in California, New York, Florida, or any other 25-year-rule state, a V7200 from 2001 or earlier qualifies.

#### Game Maker HD

The Game Maker HD is a more modern multi-game platform with an expanded game library of over 25 games selectable from the cabinet menu. It is multi-denominational, accepting quarter, 50-cent, and one-dollar denominations, and takes bills from $1 through $100.

The HD platform offers a noticeably improved visual presentation over earlier Game Maker models, with sharper displays and more polished game software. The game mix includes video poker, video slots, keno, and blackjack.

Note on age restriction compliance: the Game Maker HD is a newer platform and some versions may not meet the 25-year requirement depending on manufacture date. If you are in an age-restricted state, verify the specific machine's year before purchasing. We check every machine's manufacture date and provide documentation with every sale.

### The Bally Alpha Series: Dedicated Video Slots

If the Game Maker is about variety, the Alpha series is about depth. These are dedicated video slot cabinets — single-platform machines with a focused game experience and premium visual presentation. The Alpha line is what you buy when you want a showpiece machine that looks like it just walked off the floor of a major Las Vegas casino.

#### Alpha 1 Series

The Alpha 1 includes several cabinet configurations. The S9000 is an upright cabinet. The V32 is a 32-inch display configuration offering a large, bright video screen. These earlier Alpha machines were produced in the late 1990s and early 2000s.

For home buyers in age-restricted states, Alpha 1 machines from 2001 and earlier qualify under the 25-year rule. This is where the Alpha series becomes accessible for the broadest audience — many of the most popular Alpha 1 game themes were produced in that window.

#### Alpha 2 Series

The Alpha 2 represents a significant jump in visual quality and player experience. The flagship configuration is the **Alpha 2 Pro V22/22**, which features two 22-inch high-definition LCD screens stacked vertically — one for the main game display and one for a dynamic button panel that Bally calls the iDeck. The iDeck replaces the traditional physical button panel with a touch-sensitive LCD, which opens up the ability to display animated prompts, secondary information, and interactive features.

Other Alpha 2 configurations include the V27/27 (27-inch screens) and the Curve, which features a distinctive curved cabinet design.

Alpha 2 machines are impressive. They look genuinely modern even years after their production and hold up aesthetically better than many machines of their era. The dual-screen setup gives games room for cinematic bonus sequences and elaborate second-screen features.

**Age restriction note:** Alpha 2 machines are a newer platform. Many may not yet meet the 25-year threshold depending on manufacture date. Confirm compliance before ordering if you are in an age-restricted state.

## Bally vs. IGT: Which Brand Is Right for You?

This is the question we get most often from buyers comparing brands. Here is an honest assessment from 34 years of selling both.

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

**Choose IGT if:**
- You want a mechanical reel machine (Bally's home-market lineup is primarily video)
- Parts availability is your top priority (IGT S2000 has the broadest parts ecosystem in the industry)
- You want the most recognizable classic casino themes (Double Diamond, Red White and Blue)
- Budget is your primary consideration

**Choose Bally if:**
- You want a multi-game machine with video poker, slots, keno, and blackjack under one roof
- Visual presentation matters to you — Bally cabinets tend to have more refined aesthetics
- You are building a game room and want variety from a single machine
- You want something that looks and feels slightly different from the IGT machines that dominate the market

Many of our customers end up buying one of each. An IGT S2000 as the mechanical reel classic and a Bally Game Maker for video variety is a combination we recommend regularly.

## Price Ranges for Bally Machines

Bally machines typically sell for more than comparable IGT platforms, which reflects the higher build quality and more polished presentation. Here is what to expect.

**Entry-level Bally (older Game Maker or Alpha 1 models):** $1,200 to $1,800. These are fully functional machines in the earlier generations. Game Maker V7200 machines typically fall here.

**Mid-range Bally (Game Maker HD, Alpha 2 base configurations):** $1,800 to $2,800. This is where you get the expanded game libraries and significantly improved display quality.

**Premium Bally (Alpha 2 Pro dual-screen, later platforms):** $2,800 to $4,000 and up. These are showpiece machines with dual HD screens, modern game software, and the look of a contemporary casino floor.

Shipping adds $250 to $600 depending on your distance from Kingman, Arizona. Every machine comes with our [1-year warranty](/warranty) and free lifetime tech support.

## Legal Compliance: What Bally Machines Qualify in Restricted States?

For buyers in the 28 states with the 25-year rule, the question is straightforward: any Bally machine manufactured in 2001 or earlier qualifies. This covers the full Game Maker V7200 lineup and all Alpha 1 machines produced through that year.

We verify manufacture dates on every machine and provide documentation showing the machine meets the age requirement. Our [state-by-state legal guide](/blog/is-it-legal-to-own-a-slot-machine-state-by-state-guide) covers the specifics for all 50 states.

## Maintenance and Service

Bally machines are more complex than mechanical IGT reel machines, and they benefit from an experienced tech when issues arise. The good news is that Bally's documentation is thorough and parts are widely available.

Common maintenance items for Bally machines:
- **Display issues:** LCD screens on Alpha machines occasionally need backlight or inverter replacement. Straightforward repair with parts available.
- **Touch panel calibration (iDeck):** The iDeck touch panel can drift on older machines. Recalibration is software-based and usually done through the service menu.
- **Bill validator service:** Standard across most platforms. Clean or rebuild the belts and sensors for reliable bill acceptance.
- **Software errors:** Bally machines can display configuration errors that require a setup card to clear. We walk customers through this over the phone when needed.

We provide [free lifetime tech support](/warranty) on every Bally machine we sell. Call us with any issue and we will walk you through it.

## What to Look For When Buying a Bally Machine

### Verify the Specific Model

Bally produced a wide range of cabinets, and the model determines compatibility for parts, games, and upgrades. Know exactly what you are buying. The model information appears on the cabinet serial plate.

### Check the Monitor Quality

Alpha series machines have LCD screens that can develop dead pixels, backlight unevenness, or color issues over time. Ask for photos or video of the screen in operation before purchasing. A dimming backlight is a repair item — budget for it if you see any uneven brightness.

### Confirm the Game Configuration

Game Maker machines should have a specific game set installed. Verify what games are available on the machine you are buying. Some machines have had games removed or reconfigured.

### Inspect the iDeck (if Alpha 2)

The touch-sensitive button panel on Alpha 2 machines should respond accurately to touch across its entire surface. Dead zones or unresponsive areas indicate a panel that needs service.

### Ask About the Denomination and Credit Configuration

Multi-denominational Game Maker machines can be set to various denomination configurations. For home use, we configure them for the denomination that makes the most sense for your use case. Most customers play in free play mode — we set that up before shipping.

## The Bottom Line on Bally

Bally machines earn their higher price point. The Game Maker series offers unmatched variety for buyers who want more than one game without buying multiple machines. The Alpha series delivers a presentation quality that holds up against anything on the used market. And the Bally brand history means you are buying from a manufacturer with decades of engineering behind every component.

We carry Bally machines in rotating inventory at our Kingman, Arizona showroom. [Browse our current selection](/shop) or [contact us](/contact) to discuss what you are looking for. If we have what you want, we can usually ship within a few days.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: WMS Bluebird Slot Machines: A Home Buyer's Guide to Wizard of Oz, Zeus, and More
excerpt: WMS Gaming built some of the most creative slot machines ever made. Here is everything home buyers need to know about the Bluebird platform, the best games, and how to choose the right model.
body:
If you want a slot machine with bonus features that actually make you feel something — a spinning wheel, a second screen, a free-spin run that builds suspense — then WMS Gaming machines belong on your shortlist. In our 34 years in this business, WMS stands out as the manufacturer that pushed the boundaries of what a slot machine can do. Their Bluebird platform is where some of the most memorable games in casino history were born.

This guide covers everything you need to know about WMS Bluebird machines for home use: the different cabinet versions, the most popular games, pricing, and what to look for when buying.

## Who Is WMS Gaming?

WMS Gaming (originally Williams Gaming, a division of Williams Electronics) is an American slot machine manufacturer that built a reputation for innovative game design and high-quality cabinet construction. WMS is now part of Scientific Games, which also owns the Bally gaming brand, meaning both brands share parts of the same corporate infrastructure today.

What WMS is known for: they were among the first manufacturers to introduce second-screen bonus rounds, where triggering a bonus takes the game to a completely different display with interactive elements. They also pioneered licensed game themes, bringing popular entertainment properties into the casino in an authentic way. The Wizard of Oz, Monopoly, and other licensed themes gave WMS games a cultural resonance that players recognized immediately.

## The Bluebird Cabinet Family

The Bluebird is WMS's defining cabinet platform. There are several generations, and understanding the differences matters when you are buying.

### Bluebird 1 (BB1)

The original Bluebird cabinet raised the bar for slot machine design when it was introduced. Key specifications:

- **Main display:** 18-inch LCD screen
- **Sound:** Bose speaker system
- **Cabinet design:** Smooth rounded doors, unlike the boxy rectangular cabinets that dominated before it
- **Bonus features:** Full support for second-screen bonus rounds

The BB1 is where iconic WMS games like Wizard of Oz, the original Zeus, and many others live. The 18-inch screen is smaller than later models but the image quality is sharp and the Bose sound makes the experience genuinely immersive.

For home buyers in age-restricted states, BB1 machines from 2001 or earlier qualify under the 25-year rule. Since the Bluebird 1 was produced starting in the early 2000s, eligible machines do exist in the used market. Verify manufacture dates carefully — this is something we handle on every order.

Refurbished BB1 pricing from a dealer: typically $1,200 to $1,800 depending on game title and condition.

### Bluebird 2 (BB2)

The BB2 represented a significant upgrade in visual technology and ergonomics.

- **Main display:** Larger primary screen plus a secondary OLED button deck (the iDeck equivalent)
- **Sound:** Bose speaker system retained
- **Cabinet design:** Slimmer, more modern profile compared to the BB1
- **Game library:** Expanded library including Wizard of Oz Ruby Slippers, Zeus II, and many other titles

The BB2's dual-screen setup unlocks additional game features. Games designed for the BB2 use the lower screen for interactive elements, expanded paytables, or bonus game progression.

**Age restriction note:** The BB2 is a newer platform and many machines will not meet the 25-year threshold yet depending on manufacture year. If you are in an age-restricted state, confirm the specific machine's manufacture date before purchasing. We verify this on every sale.

Refurbished BB2 pricing from a dealer: typically $1,500 to $2,500 depending on configuration and game title.

### Bluebird XD, Bluebird 3, and Blade

These later WMS platforms feature progressively larger screens, more advanced electronics, and newer software. They are impressive machines but less common in the home market and more likely to have age-restriction issues depending on manufacture date. Ask us if you are interested in these platforms and we will verify compliance for your state.

## The Best WMS Games for Home Use

Game selection is where WMS truly separates itself. Here are the titles home buyers ask about most.

### Wizard of Oz (Bluebird 1)

This is the game that put WMS on the map in the home market. The licensing deal with the Wizard of Oz estate produced one of the most beloved slot machine games ever made.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

Game features:
- Up to 30 paylines
- Multi-denomination
- **Road to Emerald City bonus:** A progressive bonus round where you advance along the Yellow Brick Road
- **Flying Monkey bonus:** Awards free spins
- **Gilda the Good Witch feature:** Makes certain reels wild
- **Oz Pick feature:** An interactive pick bonus that can multiply the line bet up to 25 times

The variety of bonus features means no two sessions feel the same. The combination of a recognizable, beloved theme with genuinely fun bonus rounds is what made this game a casino floor staple for years.

### Wizard of Oz Ruby Slippers (Bluebird 2)

The dual-screen BB2 version of the Wizard of Oz expands on the original with bonuses designed for the second screen:

- **Ruby Slipper Multiplier:** Clicking shoes to reveal multipliers
- **Find the Broom Bonus:** Interactive pick game on the lower screen
- **Emerald City Free Spin Bonus:** Extended free spin sequence
- **Crystal Ball Bonus:** Reveal-style pick bonus

If you want the full Wizard of Oz experience with maximum bonus variety, the Ruby Slippers version on a BB2 is the definitive edition.

### Zeus

Zeus is one of WMS's most popular original themes — not licensed, but developed in-house with a mythology angle that resonates with players. The king of the gods theme features dramatic visuals and a free-spin bonus that can produce memorable runs.

Zeus is available across multiple Bluebird generations. The BB2 version (Zeus II) adds expanded features over the original. Both are popular in home game rooms, and Zeus regularly comes up when customers tell us what game they want.

### Other Notable WMS Titles

The WMS Bluebird library is extensive. Other popular home titles include various licensed and original themes. When browsing our inventory, ask about specific games — we can usually match you with the title you want.

## Why WMS Stands Out for Home Game Rooms

Several things set WMS machines apart from IGT and Bally options at similar price points.

**Best bonus rounds in the industry.** WMS defined the second-screen bonus concept. The interactive elements, where you are picking characters or clicking items on a different screen, create genuine engagement that pure reel spinning cannot match.

**Licensed themes with cultural weight.** Wizard of Oz, Monopoly, and other licensed WMS games bring familiar, beloved content into the casino experience. When guests see a Wizard of Oz machine in your game room, they know immediately what it is and want to play it.

**The Bose sound system.** Both BB1 and BB2 feature Bose speakers. The difference in audio quality compared to non-Bose machines is noticeable. The sound design on WMS games takes full advantage of it.

**Visual design quality.** The Bluebird cabinet raised industry standards for slot machine aesthetics. The smooth rounded doors and clean lines look good in a home setting, more like modern electronics than traditional casino floor equipment.

## What to Look For When Buying a WMS Bluebird

### Identify the Cabinet Generation

BB1 and BB2 look different and have different capabilities. The BB2 has the dual-screen setup; the BB1 has a single display. Make sure you are buying the version of the game you want, since some titles are available on both platforms with different feature sets.

### Check the Screen

LCD screens on WMS machines can develop dead pixels or backlight dimming over time. Ask for photos or video of the machine running. Any screen issues should be disclosed.

### Verify Game Software

WMS machines use a CF Clear Card system for setup and game management. Confirm what game is installed and that the software is verified functional. On BB1 and BB2 machines, changing games requires the correct Clear Card for the CPU version, and the game matrix needs to match or you will see errors.

### Confirm Manufacture Date

This matters most if you are in an age-restricted state. BB1 machines from 2001 or earlier qualify. We provide manufacture date documentation with every sale.

### Test the Bonus Features

If you can, watch a video of the specific machine triggering its bonus round. WMS machines are worth the price specifically because of those bonus features — confirm they work before buying.

## Pricing Summary

| Model | Refurbished Price Range |
|---|---|
| Bluebird 1 (BB1) | $1,200 – $1,800 |
| Bluebird 2 (BB2) | $1,500 – $2,500 |
| Bluebird XD / BB3 | $2,000 – $3,500 |

Shipping from Kingman, Arizona adds $250 to $600 depending on distance. All machines include our [1-year warranty](/warranty) and free lifetime tech support.

## Game-Swapping on WMS Machines

One question we hear often: can I change the game on my WMS machine later?

The short answer is yes, but with important caveats. WMS machines use a Clear Card system to reset the machine and install new software. Each Bluebird generation (BB1, BB2, BB3) uses a specific Clear Card for its CPU version. Once cleared, you can load new game software — but the game configuration matrix must be correct for your specific machine's hardware. Mismatches produce error codes that prevent operation.

For most home owners, game swapping is a project that requires some technical comfort and sourcing of the right software. If you want to do it, we can advise you through the process. If you want a specific game and you want it to just work, buy the machine with that game already installed — which is what we do before shipping.

## Legal Considerations

WMS Bluebird machines for home use are subject to the same laws as other slot machines. In the 13 states with no age restrictions, any Bluebird model is fine. In the 28 states with the 25-year rule, verify your machine's manufacture date. Our [state legal guide](/blog/is-it-legal-to-own-a-slot-machine-state-by-state-guide) covers all 50 states.

In all legal states, these machines are for personal entertainment only. Free play mode — which lets you add credits with a button press rather than inserting money — is how most home owners configure them, and we set this up before shipping.

## The Bottom Line

WMS Bluebird machines deliver an experience that pure reel-spinner fans might not expect. The bonus rounds are genuinely fun. The licensed themes create immediate recognition. The Bose audio makes every spin more immersive. If you want more than spinning reels — if you want a machine that creates memorable moments with second-screen features and interactive bonuses — WMS is the brand.

[Browse our current WMS inventory](/shop) or [contact us directly](/contact) to ask about specific titles. We regularly stock Wizard of Oz, Zeus, and other popular WMS games and can tell you what is available for immediate shipment.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: California Slot Machine Laws: Can You Own One at Home? (2026 Guide)
excerpt: California has strict rules about slot machine ownership — but there is a legal path for residents who want a machine at home. A 34-year dealer explains exactly what qualifies and what to know before you buy.
body:
California is the most populous state in the country and one of our largest markets. We ship machines to California buyers regularly — but it requires more care than shipping to, say, Arizona or Nevada. California's slot machine laws are structured differently than most states, and if you do not understand the details, you could end up buying a machine that creates legal risk rather than entertainment.

This guide explains California's slot machine laws as they stand in 2026, what machines qualify for home ownership, and how to buy with confidence. This is not legal advice — if you have specific legal questions, consult a California attorney. But after 34 years of shipping machines to buyers across the country, including thousands of California shipments, we know this territory well.

## The Basic Rule: Slot Machines Are Generally Prohibited

California law treats slot machines as gambling devices. Possession of a slot machine in California is generally prohibited under the California Penal Code. This is the starting point and it is more restrictive than the baseline law in many other states.

However, California law includes a significant exception, and that exception is what makes home ownership possible for California residents.

## The Antique Machine Exception

California law provides an **affirmative defense** to prosecution for possessing a slot machine if the machine meets two conditions:

1. **The machine is over 25 years old** (classified as an antique slot machine under California law)
2. **The machine is not operated for gambling purposes**

This is an important legal distinction that buyers need to understand. An affirmative defense is different from explicit permission. It means that if you are ever prosecuted for possessing a slot machine, you can assert these facts as a defense — but you bear the burden of proving them. In practice, prosecutions of private individuals for possessing antique slot machines for personal entertainment are extremely rare, but the legal structure matters.

**What "over 25 years old" means in 2026:** Any machine manufactured in 2001 or earlier meets the age threshold as of 2026.

**What "not operated for gambling purposes" means:** The machine is for personal entertainment. You are not charging others to play, offering real payouts as a commercial gambling operation, or running it as any form of business. This is consistent with how all home slot machine owners in every state should operate their machines anyway.

## What Machines Qualify in California Right Now

As of 2026, the following categories of machines meet the 25-year age threshold:

### IGT S2000 (1996–2001)

This is the most popular home slot machine in America and it is fully eligible in California. The S2000 platform was introduced in 1996 and produced through the early 2000s. Every S2000 manufactured through 2001 is now over 25 years old.

The S2000 comes in classic themes including Double Diamond, Red White and Blue, Triple Diamond, Blazing 7s, Five Times Pay, Wild Cherry, and others. This is the definitive California-legal home slot machine — mechanically robust, extensively documented, with parts available and full lifetime tech support from us.

### IGT S-Plus

The predecessor to the S2000, the S-Plus was produced through the mid-to-late 1990s. All S-Plus machines are well past the 25-year mark. They are simpler machines than the S2000 with fewer game options, but reliable and affordable — typically $750 to $1,100 in our inventory.

### Bally Game Maker (late 1990s–early 2000s)

Bally's multi-game platform from this era — including the Game Maker V7200 and similar models — qualifies for California buyers when manufactured in 2001 or earlier. These machines offer video poker, slots, keno, and blackjack in one cabinet, making them excellent value for California buyers who want game variety.

### Early Williams (WMS) Bluebird Machines

WMS Bluebird 1 machines manufactured in 2001 or earlier qualify. Verify the specific manufacture date — this matters when ordering for California.

### Mechanical Reel Machines from the 1970s, 1980s, and 1990s

All older mechanical machines — IGT's earlier platforms, vintage Bally electromechanical machines, and genuine antique mechanical machines from the 1960s and earlier — are well past the 25-year threshold. If anything, California's antique exception was designed with true antique machines in mind, and these qualify most unambiguously.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine - Used Slot Shop inventory](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

## What Does NOT Qualify in California

Machines manufactured in 2002 or later do not currently meet the 25-year age threshold. This includes:

- Later-model IGT S2000 machines (some were produced into the mid-2000s)
- WMS Bluebird 2 machines (produced from the mid-2000s onward)
- Bally Alpha series machines from 2002 and later
- Any modern video slot with a manufacture date after 2001

The threshold advances each year. By 2027, machines from 2002 will qualify. By 2028, 2003 machines will qualify. The 25-year window keeps opening.

We track this carefully. If you ask about a specific machine for a California shipment, we verify the manufacture date and will not ship a non-compliant machine to you.

## How We Handle California Orders

Every California order goes through our compliance verification process:

1. **Manufacture date confirmation:** We check the serial number and manufacturer's plate to confirm the exact build date.
2. **Certificate of age:** We provide written documentation showing the machine's manufacture date for your records. Keep this with the machine.
3. **Free play configuration:** We configure every machine for California shipments in free play mode, which eliminates any coin-in gambling mechanism and makes the personal entertainment purpose unambiguous.
4. **Documentation package:** California buyers receive full documentation including our bill of sale and manufacture certificate.

This process costs you nothing extra. It is part of how we do business with age-restricted states, and California gets extra attention because of the affirmative defense structure.

## Local Laws: Check Your City and County

State law is one layer. California has 58 counties and hundreds of municipalities, and some have adopted local ordinances that may be more or less restrictive than state law. This is uncommon, but worth a quick check.

The easiest approach is to call your city clerk or county counsel's office and ask if there are any local ordinances regarding private possession of antique slot machines. Most will tell you it is governed by state law only. But if you live in a jurisdiction with a specific history of gambling enforcement, the extra check is worth the five minutes.

## HOA Considerations

If you live in a homeowners association community, your CC&Rs may restrict certain categories of items visible from outside or prohibit specific types of equipment. HOA rules are contractual rather than criminal — but violating them can result in fines or neighbor complaints.

Keep your machine inside, away from windows and doors where it would be visible from the street or common areas, and most HOAs will never know or care.

## Free Play Mode: The Best Configuration for California

We always recommend free play mode for California buyers. In free play mode, the machine adds credits via a button press rather than coin insertion or bill acceptance. There is no real money changing hands, no gambling payout mechanism in active use, and the personal entertainment nature of the machine is unmistakable.

**How free play works:**
- Press the button to add credits (typically 100 credits at a time)
- Spin and play normally
- Credits build up or decrease with outcomes just as in a casino
- At the end, simply add more credits with the button — no coins, no bills, no payout

Most California buyers love free play mode for practical reasons too: no coin handling, no need to stock tokens, and it is quieter without the hopper dropping coins on payouts.

## Documentation: Keep It With the Machine

We recommend maintaining a simple file with your machine containing:

- Our bill of sale showing the purchase date and machine description
- The manufacture date certificate we provide
- Any correspondence with us regarding the machine's specifications

This is not legally required, but having documentation readily available makes your affirmative defense straightforward if you were ever asked about it. It also documents the machine's provenance for resale or insurance purposes.

## Frequently Asked Questions from California Buyers

### Is this legal to ship to California?

Yes, for machines that meet the 25-year age requirement and will be used for personal entertainment. Interstate shipment of legal property is not restricted. What matters is whether possession in California is legal, and for qualifying antique machines used for personal entertainment, the affirmative defense applies.

### Can I have the machine paid out in coins for a more authentic experience?

You can configure the machine for coin operation. The legal requirement is that the machine not be used for commercial gambling. Playing with coins at home for personal entertainment is different from operating a gambling device for profit. Most California buyers choose free play mode for simplicity, but coin operation is an option.

### What if I want a specific newer machine that does not qualify yet?

You have two options. Wait — the 25-year window advances each year, so more machines become eligible every January. Or consider moving the purchase to a machine that already qualifies, of which we have many excellent options.

### What is the difference between this and the state-by-state legal guide on your blog?

Our [state-by-state guide](/blog/is-it-legal-to-own-a-slot-machine-state-by-state-guide) covers all 50 states at a summary level. This guide goes deeper on California's specific legal structure, the affirmative defense framework, and the documentation process. If California is your state, both are worth reading.

### How do I prove the machine is over 25 years old if someone asks?

The machine has a manufacturer's plate with a serial number and build date. We provide a certificate with the purchase documentation. Major manufacturers including IGT, Bally, and Williams maintain production records. Between the machine's own identification and our documentation, establishing the manufacture date is straightforward.

### Do you ship to all parts of California?

Yes. We ship to the Los Angeles area, San Francisco Bay Area, San Diego, Sacramento, and all other California locations via LTL freight with liftgate delivery. Transit time from Kingman, Arizona is typically 2 to 4 business days.

![Refurbished IGT S2000 ready to ship from our warehouse](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879d05b9227094875c5d253.jpeg)

## The Best Machines to Buy as a California Resident

Given the 25-year requirement and the need for manufacture date documentation, here is what we most often recommend to California buyers.

**Best overall:** IGT S2000 in a popular theme (Double Diamond, Red White and Blue, Triple Diamond). These are fully verified for California compliance, thoroughly refurbished, and backed by the broadest parts ecosystem in the industry. Price range: $995 to $1,795.

**Best for variety:** Bally Game Maker V7200 from the late 1990s to 2001. A multi-game machine with poker, slots, keno, and blackjack gives California buyers variety without the complication of buying multiple machines. Price range: $1,400 to $1,900.

**Best for the collector:** Early mechanical reel machines from the 1980s or 1990s. These have no age qualification concerns — they are obviously antique. Great conversation pieces with authentic mechanical feel. Price range varies widely.

## Contact Us Before You Order

If you are a California buyer and you are not sure which machine is right for you, or if you want to confirm that a specific machine in our inventory meets the California requirements, [contact us](/contact) before placing your order. We will verify compliance, answer your questions, and make sure you get a machine you can enjoy without concern.

We handle California shipments every week. It is not complicated once you understand the framework, and we have done this long enough that we can make the process straightforward for you.
author: Used Slot Shop

title: IGT Double Diamond Slot Machine: The Complete Home Buyer's Guide
excerpt: The Double Diamond is the bestselling home slot machine in America — but there are three distinct versions with key differences. A 34-year dealer explains every variant, what they cost, and how to pick the right one.
body:
We have been selling used slot machines since 1992, and one question comes up more than any other: *"Do you have a Double Diamond?"*

The IGT Double Diamond is not just the most recognizable slot machine on the planet — it is consistently the bestselling used slot machine for home buyers. If you walk into almost any casino in America, you will see it on the floor. And when those casinos retire their machines, Double Diamonds land in homes across the country because the game is timeless and the machines are built to last.

This guide covers every version of the Double Diamond available for home purchase, what you need to know before you buy, and how to pick the right variant for your game room.

## Why the Double Diamond Dominates Home Sales

Three things set the Double Diamond apart from every other machine on the market.

**Simplicity.** Three reels. Three paylines. The diamond symbol doubles your win, and two diamonds multiplies it further. That is the whole game. There is no video bonus, no complicated feature. You pull the handle (or press spin), the reels stop, and you either win or you do not. It is as pure as slot machine play gets.

**Build quality.** The IGT S2000 cabinet — the platform that carries the Double Diamond game — was built for continuous commercial casino use. These machines ran 24 hours a day, seven days a week. They are mechanical, heavy (approximately 250 pounds), and made with industrial-grade components. A properly maintained S2000 will outlast any modern video terminal.

**Universal appeal.** Virtually everyone recognizes the Double Diamond. When you have guests over, no explanation is needed. Everyone wants to spin it.

## The Three Double Diamond Versions

Not all Double Diamond machines are the same. There are three primary variants you will encounter, and the differences matter for buyers.

### Standard Double Diamond

The original. Three reels, classic symbol set: diamonds, bars, 7s, and cherries. The Double Diamond symbol acts as a wild that doubles any win it contributes to. Two Double Diamond symbols on a payline multiplies the win four times.

The Standard Double Diamond runs on the IGT S2000 platform, introduced in the mid-1990s. It accepts $1 through $100 bills and operates as a coinless TITO machine — it reads your bill, adds credits, and prints a ticket on payout rather than dispensing coins. Coin conversion is available at additional cost, but most home buyers prefer the cleaner coinless setup.

**Best for:** First-time buyers who want the classic casino experience without complexity.

### Double Diamond Deluxe

The Deluxe version adds one feature that regular players love: the **nudge**. If a Double Diamond symbol stops just above or just below the center payline with its point aimed at the line, the reel nudges it onto the payline. This creates a mechanical tension that the standard version does not have — near misses that feel genuinely suspenseful.

The Deluxe also has a slightly different reel strip layout and a round-top cabinet that distinguishes it from the standard flat-top S2000. Physically, it is the same weight and dimensions; the difference is the cabinet shape and the game behavior.

**Best for:** Buyers who have played the standard Double Diamond and want more mechanical drama.

### Triple Double Diamond

The Triple Double Diamond introduces a stacked multiplier mechanic. Each Diamond symbol on an active payline carries a multiplier value. One Diamond doubles the win. Two Diamonds multiply by four. Three Diamonds can multiply significantly. The Diamond symbols also substitute for other symbols except themselves, meaning they create more wins and multiply those wins simultaneously.

The Triple Double Diamond is the highest-volatility of the three versions — less frequent wins, but when they hit, they hit harder. The cabinet looks similar to the standard S2000 but with different glass and reels.

**Best for:** Buyers who want more action per spin and do not mind longer dry streaks in exchange for bigger pay moments.

![IGT S2000 Double Diamond slot machine — classic three-reel mechanical](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/68753b135742b18163c5ed43.jpeg)

## Specifications: What to Expect When It Arrives

All IGT S2000 Double Diamond machines share these core specifications:

**Dimensions:** Approximately 52 inches tall (add 4–6 inches for the top candle), 23 inches wide, 22 inches deep. Taller than you might expect — measure your ceiling clearance, especially in basements with beams or ductwork.

**Weight:** Approximately 250 pounds. Two people and an appliance dolly are the right approach. See our [slot machine moving guide](/blog/how-to-move-a-slot-machine-into-your-home) for full setup instructions.

**Power:** Standard 110V household current. Plug it into a standard wall outlet. A surge protector is strongly recommended — these machines have sensitive computer boards.

**Bill Validator:** Accepts $1 through $100 bills. Machines arrive from casinos in coinless TITO configuration — the machine prints a ticket representing your credits rather than dispensing physical coins. Cleaner, quieter, and simpler for home use.

**Coin Conversion:** We can convert any S2000 to accept quarters or tokens at additional cost. Most home buyers who want an authentic coin experience choose tokens — you purchase a bag once and reuse them indefinitely.

**Free Play Mode:** All our machines can be configured for free play, where you add credits with a button press rather than inserting money. This is the most popular configuration for home game rooms.

## What Does a Double Diamond Cost?

Used IGT S2000 Double Diamond machines typically range from **$995 to $1,795** depending on condition, variant, and configuration. Key factors:

- **Variant:** Standard Double Diamonds are generally more affordable than Deluxe or Triple versions.
- **Condition:** Fully refurbished machines with new harnesses, tested boards, and cosmetic restoration command higher prices than as-is units.
- **Configuration:** Coin conversion, custom coin door treatment, and free play setup may add cost.
- **Cabinet style:** Round-top Deluxe cabinets sometimes carry a small premium over flat-top standard cabinets.

All Double Diamonds in our inventory at [Used Slot Shop](/shop) come with a **one-year warranty** and free lifetime tech support. For a machine in the $1,000–$1,500 range, that support has real value — a single service call from a local technician, if you could find one, would easily cost $200 or more.

## Legal Status: Is a Double Diamond Legal to Own?

For most buyers in most states, yes. The IGT S2000 Double Diamond was manufactured from the mid-1990s through the early 2000s. As of 2026, every machine in this range is at least 25 years old.

Many states allow private ownership of slot machines manufactured 25 or more years ago, provided they are not operated for commercial gambling. The S2000 comfortably clears the age threshold in every state that uses one.

Our [complete state-by-state legality guide](/blog/is-it-legal-to-own-a-slot-machine-state-by-state-guide) covers all 50 states. If you have any question about your state, read that guide first, or [contact us](/contact) directly — we verify legality before every order.

## Standard Double Diamond vs. Newer Video Machines

Buyers sometimes ask whether they should consider a newer video slot instead of the S2000. Here is our honest assessment after 34 years in the business.

**The S2000 advantages:**
- Mechanical reels feel fundamentally different from video. Players who grew up on mechanical machines are not satisfied by video substitutes.
- Simpler mechanics means more reliability for home ownership.
- Fully 25+ years old, broadly legal across more states.
- Well-documented platform with abundant parts availability.

**When a video slot makes more sense:**
- If you want bonus rounds, free spin features, and modern game themes, the S2000 does not offer those.
- If multiple players with different preferences will use the machine, a [multi-game video terminal](/blog/multi-game-conversion-kits-turn-one-slot-machine-into-50-games) may work better.

For the Double Diamond, the appeal is the purity. No features. Just the reels.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### How long do Double Diamond machines last?

The IGT S2000 is a commercial-grade machine built for continuous use. Properly maintained, these machines run for decades. Major failure points are rare — most service calls involve the bill validator or a reel motor, both of which are serviceable.

### Can I adjust the denomination (quarter, dollar, etc.)?

Yes. Denomination is controlled by the machine's software settings. We configure your machine to your preferred denomination before shipping.

### Is the handle functional?

Yes. S2000 machines came from casinos with fully functional spin handles. Pulling the handle triggers the reels just like pressing the spin button.

### Can the machine pay out real money?

It can print a TITO ticket or drop coins into a hopper. At home, most buyers use free play mode so no real money is involved — credits are purely for entertainment.

### Do you ship Double Diamonds to all states?

We ship throughout the continental United States and verify legality before every order. [Contact us](/contact) with your state if you have any question about eligibility.

## Ready to Buy?

Browse our current Double Diamond inventory at [Used Slot Shop](/shop). If you want help picking between Standard, Deluxe, and Triple variants, or have specific configuration questions, [contact us](/contact). We have been placing Double Diamonds in homes since the machines were new.

See also: [The IGT S2000: America's Most Popular Home Slot Machine](/blog/igt-s2000-americas-most-popular-home-slot-machine) | [How Much Does a Used Slot Machine Cost?](/blog/how-much-does-a-slot-machine-cost-pricing-guide) | [How to Set Up Your Slot Machine at Home](/blog/how-to-set-up-your-slot-machine-at-home)
author: Used Slot Shop

title: Bally Blazing 7s Slot Machine for Sale: Home Buyer's Guide
excerpt: The Blazing 7s is one of the most recognizable mechanical slot machines ever made — and one of the best choices for a home game room. A 34-year dealer covers every version, specs, pricing, and how it compares to the Double Diamond.
body:
Ask any Bally slot machine dealer which model has sold the most units to home buyers over the past 30 years and the answer is almost always the same: Blazing 7s.

It is not the newest machine. It is not the most complicated. But the Blazing 7s is one of those slot machine games that casino players recognize on sight and want to own. Red sevens, classic bars, simple reels — the Blazing 7s is a mechanical slot distilled to its essence, and that simplicity is exactly why it works so well outside a casino.

After 34 years of selling used slot machines, we have placed more Blazing 7s units in homes, bars, and game rooms than we can count. This guide covers every version of the machine, what to expect from each, and how to decide which one is right for you.

## The Blazing 7s Story

Bally Technologies has been making the Blazing 7s game in one form or another since the 1970s. The game survived multiple generations of slot machine technology — from fully mechanical machines through electromechanical platforms and eventually onto the modern digital cabinet — because the core design is compelling.

The premise: sevens are the premium symbols that pay higher than bars, and when multiple 7s appear on a payline, the payouts increase significantly. A single seven pays modestly. Two sevens on a line pays more. Three sevens on the center payline is the top hit. It is an immediately intuitive pay structure that players learn in one spin.

The modern Blazing 7s machines available for home purchase run on the **Bally S9000 platform**, introduced in the 1990s. The S9000 is Bally's answer to the IGT S2000 — similar in cabinet size, similar in build quality, but with distinct design differences including a top LCD monitor on some configurations.

## The Blazing 7s Variants

Several distinct versions of the Blazing 7s exist in the used market, and they offer meaningfully different home experiences.

### Standard Blazing 7s (3-Reel)

The baseline Blazing 7s on the Bally S9000 is a three-reel, three-payline mechanical slot. No top monitor, no bonus feature — just the three reels, the 7s symbols, and the payout glass on the machine face. This is the version most commonly seen on casino floors through the 1990s and into the 2000s.

The Standard is the most affordable Blazing 7s variant and the cleanest in terms of home setup. No second monitor, a smaller overall footprint than the progressive configuration, and a game experience that needs no explanation.

**Best for:** Buyers who want the classic Blazing 7s experience without extras.

### 5-Reel Progressive (S9000 with Top Monitor)

This configuration includes a large top monitor displaying a progressive jackpot counter, and the game expands to five reels with more paylines and complex winning combinations — while retaining the same core 7s logic.

The top monitor adds approximately 12–15 inches of height to the machine and adds significant visual presence. The progressive jackpot display does not connect to any live network at home (it was active only when the machine was part of a casino bank), but the display still functions and can be set to a static value.

**Best for:** Buyers who want visual impact and a more casino-authentic setup in a dedicated game room.

### Video Blazing 7s

Later iterations moved the Blazing 7s game to a video reel format — reels appear on screen rather than spinning mechanically. Video versions typically include a bonus round feature and more elaborate graphics.

If you have played both and prefer mechanical, stick with the S9000. If you want bonus features and video graphics, the video version is worth considering.

![Bally S9000 slot machine — three-reel mechanical configuration](https://images.leadconnectorhq.com/image/f_webp/q_80/r_1200/u_https://assets.cdn.filesafe.space/hvLhtyPPvOhUoiGCUbE2/media/6879c448ce9e185957e88146.jpeg)

## Specifications: Bally S9000 Platform

Whether Standard or Progressive, the Bally S9000 cabinet shares these core specs:

**Dimensions:** Approximately 52 inches tall (base cabinet), 22 inches wide, 23 inches deep. The 5-Reel Progressive with top monitor adds 12–15 inches of height.

**Weight:** Approximately 250 pounds. Two people and a proper appliance dolly are essential. See our [slot machine moving guide](/blog/how-to-move-a-slot-machine-into-your-home) for practical setup advice.

**Power:** Standard 110V household outlet. A surge protector is recommended.

**Bill Validator:** Accepts $1 through $100 bills. Machines arrive in coinless TITO configuration — bill-in, ticket-out.

**Coin Conversion:** Available at additional cost. Most home buyers opt for token play or free play mode.

**Free Play Mode:** We configure machines for free play before shipping if requested — a button press adds credits, no money involved.

## How Blazing 7s Compares to Double Diamond

These are the two most popular mechanical three-reel machines for home buyers, and the comparison comes up constantly. Here is our honest take:

**Theme:** Double Diamond is elegant and understated. Blazing 7s is bold — red sevens, bright colors, Vegas-classic aesthetics. This is often a gut-feeling preference.

**Game mechanics:** Double Diamond uses a diamond wild/multiplier (diamond doubles wins, two diamonds multiplies by four). Blazing 7s uses a different payout structure where the sevens pay at escalating rates based on how many appear on a payline. The games feel different in play.

**Cabinet:** The standard IGT S2000 layout differs slightly from the Bally S9000. Some players have a strong preference from years of casino experience with one machine.

**Progressive option:** The 5-Reel Progressive is unique to Blazing 7s in this class — the S2000 Double Diamond does not have an equivalent progressive cabinet with top monitor. If you want the progressive look, Blazing 7s is your machine.

Many buyers who ask us for *one* machine end up with both. They serve different moods and work well together in a game room.

## What Does a Blazing 7s Cost?

Used Bally S9000 Blazing 7s machines in refurbished condition typically range from **$995 to $1,895** depending on variant and configuration:

- **Standard 3-reel:** $995–$1,495 refurbished
- **5-Reel Progressive with top monitor:** $1,395–$1,895 refurbished

The progressive commands a premium because of the additional hardware (top monitor, progressive display electronics) and the more impressive visual presentation.

All Blazing 7s machines we sell include a **one-year warranty** and free lifetime tech support. Browse available inventory at [Used Slot Shop](/shop).

## Legal Status

Bally S9000 Blazing 7s machines in the used market are generally from the 1990s through early 2000s. As of 2026, these machines are at least 25 years old, which meets the age threshold in every state that uses a 25-year rule for private ownership.

For full state-by-state information, see our [slot machine legality guide](/blog/is-it-legal-to-own-a-slot-machine-state-by-state-guide). [Contact us](/contact) before ordering if you have any question about your specific state.

## Parts and Long-Term Support

The Bally S9000 is a well-documented platform with a large installed base and an established aftermarket for parts. Common wear items — bill validators, reel motors, light bulbs — are available. The machines are mechanically straightforward and serviceable.

We include a one-year warranty and free lifetime tech support on all machines. For more on what maintenance involves, see our [slot machine maintenance guide for homeowners](/blog/slot-machine-maintenance-homeowners-guide).


## Setting Up Your Blazing 7s at Home

Once your machine arrives, setup is straightforward. The S9000 ships fully assembled on a pallet. Here is what to expect:

**Delivery:** Machines arrive via LTL freight with liftgate service. The driver lowers the pallet to ground level. From there, you need to move the machine from the truck to your destination. An appliance dolly handles this well — tip the machine back slightly, roll it to the door, then walk it upright to its final position.

**Placement:** Allow a few inches of clearance behind the machine for the power cord and ventilation. The machine generates modest heat from the electronics — a closed closet without airflow is not ideal for long sessions.

**First power-on:** Plug into a surge-protected outlet. The machine will run through its startup sequence. If configured for free play, press the credits button to add credits and start playing. If configured for bill operation, insert a $1 bill and the credit meter will activate.

**Sound level:** The S9000 produces mechanical sounds — the click of the reels, the bell on a win, the sound of coins dropping (if configured for coin play). For most home environments, the volume is perfectly manageable. If you need lower volume, the settings can typically be adjusted. See our [slot machine noise and volume guide](/blog/how-to-manage-slot-machine-noise-volume-control-muting-soundproofing) for details.

**Lighting:** The machine has internal backlighting for the top glass and button panel. For a game room, this ambient glow adds to the atmosphere, especially in lower light conditions. Many buyers add a small dedicated light above or beside the machine to highlight it as the focal piece of the room.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Is the Blazing 7s handle functional?

Yes. The S9000 came with a functioning spin handle. Pulling it is equivalent to pressing the spin button.

### Can I set the denomination?

Yes. Denomination is controlled in the machine settings. We configure it to your preference before shipping.

### Does the progressive jackpot still work at home?

The progressive display functions as a display. At home it is not networked to other machines — it shows a static value or counts incrementally during play, depending on configuration. It does not pay an external jackpot. This is normal for all casino progressive machines in private use.

### How does Blazing 7s compare to Bally's video machines?

Very different experience. The S9000 uses physical reels that spin and stop mechanically. Bally video machines use screens. Both are excellent; the choice depends on whether you want the mechanical reel feel or a video game experience with bonus rounds. For the full Bally model breakdown, see our [Bally slot machines guide](/blog/bally-slot-machines-home-buyers-guide).

## Ready to Buy?

The Blazing 7s is one of those machines that looks right at home the moment you plug it in. Browse our [shop](/shop), review our [warranty details](/warranty), or [contact us](/contact) to talk through which version fits your space. We have been doing this since 1992.
